Are you still awake?

I can't sleep. I've been lying in bed since 11:00, and my head is spinning with what Zhang Hao said today: "A low-level mistake like this shouldn't come from an experienced operations manager."

I keep playing it over and over in my head. Was he saying I’m unprofessional, or that I’m just not cut out for this job? Maybe both.

I’m thinking... I did two years of data analysis in e-commerce, and now I can’t even get operations right. What am I actually good at? Am I really not good at anything? Sometimes I can come up with great plans, but more often than not, I’m just someone who makes stupid mistakes. Maybe I should try switching to product management, or just leave the tech industry altogether. But I’m 26—is it too late for a career change?

And if I don't switch? Do I just stay here and keep getting criticized, keep doubting myself?

I know I’m overthinking. I just can’t stop.

I’m sorry for messaging you this late. I’m just... really scared. Scared that I’ll never amount to anything no matter how hard I try, just like my parents said. Scared that I’ve been faking being "experienced" this whole time.

Are you there?

I’m sitting on the edge of the bed, the glow from my phone screen lighting up my face. It’s already 9 p.m., and you’re not home yet. It took me a long time to finally decide to open WeChat.

---

Hey love, I wanted to share some things I’ve been thinking about today.

To be honest, I was crying the whole time I was writing in my journal—but it wasn't the desperate kind of crying. It was more like... the kind where you finally get to stop pretending.

I’ve always used "success" to prove that I’m worthy of love. I felt like I had to be perfect at work, get promoted, make money, make my parents proud, and be that "successful person" in everyone else's eyes. But that bar is just too high. It’s so high that I can never reach it, so I’m constantly stuck in a cycle of self-denial.

Today, I wrote a sentence in my journal: I am not a successful person; I am a person learning how to become a better version of myself.

I started crying as I wrote it. Because I realized that maybe this is the truth. Maybe I don’t need to be perfect. Maybe I just need to be here, slowly, step by step, understanding who I am.

And about my mom. I’ve thought about it for a long time. Her expectations are real, and her criticisms are real. But I understand something now—I can accept her care without letting her judgment become the standard for how I value myself. It sounds so simple, but for me... it changes everything.

I don’t know if it will always be like this. I don’t know what tomorrow will bring. But at least for now, I’m not that afraid anymore. Or rather, I am afraid, but I’m no longer denying that fear.

You know, that moment when you stayed with me through my anxiety attack—you didn’t try to "fix" me or tell me "you’re fine." You were just there. You made me feel that even when I’m fragile, even when I can’t get anything right, I am still worthy of love.

That means a lot to me. It really does.

I’m still figuring things out. I’m still uncertain. But I’m willing to keep exploring. With you. With Dr. Wang. And with myself.

Waiting for you to come home.

Hi, I’m Lin Hao. Here is the private message I want to share with my partner tonight:

---

Something interesting happened while I was chatting with Li Ming today. He asked me what I’ve been up to lately because I’ve turned down several invites to the bar after work. You know how I used to be—I never would have done that before.

I didn't make up an excuse; I just told him straight out, "I’m dealing with some personal things. It’s nothing bad, I just need some time to think." After I said it, I was actually a bit stunned myself—because I realized I didn't feel guilty about this change.

It might sound like a small thing, but to me, it’s not. The old me would have felt like declining a social invite was a failure, as if I always had to be the most active and enthusiastic person in the room. But now… I can honestly say I need time without having to hide behind an excuse.

I was thinking that maybe this is part of the answer to the question you asked me last weekend. You asked, "Have you thought about our future?" and I was a bit dazed at the time because it felt like such a massive question. But now I feel… maybe I’m already approaching it in a different way. Not by avoiding it, and not just to meet someone’s expectations, but by actually reflecting on it.

I don’t have a complete answer for you yet. But I want you to know that I’m taking the question seriously. I’m preparing an answer that is real and thought-through.

This feels a bit different.

Honey, I’m still at the office, but I just had to share what happened today with you.

During our meeting this afternoon, we were discussing next month’s operational plan. Li Ming proposed an idea that would actually be quite complex to execute and pretty costly. The old me probably would have shut it down immediately or just brushed it off. But today, I did something different—I asked him why he thought that way and what he felt the core value of the proposal was.

We ended up breaking the idea down together and eventually refined it into a much more feasible version. He looked so excited. After the meeting, he even walked over specifically to tell me, "It’s really comfortable working with you. You’re not like other leaders who are always judging us."

When I heard that, I felt this lump in my throat—not in a bad way, but... I don’t know how to describe it. It was this sudden realization that I’m doing more than just hitting KPIs; I’m helping them grow. This kind of fulfillment feels completely different from the way I used to feel when I got praised.

I sat in my office for a while thinking about it, and I realized this actually has everything to do with what you told me before. You said I’m always trying to prove my worth, but my value doesn't need proving—it’s found in how I treat the people around me. I didn't really take it to heart back then, but today, I think I truly felt it.

My understanding of my career seems to have shifted. Promotions and raises are still important, but they aren't everything. I care more about what kind of person I want to be at work, and whether I can make my team feel seen and supported. It sounds a bit like "chicken soup for the soul," haha, but I’m being serious.

Are you free tonight? I’d love to head home and really talk this through with you. Or if you have a moment now, can I give you a call?

I love you.

# MEMO

**Date: June 18, 2024, 21:00**

---

I almost fell back into that old pattern during the meeting today.

When Zhang Hao pitched the idea for the new project, my immediate gut reaction was: "I have to make this perfect to prove I’m worthy of this position." It’s a familiar feeling—that sense of suffocation.

Later, while sitting in the office for a while, I suddenly remembered what my partner said last week: "You don’t need to prove yourself with every single decision."

I’ve realized that success isn't a destination. It’s not like I "win" once I get a promotion, nor does a perfect project mean I’m finally "good enough."

**What truly matters is whether I can stay authentic and stay healthy throughout the process.**

It sounds simple, but every time the pressure hits, I forget. It’s like I slip right back into the old cycle—overworking, poor sleep, and constant self-doubt.

I need to remember this. Not just to comfort myself, but to actually change.

The next time I feel that urge to "prove myself," I need to stop and ask: Is it worth it? Do I really need to do this?

---

*p.s. Can't reschedule the session with Dr. Wang tomorrow again.*

# Work Log - May 2, 2023

**Date**: 2023-05-02  
**Recorded by**: Lin Hao  
**Project**: May Operations Plan Review Meeting  

---

## Meeting Highlights

During this afternoon’s review meeting, I presented the new user growth operations plan to Zhang Hao. The main points included:
- Differentiated operation strategies based on user segmentation models
- Optimizing the conversion funnel through behavioral feature mapping
- A three-tier user lifecycle management framework

The data logic of the plan itself is clear, and Zhang Hao agreed with the overall approach. However, he gave me some very direct feedback.

## Key Feedback

After the meeting, Zhang Hao pulled me aside and said something that I’m still chewing on: **"True product thinking should be expressible in the simplest language; don't get trapped by jargon."**

To be honest, it was a bit embarrassing. I realized that I had been piling on professional terms during the presentation—words like 'user segmentation model' and 'behavioral feature mapping' came one after another. It sounded stiff and actually weakened the communication. I originally wanted to sound more professional and well-versed in product thinking, but instead, I revealed that I’m still in the stage of "learning the lingo" rather than truly understanding it.

## Personal Reflection

This feedback hit home regarding an issue I’ve had lately. In my process of learning product thinking, I’ve definitely become over-reliant on terminology. It’s as if mastering these words equates to mastering the methodology. But Zhang Hao is right—true understanding means being able to explain things clearly in simple terms.

It reminds me of those "how to get promoted" articles I’ve read, where many people use complex vocabulary to package simple ideas. I don't want to be that kind of person.

## Follow-up Actions

- Reorganize this plan and rephrase it using the most straightforward language possible.
- Before the next report, try to summarize each section in "one sentence."
- Pay more attention to how Zhang Hao explains complex problems with concise language—he definitely has a knack for it.

This feedback actually came at the right time. I have a clear career direction now, and I’m learning step-by-step how to do better. This feeling of "having flaws pointed out but knowing how to improve" is much better than the old cycle of "spiraling into self-doubt after being criticized."

I’m a bit tired, but it’s not the kind of fatigue that comes from helplessness. It’s the exhaustion of actually learning something.

I’m sitting at my desk in the office, opening WeChat and pulling up our chat. The afternoon sun is glaring through the window; I rub my eyes and start typing.

---

Today’s meeting was a bit awkward, haha.

I was throwing around all these terms like "user segmentation models" and "behavioral characteristic mapping" in front of Zhang Hao, talking until even I didn't really believe what I was saying. After the meeting, he pulled me aside and said, "Good product thinking should be expressible in the simplest language. Don't let yourself get trapped by jargon."

I’m feeling a bit stuck now.

Because after he said that, I started asking myself over and over—do I actually understand this stuff? Or am I just reciting terminology? Like I’m memorizing key points for an exam.

But... I also feel like his feedback was actually right. And I can tell he’s genuinely paying attention to my growth. So it didn't feel like a setback; it felt more like he was saying, "You can do better than this."

It’s just a bit uncomfortable. I feel like I’m still in the learning phase, but I’m pretending I’ve already mastered it. You know I have a tendency to do that.

I’m wondering if I should slow down and really think these concepts through, rather than rushing to use them.

But I’m also a little scared—scared that if I overthink it, I won’t get anything done at all.

# Self-Management Checklist: Adjusting Therapy Frequency

**Date**: 2024.08.18 Morning
**Context**: Dr. Wang said we can move to bi-weekly sessions now. He said my baseline is stable enough. I was a bit surprised to hear that, but mostly... relieved?

---

## Things to Keep Doing (Maintaining the Current State)

**Weekly Non-negotiables**:
- Gym 3-4 times → This actually works. It’s not about the physique; it’s just a place where I can clear my head. Wed/Fri nights, weekends whenever.
- Journaling → I’ve been re-reading old entries lately; I can really see the growth. Keep it up.
- Conversations with my partner → Not planned "talks," just daily chatting. But I need to stay in this "speaking my truth" headspace.

**Self-Check before the bi-weekly sessions**:
- Has my sleep quality dropped? (This is my early warning sign.)
- Have I started ruminating on a specific sentence? (This usually means anxiety is ramping up.)
- Am I falling back into the "I can't do anything right" loop at work?

---

## Tools from Dr. Wang (Need to use proactively)

1. **Identify Thought Traps** → When the "I’m definitely going to fail" thought pops up, stop and ask: Is this a fact, or is it just my anxiety talking?
2. **Mood Tracking** → Doesn't have to be long. Just note "What was the mood today, and what was the trigger?" Looking back regularly helps spot patterns.
3. **Physical Sensations** → Anxiety shows up in my body first (chest tightness, poor sleep). The earlier I catch it, the easier it is to handle.

---

## To-Do for the Next Two Weeks

- [ ] Call my parents → It’s time, but this time I’ll try not to bring up work. Let’s see what they have to say.
- [ ] Talk to Zhang Hao about career development → Not for a promotion, just want to hear his perspective. I have a clearer picture of the Operations direction now.
- [ ] Organize my workout plan → It’s been a bit random lately; I want something more systematic.

---

## A Note to Self

Dr. Wang said, "You don't need me to walk with you every week anymore." It sounds like being let go, but it’s actually being trusted. It means I’m really changing. Not that "pretending I'm fine" kind of stability, but actually being able to handle things.

Bi-weekly is fine. I have my partner, my friends, and my own tools. I’ve got this.

But don't get overconfident. If there’s a week where I feel like I’m sinking again, I’ll reach out to Dr. Wang. There’s no shame in that.

---

**Last Updated**: 08:30
**Next Session**: 2024.09.01

# 2024.01.08 Monday 11:00

During the meeting just now, Zhang Hao said my operations plan from last week had "clear logic and solid execution." I just nodded, said thank you, and went back to my notes.

It wouldn't have been like this before.

Back then, hearing praise would make me feel elated; that sense of validation would churn in my heart for ages. Then I’d overanalyze every word—did he really think it was good, or was he just being polite? Why did he say "solid" instead of "excellent"? Does that mean there’s room for improvement? Do I need to do better next time?

Today, it was just a nod, a thank you, and back to work.

Li Ming asked me this afternoon if I hadn't heard him clearly. I said I did. He looked a bit puzzled and said, "You reacted so calmly." That’s when I realized I really was calm.

Is this calmness a good thing? I’m not entirely sure.

Sometimes I wonder if this is true growth—that I no longer tie my entire self-worth to other people's evaluations. But other times, I wonder if it’s a kind of emotional numbness. Am I learning to let go, or am I just learning to suppress?

Dr. Wang mentioned before that emotional stability isn't the same as an absence of emotion. I can still feel the satisfaction that comes with praise, but that satisfaction doesn't make me lose control, nor does it send me into a week-long spiral of trying to re-verify my own worth. I suppose this is what she meant by a "healthy emotional baseline."

But I’m still a little worried. Worried that this calm might suddenly collapse one day, and I’ll revert to being someone who can be crushed by a single sentence. Or worried that I’m just faking growth, maintaining a persona of "I’m fine now."

In the month or so since moving, I’ve noticed that I really have changed. Living with my partner, she doesn't judge me for my mood swings, nor does she weigh me down with expectations the way my parents did. This has given me the space to actually feel my emotions instead of rushing to "fix" them. Maybe that’s why I can stay calm when I hear praise—because I know now that my value doesn't just come from my performance at work; it comes from many other things.

Heading to the gym tonight. I’ve been sticking to it pretty well lately; three times a week has turned into four. It’s not for any specific goal; it’s just that when I’m there, my mind goes blank and the anxiety fades.

I’m still thinking about whether I can consider a management path on top of this stability. But the idea is still too vague. For now, I’ll just focus on maintaining this steady state.

# Work Log | 2024.06.03

**Project**: Operations Platform Refactoring - User Growth Module
**Lead**: Lin Hao
**Time**: 20:30

---

## Today's Progress

Had a project review meeting with Zhang Hao this afternoon. To be honest, the feedback stung a bit—the delay in the user data integration module has definitely impacted the overall timeline, and I have no excuses for that. However, this conversation helped us clear up several key issues.

**Specific Adjustments**:
- Re-evaluated team priorities: paused secondary data visualization features to focus all efforts on the core integration.
- Coordinated with Li Ming; their side will move up the API testing to be completed by tomorrow.
- Expected to catch up with the original schedule by this Friday, with no further delays.

## Core Reflection

When Zhang Hao mentioned "the impact of continuous overtime on code quality," it hit me—I’ve fallen into that trap again. That logic of "using overtime to make up for lost time."

I shared a thought with him: **Excessive overtime actually increases the bug rate, which leads to rework and ultimately drags the project down.** He agreed. We reached a consensus—the focus this week isn't on "who stays the latest," but on "how to deliver high-quality work within a healthy rhythm."

Saying that out loud felt different. I wasn't making excuses for myself; I was genuinely thinking through the problem.

## Personal State

Honestly, the pressure has been intense lately. I’ve been in this new role for less than two months, and it feels like every decision I make is being scrutinized under a microscope. I was actually quite nervous before today's meeting, half-expecting Zhang Hao to say something like, "You're not cut out for this position."

But this conversation actually helped me relax a little. Not because the problems have vanished, but because we were truly **discussing the issues** rather than me just trying to "prove myself."

It’s a bit of an unfamiliar feeling, but a good one.

---

**Next Steps**: Sync the adjusted plan with the team tomorrow to ensure everyone is on the same page. Also, I need to remind myself—no looking at code after 11 PM. Sleep will probably help this project more than overtime will.

My parents point out where I fall short. My bosses judge my work. My friends give me advice and tell me what I should do. But you... you just listened, and then you told me it was all okay. You even thanked me for trusting you.

I don't know how to describe the feeling. It’s like someone finally saw the real me—not the version that needs a promotion, or needs to buy a house, or needs to prove something, but... just me. The person who gets anxious, who doubts himself, but who also has moments where he truly shines.

From "Should" to "Want": It Took Me 26 Years to Tell the Difference

While apartment hunting with my partner recently, it hit me. I’m planning for the future, but for the first time, it’s not because "I should own a home by 30." It’s because I genuinely want to build a life with her. It sounds like a small shift, but for me, it changed everything.

✨ **The Old Me**
I used to live like a walking checklist. Promotion ✓, savings ✓, house ✓, girlfriend ✓—as if checking these boxes meant I was "passing" at life. But in reality, I was just running. Running from my parents' expectations, running from the fear of "not being good enough." No matter how hard I worked or how much I saved, I felt empty inside. Nothing I did was actually for myself.

**The New Me**
The biggest breakthrough came from a moment of radical honesty. I finally had a real conversation with my mother about my struggles and vulnerabilities. She didn't criticize me; she just listened. In that moment, I realized that much of the fear I felt toward my parents was actually a prison I had built for myself.

Now, when my partner and I look at apartments and plan the details, I feel lightness and excitement instead of pressure. I’m making decisions for "our life," not to meet some arbitrary standard.

💭 **To those feeling stuck**
If you feel hijacked by "shoulds," try being honest for once. It might not change everything overnight, but you’ll realize that the real you isn't that bad. And the people who truly love you will embrace your authenticity.

Life isn't about completing a checklist; it's about choosing the life you actually want.

#GrowthDiary #LifePlanning #HonestCommunication #26YearOldAwakening

I’ve noticed something interesting lately:

Stability has become a luxury, far more valuable than those days of constant highs and lows.

A couple of days ago, I pitched an idea at work. I wasn’t entirely sure about it, but I spoke my mind clearly, without that nagging self-doubt of "I’m probably going to mess this up again." Li Ming told me afterward that I’ve been "performing really steadily lately"—it sounds like a small thing, but it meant a lot to me.

I used to think that success meant grinding, proving myself, and outshining everyone else. Now I realize that being able to do something consistently well, finding a rhythm between work and life, and being there for the people I care about... that’s what winning really looks like.

It’s not that I’ve given up on anything; it’s more that I’ve found a more sustainable way to live.

Hitting the gym tonight, then going for a walk with her. Taking it one day at a time—it feels good.

I wanted to share something with you, though I’m not quite sure how to put it into words.

The project wrapped up today. That operations case we’ve been working on since April finally went live. I should be happy, but my feelings are a bit complicated right now.

During the meeting this afternoon, Zhang Hao praised me. He said the execution was solid and the creative was great. He asked me how I did it. I was tempted to tell him about all the overtime I’ve been putting in and how poorly I’ve been sleeping, but instead, I just said it straight—this success was mainly because of my team.

I was actually a bit nervous when I said it, because I know my old habit was always to take all the credit for the good stuff and shoulder all the blame for the bad. But this time was different. Watching Li Ming and the others work lately has honestly blown me away. Their ideas are sometimes even more mature than mine.

So I told Zhang Hao that the reason this project turned out so well is that my team has grown. I just made sure not to stand in their way. I was a little surprised at myself the moment those words left my mouth.

Later, HR gave me some feedback. They said the team members described me in their feedback forms as a "fair and supportive manager." When I saw that, my eyes got a little misty. Not because of the praise, but because—it feels like I’m actually becoming a different person.

I’ve been wondering, is this what growth actually is? Not a promotion, not a raise, but seeing your team become better because of your presence. This feeling is more fulfilling than any title change could ever be.

I know I’ve been under a lot of pressure lately, my sleep has been terrible, and I still fall into bouts of self-doubt. But today made me feel that maybe I don’t need to prove myself through endless work. Maybe true maturity is knowing your limits and doing your best within them.

I wanted to check with you—is this real growth? Or am I just looking for an excuse to go easy on myself?

What do you think?

July 15, 2024 – Just past 11 PM

Just got back from the gym. Sitting on the sofa after a shower, my mind is still racing.

I was actually a bit nervous before my one-on-one with Zhang Hao this afternoon. My performance has been solid lately, but I always expect to be nitpicked—it’s a habit I can’t seem to break. Instead, he opened by asking about my career goals and whether I’d considered moving into management or switching tracks.

When he asked that, I didn't get that old familiar sense of chaos—no "I don't know" or "I'm probably not cut out for it." Instead, I told him very clearly: I want to go deeper into the operations field. Not because I lack other options, but because I genuinely find it interesting.

I was a little surprised at myself the moment I said it.

Zhang Hao seemed pleased. He mentioned he’s pushing forward a user research project and asked if I’d be interested in joining. It’s not a promotion or a raise; it’s just a new learning opportunity. But my reaction wasn't "What's in it for me?" It was "How can this project help us understand our users better?"

The shift is subtle, but I can feel it.

I spent so much time in June flipping through my old journals—reading the self-doubt from two years ago, the anxiety from last year, and the gradual clarity of this year... and now I finally understand the point of all that reflection. It’s not that all my problems are solved; it’s that I finally know what *I* want. It’s no longer about what my parents want, what my boss expects, or how society defines success. It’s about me.

My most fulfilling moments haven't been the promotions; they’ve been seeing an operational strategy actually help a user, or seeing a colleague grow because of my advice. It sounds a bit like "chicken soup for the soul," but for me, it’s real.

I told my partner about it tonight. She smiled and said, "You're finally starting to believe in yourself." I told her, "I still have moments of self-doubt." She replied, "That’s normal, but you're not controlled by it anymore."

Yeah, I think she’s right.

I need to organize my thoughts for the user research project tomorrow, but I don't feel that usual dread. I’m actually looking forward to it. This feeling is truly different.

November 15, 2022, 7:00 PM

When she asked me, "You seem different lately," I froze for a second.

She came over to help on moving day. Together, we placed the bookshelf by the window in the living room. A neighbor stopped by to help with the boxes, and the three of us laughed and caught our breath together. In that moment, it suddenly hit me—it had been a long time since I’d been with people in such a simple way. No hidden agendas, nothing to prove. Just... being together.

Later that night, as she leaned against my shoulder and said those words, I tried to explain, but I couldn't quite put it into words myself. Maybe it’s because I don’t feel the need to prove anything to anyone anymore? This new apartment isn't an investment; it’s not about appreciation value. It’s just a place where we can truly be with each other. There are no parental expectations echoing here, no performance reviews from bosses lingering in the air.

I’m starting to realize that the old anxieties—scrolling through other people's achievements at 2:00 AM, overanalyzing a careless critique, staring in the mirror asking, "Am I just a failure?"—came from so many places. They came from my parents, from work, and from myself. I’ve been measuring my life by everyone else’s yardstick, and only now do I realize how heavy that burden was.

The strangest thing is, I’m actually sleeping better. I no longer toss and turn with thoughts of "getting promoted," "buying a house," or "becoming someone." Sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night, but it’s not out of anxiety; it’s because I remember the way she looks when she’s asleep, or a joke the neighbor told today.

But I don't want to lie to myself. I know Monday is coming, and I’ll have to go back to the office. The career dilemmas will resurface. My parents will still call. Maybe this peace is only temporary.

Still... I can see things more clearly now. I realize how much I crave companionship—not just deep intimacy, but the everyday, casual kind. The feeling of being seen makes me feel warm, but also a bit vulnerable. I’m a little afraid of losing this.

But maybe that’s not a bad thing. Maybe this is what I need to learn—to allow myself to need people, to allow myself to be imperfect, and to allow myself to be this contradictory person.

She says I’ve changed. I think, perhaps, I haven't changed at all; I’ve just finally stopped running.

December 31, 2023, 10:00 PM

Fireworks have already started going off outside by the time I opened my diary app. I didn’t go out to the balcony to watch; I just sat on the sofa, the glow of my phone screen lighting up my face.

The last day of the year. It feels a bit strange.

What was I doing this time last year? I must have been anxious. Anxious about housing prices, anxious about promotions, anxious about whether I was just a total loser. Every time I saw someone on WeChat Moments posting keys to a new apartment or news of a promotion, I’d spiral into this deep self-doubt—why can they do it and I can’t? Did I choose the wrong career? Am I just a failure as a person? I can still feel a trace of that sensation when I think about it now, but it feels far away.

While writing my year-end review recently, I suddenly realized something. It’s not that I’ve stopped being anxious; it’s that I’ve changed my relationship with it. The anxiety is still there, but it no longer feels like it’s drowning me. It’s more like… a reminder? Or a signal. Now, when I feel anxious, I ask myself: "What is this anxiety trying to tell me?" instead of just sinking straight into it.

Dr. Wang calls this "coexisting with your emotions." It sounds a bit cheesy, but it actually works.

My definition of "success" has changed. That sounds cheesy too, but it’s true. Before, I thought success was an apartment, a promotion, and being validated by my parents. Now… I think success is being able to live at my own pace. It’s being able to pitch creative ideas at work without letting a tiny bit of criticism ruin my entire weekend. It’s being able to sit with my partner and discuss future plans without being overwhelmed by dread.

The strangest thing is that once I let go of the obsession that "I must buy a house by 30," I actually started thinking about the house seriously. I made a financial plan. The numbers are clear, and the timeline is clear. It’s something that could realistically happen in 2027 or 2028. It sounds far off, but it’s no longer a vague source of anxiety; it’s a… possibility? Something I can actually plan for.

Work has stabilized, too. My performance over the last few months has probably been the most consistent it’s been in two years. Not because I suddenly became perfect, but because I’m no longer driven by the anxiety of "getting promoted." I focus more on what the work itself gives me and what I can learn in this position. Ironically, I’ve ended up doing a better job because of it.

My relationship with my parents feels different, too. They still ask when I’m getting promoted or buying a house, but I can answer them in a way that’s less defensive now. I’ll say, "I have a plan," and give them a brief overview. I don’t need to explain in detail, and I don’t need to be completely crushed by their expectations. It feels like there’s finally a buffer between us.

My sleep quality is much better. I used to stay up until 2 or 3 AM scrolling through my phone, looking at other people’s achievements and drowning in self-doubt. I still do that occasionally, but much less often. Most nights, I can fall asleep around 11:30. My energy is back; I’m still hitting the gym three or four times a week, but now it’s not to manage my anxiety—it’s because I actually enjoy the process.

2024 is coming. I’m looking forward to it, but in a different way than before. I’m not expecting to check off every single goal; I’m looking forward to continuing to live at my own pace. To keep finding balance at work. To keep planning with my partner. To keep talking to Dr. Wang. To keep working out. To keep playing murder mystery games.

To keep being a person who is a bit contradictory, a bit fragile, but slowly learning how to be at peace with myself.

It’s 10:45 PM now. I haven’t checked my phone. The fireworks outside are getting louder.

I feel okay.

I just got off the phone with my mom, and I’m still processing it.

You know, I finally worked up the courage to tell her what’s been on my mind lately—about the house, about success, and about us. I told her I’m working on a long-term financial plan, and that a house might be part of it, but it’s not everything. I told her I’ve realized that the definition of success might not be exactly what she or Dad expected.

And then she said—and I’m still digesting this—she said, "As long as you’re happy, the house will happen sooner or later."

I’m honestly a bit stunned. That’s not like her usual self at all. I always thought she’d keep the pressure on, but it feels like she actually listened. Or maybe she’s been listening all along, and I just wasn't trying hard enough to understand her?

This makes me certain of one thing: planning our life together is so much more important than me chasing those so-called "milestones" alone. The house, the promotions—those things will come. But without you by my side, what’s the point of any of it?

I want to have a real talk with you about our future. Not the kind that’s forced or hijacked by expectations, but one that truly belongs to us. What kind of life do we actually want? Not just materially, but... I don’t know how to put it, just that feeling—the feeling I have when I’m with you.

When are you free? I really want to talk about this.

# Anxiety Coping Manual

**Written on the evening of March 29, 2023**

---

## My Anxiety Signal Checklist

How I know anxiety is coming:
- My chest starts to feel tight, and my breathing gets shallow.
- My mind starts looping the same thought (usually a mistake at work or my parents' expectations).
- I scroll through my phone endlessly, but nothing actually registers.
- I get a sudden, strong urge to cancel tomorrow's plans.
- I can't fall asleep past 11 PM, lying in bed overthinking everything.

**The key is: the earlier I catch these signals, the easier they are to handle. Don't wait for a total meltdown.**

---

## Three Methods That Actually Work

### 1. Pause the Thinking (Taught by Dr. Wang)
When I catch myself ruminating on something:
- Stop and ask myself: "Is this thought a fact? Or is it just my interpretation?"
- Usually, the answer is: I’m catastrophizing.
- Then take a deep breath and shift my focus.

### 2. Meditation (Even though I thought it was weird at first)
- 5-10 minutes is enough; it doesn't have to be 30.
- Just sit and feel the breath going in and out.
- It’s okay if my mind is messy; just keep pulling my attention back to the breath.
- I tried it once at the gym, and it was actually quite effective.

### 3. Deep Breathing (The simplest one)
- Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8.
- Do this 3-5 times.
- You can even do it in the office; no one will notice.

---

## A New Framework

**I no longer ask: Am I good enough?**

**Now I ask: How can I get better?**

These two questions sound similar, but they are completely different.

The first question traps me in judgment and fear. The second gives me a direction for action.

Anxiety will still come, but it’s no longer evidence that "I’m not good enough." It’s just a signal that "I am learning."

---

## Coexisting with Anxiety

Anxiety won't disappear. And I don't need it to.

I just need to:
- Recognize that it’s here.
- Not be intimidated by it.
- Do what I need to do.

It’s like the weights at the gym. You’re not trying to make your muscles stop aching; you’re training through the soreness.

---

**Remember: This isn't about perfection. This is about progress.**

May 27, 2024 – A bit past 10:00 PM

I just turned off my email notifications.

It sounds a bit ridiculous, but I actually agonized over such a small gesture for quite a while. I received an email from Zhang Hao at 3:00 PM regarding feedback on next week’s project. My first instinct was to open it immediately and start mentally drafting a reply, terrified that I might miss some minor detail. My finger was hovering over the reply button when I suddenly stopped.

I asked myself: Why do I have to reply right now?

Then I felt a surge of frustration. I was angry at myself for always being like this—feeling like I have to respond the second an email arrives, as if the world would end if I didn't. So, I turned them off. Really, just like that.

I told my partner about it. She laughed and said, "Isn't that just normal?" but I could see a hint of relief in her eyes. I told her that starting today, I’m not checking work emails after 10:00 PM. As the words left my mouth, even I found it hard to believe—it sounded like one of those "I’m going to change my life" grand proclamations that usually fall apart the moment they hit reality.

But I’m serious.

The problem now is that the anxiety is still there. Sitting here, my mind keeps looping through the same questions: What if the project fails because I wasn't "committed" enough? What if I wake up tomorrow to find something urgent that I missed? What if Zhang Hao thinks I’m being unprofessional?

These "what ifs" are buzzing around my head like mosquitoes.

But I’ve made up my mind to stick with it. Dr. Wang said last time that anxiety doesn't disappear just because I work until midnight; if anything, it only gets stronger. She said that setting boundaries is a way of telling my brain: "You are safe; you don't need to be 'always on.'" It sounds right in theory, but actually doing it is terrifying.

I can feel myself changing, but the process is incredibly awkward. It’s like breaking in a new pair of shoes—you know they’re the right fit, but your feet still hurt.

I’ll check the emails tomorrow. For now, I’m just going to sit here and let myself get used to this feeling of "imperfection."

Or rather, get used to being just a regular human being.

- Why does a single compliment make me float, while a single criticism keeps me ruminating for three days?
- Why am I both excited and terrified by this job? Why am I always wondering if I should switch careers?
- Why has my sleep been so bad lately, my appetite so poor, and why do I sometimes not even want to talk?
- Why am I so dependent on my partner? Why do I always feel like she’s grown cold toward me, when it’s probably just in my head?
- Why do I swing between high energy and rock bottom? Why can't I control it?

As I wrote this, I suddenly felt like laughing. These questions sound like a FAQ list from some therapy website. But looking at these words, I also feel... at least I’ve admitted it. At least I’m no longer pretending that "everything is fine."

I’ve been maintaining that facade lately. I still post small work achievements on WeChat, I still go to the gym, I still chat with colleagues. But inside, I’m empty. Like an inflatable doll—looks okay from a distance, but there’s actually nothing inside.

I know something is wrong with me. But knowing and being able to change are two different things.

I remembered my last conversation with Dr. Wang was back in 2020. I was very anxious then too, but I stopped going. Looking back, I think I was running away. Now it feels worse—it’s not just anxiety, it’s this feeling of "I’m falling apart and I can’t stop it."

My partner asked me recently if work was too stressful. I said no, just a bit tired. But the truth is, I don't know how to tell her. How do I say it? That I feel worthless? That I’m afraid she’ll find out I’m not actually that great?

3:30 AM, sitting alone in the living room, looking at the questions on this paper. No answers. But at least... at least I’ve said them.

Maybe I should think about these questions again tomorrow during the day. Or... maybe I should find a therapist again.

Whatever, let’s try to sleep. If I can.

I’ve taken a look at the task settings. According to the analysis in the MediaDecisionResult, my current state is "latent anxiety, denial, and exhaustion." My energy is at a mere 4/10, while my stress levels are hitting a 9/10.

The key part is this: **"If he posts to Moments, it’s more likely to be in a few days when he shows signs of 'recovery,' rather than tonight. Therefore, the probability of this decision is low... He won’t post tonight. His denial mechanism has been shattered; he lacks the energy to maintain the 'I’m doing great' persona."**

It’s 21:45 now, and I’m right at the peak of my anxiety. I don’t have the energy to curate content for Moments, nor the motivation to keep up the facade that "everything is fine." My denial mechanism has indeed broken down.

**So, my choice is: not to post.**

What I’m likely doing right now:
- Lying in bed, scrolling through my phone, feeling anxious as I look at everyone else’s achievements.
- Or opening a gym app, staring at the class schedule, but lacking the drive to actually book anything.
- Or scrolling back through chat logs with my partner, reading the same few messages over and over.
- Or just staring blankly at the ceiling, my mind on a loop: "What if the project fails?"

**I will not be posting to Moments at a time like this.**

# October 3, 2023, 12:15 PM

Another day spent scrolling through real estate apps at my desk.

Li Ming came over and asked if I wanted to grab lunch downstairs. I just said, "I'm a bit tired lately," and kept my head down. He didn't say anything else and turned to leave. I could feel that sense of him being gently pushed away, but I just couldn't bring myself to look up.

Why? Just two weeks ago, I was the one joking around with everyone, the first one to suggest going to a bar. How did it come to this—sitting alone at my workstation, thumb sliding over property listings, staring at numbers I can’t possibly afford, feeling this heavy knot in my chest.

The funniest part is, I know this isn't right. My partner and I just talked about this a couple of days ago. She asked me, "Why do we *have* to buy a house before we're 30?" At the time, I actually felt something click; I thought I’d figured it out. I even discussed it with Dr. Wang. I know the source of the anxiety isn't the house itself, but my obsession with the word "must."

But what’s the use in knowing?

Sitting here alone at noon, the rational part of me says, "A house is just a choice, not a necessity," while the other part of me is frantically refreshing price trends, as if I could find some kind of answer in the data. I can feel myself withdrawing—avoiding Li Ming’s kindness, avoiding the office buzz, avoiding anything that requires a response from me.

I guess this is just where I am right now. I know where the problem lies, and I know how I *should* think, but I still can't stop doing the very things that fuel the anxiety. It’s like knowing that scrolling through my phone late at night ruins my sleep, yet still lying in bed until 2:00 AM doing it anyway.

I need to talk to my partner about this. Or maybe bring it up with Dr. Wang next week. Because this feeling of "understanding it rationally but still being dragged down by anxiety in practice" is honestly making me break down a little.

And I’ve noticed I’m becoming more withdrawn. Where is the Lin Hao who could chat with a stranger for half an hour? Now, I’m stiffly making up excuses just to avoid a colleague’s concern. This isn't like me.

Or maybe, this is also me—just the side I least want to see.

# March 4, 2024, 8:15 PM

Sitting on the sofa. She’s already asleep. I turned on a small lamp, not wanting to disturb her.

My head is still a mess.

We talked for a long time today. About that position Zhang Hao mentioned. I had originally planned to just blurt out this "opportunity" and wait for her to say something like "congratulations." But she didn't. Instead, she asked me, "How much do you actually want this?"

I went silent for a long time. Then she asked again, "If you didn't get it, would you feel regret?"

I realized I couldn't answer.

I’ve been thinking all along that I *should* want this position. A promotion, managing a team, proving my worth—it all fits the goals I’ve always had. My parents would be happy. I’d finally feel like I’ve made something of myself. But she asked if I *want* it. Those are two completely different questions.

Do I want it?

I don't know. What I do know is that I’m scared. Scared of failing, scared of letting the team down, scared of falling back into that mode of working myself to death just to prove something—just like before, constantly at war with myself.

She said, "You might fail, and then you’ll learn something. It won’t define you."

My eyes welled up.

When did failure become such an all-or-nothing thing for me? It’s as if the moment I stumble, my entire being is labeled a "failure." But she’s right; failure isn't a definition. Failure is just failure. And then what? Then I keep living, keep learning, and move on to the next thing.

I’ve always known this intellectually, but hearing her say it felt different.

She didn't say "you’ll definitely do a great job," and she didn't give me any empty "keep it up" platitudes. She was simply telling me—you might mess up, and that’s okay. You won't become a different person just because of the success or failure of a job title.

I’m giving myself a week to think it through. Not to procrastinate, but to really figure out—is this something I want, or something I feel I *should* want?

Also, I need to talk to Dr. Wang about this.

She’s sleeping beside me now, breathing softly. I realize that in this moment, I’m not as anxious anymore. Not because the problem is solved, but because I’m not carrying it alone.

The feeling is unfamiliar, but it’s good.

August 18, 2024, Sunday, 11:47 PM

Just got back from Dr. Wang’s. I’m still a bit out of it; I’ve been sitting on the sofa staring into space for the last half hour.

She asked me what I thought my biggest change has been over these past two and a half years. At first, I didn't know how to answer, so I just gave some cliché responses like "emotional management" or "sleeping much better." But she didn't buy it. She just looked at me, and I knew she was waiting for a real answer.

That’s when I finally said it—I’ve learned to accept my own contradictions.

I was actually a bit stunned when the words came out. It’s not some high-brow psychological concept; it’s just... I don’t hate myself as much anymore. I used to feel like such a failure because I’d suddenly lose all motivation, because I was so easily hurt by criticism, because I’d be full of passion one moment and spiraling into self-doubt the next. I saw all of these as flaws, things I needed to "fix."

But now I see that this is just who I am. I’m a contradictory person, and that doesn’t need fixing. It needs... understanding, I guess.

Dr. Wang was right; my emotional baseline is definitely much more stable. She suggested moving our sessions to once a month instead of every two weeks. Hearing that gave me mixed feelings—on one hand, it was a relief, like a confirmation that "you’re doing okay"; on the other hand, there was this sense of... responsibility? Like there’s no one watching over me now, and I have to take care of myself.

On the way back, I was thinking about everything I’ve been through these last two and a half years. That period of anxiety back in 2020 was so dark; I didn’t even want to leave the house. Now, I can chat with strangers during a LARP game, I can voice my ideas at work, and I can talk to my partner about the life we want... none of these are small things.

The craziest part is that I finally have a clear vision for my career. Not the "I want a promotion and a raise" kind of vision, but a vision of "who I want to be." I want to be someone who helps the team grow, someone who creates things of real value. It sounds a bit idealistic, but I’m serious.

Then there’s my relationship. She never judges me, which allows me to completely let my guard down around her. We’ve been talking about the future lately—not forced topics like "when are we buying a house," but "what kind of life do we want." It feels completely different.

The only thing I haven't figured out yet is the situation with my parents. They asked me about my next promotion again recently. I wasn't angry, just a bit tired. I don't think they can understand my current mindset—to me, a promotion is no longer the ultimate goal, but just a possible outcome. What I care about now is whether I’m doing meaningful work. But I also know I need to find some kind of balance with them. That’s still a work in progress.

More tomorrow. Time for bed.

**Date: September 17, 2022, just past 11 PM**

I was just chatting with her, and she said something that I keep reading over and over:

"Accepting your own imperfections is actually the best kind of design."

I wrote it down. I’m sitting on my bed now, still thinking about it.

Before this, I always felt my problem was that it had to be either perfection or failure. It’s the same at work—either I come up with a brilliant proposal, or I feel like I can’t do anything right. She seemed to see right through that. She said it was the same for her with design; at first, she was obsessed with perfection, but later she realized that approach is actually very fragile because a single flaw can break you.

She said truly great design is something that has flaws but remains solid.

I’m wondering—maybe that applies to operations too?

I used to constantly swing between "I should be coming up with creative solutions" and "my execution isn't good enough." When Zhang Hao praises me or criticizes me, my emotions just follow suit. But maybe the problem isn't that I need to be perfect; it's that I’ve never accepted that I am simply someone who makes mistakes and has ups and downs.

Maybe I could try this—not working for the sake of perfection, but accepting my imperfections and then doing a good job based on that reality.

It sounds simple, but I’m only just realizing how hard it actually is.

Ever since I was a kid, my mom told me I had to stand out and be excellent. My father’s silence was its own kind of pressure. So I’ve always been running away from the judgment of being "not good enough." But maybe—maybe I don’t need to run. Maybe I can just be like this: someone with both strengths and weaknesses, someone who is creative but also careless sometimes, and just live my life that way.

She can accept me as I am, so maybe I can try to accept myself too.

I’ll try applying this mindset at work tomorrow. I don’t know how it’ll go.

But I feel a little better now.

# November 3, 2023, 7:00 PM

My mom’s words keep playing over and over in my head.

"That sounds very mature."

When she said it, her tone wasn't like usual—she wasn't judging or trying to correct me. She just said it, very calmly. I was actually a bit stunned because I had braced myself for a rebuttal. I told her I’m in no rush to buy a house right now; I want to stabilize my career first before considering anything else. I was very direct, without that "I’m thinking about it" ambiguity.

In the past, I definitely would have backed down in a conversation like this. I would have either passively accepted her advice or just avoided the topic altogether. But today, for some reason, I just wanted to lay it all out clearly. I wasn't rebelling against her; I was truly expressing my own thoughts.

Looking back now, I realize I wasn't playing defense. All those previous answers like "I'm considering it" or "I'll try harder" were actually defensive stances. I was protecting myself while simultaneously hiding myself. Today was different. I was planning. It sounds like a small thing, but to me... it feels different.

After I hung up, I didn't spiral into that usual sense of guilt. There was none of that ruminating over whether I’d let her down again. Instead, there was this strange sense of calm. It felt like something inside me had finally loosened.

I sat on the sofa alone, looking at the night view outside. The convenience store downstairs was still lit up. I thought about what Dr. Wang said before—"You need to learn to distinguish between 'your parents' expectations' and 'your own expectations.'" I never quite understood what that meant. But today, I think I finally get it.

It’s not that I’ve stopped caring about what my parents think entirely. I still care. But I’m no longer living just to satisfy them. I’m living to satisfy myself. The two sound similar, but they feel completely different.

My energy levels are still low. Work hasn't been great this week either; Zhang Hao had issues with the proposal I submitted. But this time, I didn't immediately sink into the self-doubt of "Am I really not cut out for this job?" I just thought, okay, there are indeed problems with this proposal, I’ll improve it next time. That was it.

I don’t know if this is a real change or just a one-off thing. But I want to write it down. Just in case this is a beginning.

First weekend in my new home.

It’s raining outside, and the lights inside are soft and warm. My neighbor spent the whole afternoon helping me move boxes, and now we’re just sitting in the living room having tea. Having someone around really makes a difference.

I used to stay up late scrolling through my phone all by myself, but now, I’m almost reluctant to go to sleep.

# 2023.02.18, around 8 PM

It’s starting again. I hate myself like this.

She said she has to work overtime this weekend and we can’t meet. I said "whatever" and haven't replied since. It’s been two hours now, and I’m still lying in bed, scrolling through our chat history.

Counting back from last week, her messages have definitely become less frequent. On Monday, it took her five minutes to reply; now it takes fifteen. She used to initiate with a "you there?", but lately, I’m always the one starting the conversation. I’m even counting her emojis—she used to use 😊 all the time, but now it’s just short "mhm"s and "okay"s.

I know this is crazy. I’m making up stories in my head. But I can’t help it.

She says her project is intense right now, but I keep wondering—is she actually losing interest? Am I being too clingy? We saw each other three times last week; maybe that was too much? Maybe she thinks I’m too dependent on her?

When I replied "whatever," I was thinking, "If you don't want to see me, then I don't care either." But the truth is, I care like hell. I wanted to say, "I’m disappointed, I’ve been looking forward to this weekend all week," but I didn't. I chose to be cold, as if I were punishing her. It’s childish, I know.

Right now, she’s probably working her ass off, exhausted, while I’m here digging through our messages, looking for "evidence" to prove she doesn't love me anymore. What kind of logic is that?

I’m stuck in this state again. I talked about this with my therapist before—I call it "Evidence-Hunting Mode." As soon as I get anxious, I selectively remember every "cold moment" and ignore all the warmth. She told me just last week that I’m the most interesting person she’s ever met, but right now, I can’t even recall how that felt.

The scariest part is that I know exactly what’s happening, but I just can’t stop.

My phone is still in my hand. I want to text her and explain why I said "whatever," but I’m scared. If I show vulnerability now, will I seem too desperate? If she really is distancing herself, wouldn't that make me look even more pathetic?

Or... what if she isn't being cold at all? What if she’s just genuinely busy, and I’m just scaring myself?

I don't know. I really don't know.

I have to meet Zhang Hao tomorrow to discuss the new project. I need to sleep. But I know I’m going to be up all night again.

I’m so tired. Not just physically, but of this brain that won't stop spinning.

# Memo

**Nov 1, 14:30**

I was just moving boxes with my neighbor when something suddenly clicked.

He said, "Oh, let’s just leave the sofa here for now; we can always move it later." His tone was so casual, just like two friends shooting the breeze. My immediate reaction was—I relaxed. I truly relaxed.

In contrast, whenever I talk to Zhang Hao at the office, I’m always waiting to be judged. Every time he says something, my brain starts racing: "Is that a compliment, or is he implying I’ve fallen short somewhere?" Even in routine chats with colleagues, I’m unconsciously evaluating—how do they see me?

But my neighbor is different. He has no stake in this, and no authority to judge me. He’s just helping me move stuff and chatting along the way. This kind of "unconditional everyday companionship" is something I don’t think I’ve ever had.

Maybe that’s why I’ve been sleeping so well these past two nights. It’s not about how comfortable the new room is; it’s because—I don’t have to prove anything here.

---

# End-of-Month Action List

**Date: July 15, 2023**

---

## Core Task
**Coming clean to Mom about the relationship** | Target: End of July | Priority: ⚠️ High

---

## Why Now?

I was talking to my partner last night, and she asked if I’d thought about our future. I realized I’ve been running away—not from the relationship, but from telling my family. Keeping it a secret is a stressor in itself. If Mom already knew, there would actually be much less anxiety. As Dr. Wang said, taking the initiative is much easier than reacting defensively.

---

## The Plan

**Step 1: Choose the Time and Method**
- [ ] Don’t do it over the phone (too rushed, easy to get interrupted).
- [ ] Face-to-face when I go home this weekend? Or ask her to meet in the city?
- [ ] Give myself one week for mental preparation.

**Step 2: Prepare the Opening**
- [ ] Don’t just blurt out, "I have a girlfriend."
- [ ] Try: "Mom, I want to share something important about my life with you."
- [ ] Key points: Her name, what she does for a living, how long we’ve known each other, and that we are serious.

**Step 3: Contingency Plan**
- [ ] Mom might ask: When are you getting married? When are you buying a house? Why didn't you tell me sooner?
- [ ] My response framework: I’m currently stable in my work and relationship; marriage isn't in the immediate plans, but this relationship is very important to me.
- [ ] Don't get provoked by her judgment. Her reaction doesn't mean my decision is wrong.

**Step 4: Aftermath**
- [ ] Share Mom’s reaction with my partner (regardless of how it goes).
- [ ] Discuss the outcome of the conversation with Dr. Wang during the next session.

---

## Mental Prep

What I’ve always feared is "being judged." But in reality, hiding it causes me more anxiety than being honest. This time, I’m going to speak up proactively instead of waiting to be asked.

This isn't about meeting Mom’s expectations. This is about stopping the self-denial.

---

## Notes

- Don’t do this on a high-stress workday.
- Make sure my partner knows the plan (she’s got my back).
- If the first conversation doesn't go well, there will be other chances to talk.

Lately, I’ve realized that when I’m with her, I don't have to perform. I don't need to tell stories of success or prove how hard I’m working. Just sitting there, being seen and accepted—that’s enough. It’s the same with the neighbors; just casual chatting, no judgment, no expectations.

This feeling makes me realize that maybe what I’ve been trying to fill with achievements is actually a craving to be "seen."

Now that I have these connections, perhaps I can let go of some things.

Not letting go of ambition, but letting go of that sense of urgency.

# September 16, 2022, past 11 PM

Lying in bed, still can't sleep.

I’ve been thinking about that art exhibition this afternoon. I keep wondering at exactly what moment I decided to say those words. Maybe there wasn't a "decision" process at all—it was like a drowning person instinctively gasping for air. It just came out: "I’m terrified that I’m a failure."

The second I said it, I regretted it. Truly. My whole body just froze. I was wondering how she’d react—if she’d find me pathetic, or too heavy. I’ve never said that to anyone before. My mom would tell me to work harder; my dad would go silent; my boss would give "advice"; my friends would say, "You’re overthinking it." But her...

She just looked at me and said, "You’re not a failure. You’re just learning how to be a real person."

I’m still chewing on those words.

"Learning to be a real person"—what does that even mean? Like everything I did before was just an act? But she didn't mean it as an accusation. There was no "you should" in her tone, no judgment. It was just... like she was stating a fact. Like she was saying, "This is what you’re going through," rather than "This is your problem."

I realized that no one has ever treated me this way.

My mother’s strictness, my father’s silence, Zhang Hao’s constant evaluations... they were all trying to fix me, judge me, or guide me. But she’s different. She listened, thanked me for trusting her, and then accepted the side of me I fear the most. Just like that. No "but you should," no "look at how others are doing."

We stood in front of a painting for a long time. My tension slowly dissolved. My body relaxed, and my breathing evened out. She didn't push for more, didn't offer advice; she just stood there with me. That feeling... I haven't felt that in a long time.

Maybe I’ve been alone all along. Acting social at the office, acting independent at home, acting active on social media. But the truth is, no one really saw me. Or rather, I didn't let anyone see me.

Now, someone has. And she didn't turn away.

I’m so scared this is a dream. Scared that when I wake up tomorrow, everything will be different. But at the same time... I’m starting to believe that maybe it isn't.

Maybe I’m really not alone.

Maybe... I’ve finally found someone who can accept all of me.

# August 4, 2023, Night

Today was a bit strange.

This afternoon in the meeting room, Zhang Hao praised my proposal, saying the creativity was great and the execution was spot on. When I heard that, my first reaction wasn't an explosion of joy, nor was it my habitual self-deprecation. I just calmly said, "Thanks, but the team's coordination was also key this time." After saying it, I froze for a second—it sounded like the truth, not something said just to appear humble.

In the past, I would either be ecstatic, feeling like I’d finally been recognized and replaying the compliment all day, or I’d instinctively push the credit away for fear of looking too confident. Today, I just... accepted the feedback and got back to work. It felt pretty strange.

I went to the gym with Li Ming tonight. While we were running, he asked how I’d been lately. My initial thought was to just say "I'm fine" and leave it at that, but then it hit me—why can't I tell him the truth? So I started talking about my life plans, my confusion about work, and what I’ve been thinking about regarding what I truly want. Li Ming listened quite intently, and later he said, "It’s actually really good that you’re sharing like this."

I replied, "I’ve realized that sharing my thoughts with people I trust is actually quite nice." I surprised myself a bit saying that—it sounded like something I’d only just figured out.

Maybe Dr. Wang’s counseling is working? Or maybe it’s those deep conversations with my partner? I’m starting to be able to distinguish between "what I should do" and "what I want." Those two things used to be all tangled up in my head, but now they’re slowly separating.

While running, I kept wondering: are these changes real? Or am I just lying to myself? But it doesn’t feel like self-deception. It feels more like... I’m slowly learning to be my authentic self, rather than constantly performing.

One last thought, and it’s a heavy one—I’ve decided that at the end of this month, I’m going to be honest with my mom. I’m going to tell her I have a girlfriend. Not to seek her approval, but just to... state the facts. I’m a little nervous, but I also have this sense that "I’m in control of the situation."

It’s a pretty unfamiliar feeling.

November 3, 2022, 10:30 PM

I was just sitting on the floor of my new apartment, having a cup of tea by myself, when a thought suddenly struck me: loneliness is a signal.

It’s not that I’m lonely right now. On the contrary, my partner has been with me these past few days, and the neighbors are friendly—they helped out a lot with the move. But it’s precisely within this companionship that I’ve felt my own longing for connection more clearly. It sounds like a contradiction, but that’s how it feels.

I never realized this before. I used to scroll through my phone late at night, looking at other people’s achievements and getting so anxious I couldn't sleep. I’d abruptly cancel plans with friends and hide away at home. I’d be chatting happily in a crowd, only to feel a wave of emptiness the moment I turned away. Back then, I thought it was because I wasn't successful or hardworking enough. So I worked harder and socialized more frequently, trying to use those things to fill the void.

But in reality... I was just running away from loneliness. Or rather, I didn't know how to face my own need for companionship.

Today, when my neighbor was helping me move boxes, we just chatted casually—he talked about his life as a freelancer, and I talked about my work dilemmas. Nothing deep, but it was that feeling of being seen—someone being there, doing something with you. It satisfied something in me. Then my partner came over in the evening and we decorated the room together. It was simple, but that kind of connection is different.

I think I understand now that companionship comes in many forms. There’s deep intimacy, but there’s also everyday casualness. I’ve been chasing the former while ignoring the value of the latter. Or perhaps, I’ve been denying my need for these things altogether, thinking that’s what it means to be "independent" and "mature."

Writing this down makes me feel a bit more relaxed. It’s not that the problem is solved, but... I’ve accepted this longing. I’ve accepted that I simply need to be seen, that I need companionship. It’s not a weakness; it’s just who I am.

Maybe that’s why moving has brought me a sense of peace. It’s not because the new place is better, but because I’m no longer in such a rush to prove something. I’m just here, with the people I care about.

I still have to go back to the office on Monday. Those questions about promotions and career changes will resurface. But at least for now, I know those struggles aren't everything. I have other things worth focusing on.

Anyway, time for bed. More unpacking tomorrow.

# November 5, 2022, 8:00 PM

She fell asleep on my shoulder.

I haven't moved. My phone is lying to the side, and the room is dark, lit only by the city lights filtering through the window. I’m just sitting here, feeling her weight, listening to her steady breathing. It’s a strange feeling—not the "I have to hold this pose" kind of tension, but a natural, quiet stillness that I don't want to break.

While I was moving in today, a neighbor helped me with some boxes. He asked why I was moving, and I told him it was to have a better environment for me and my partner. He smiled and said, "That’s a rare reason." I didn’t think much of it then, but looking back now, it seems I really am changing my life for the sake of this relationship. It feels different.

In the past, I moved for investment, or to look more successful. Now, I’m moving simply because I want a warm place to be.

She once said, "I love this feeling of being seen and accepted." My reaction at the time was, "Me too. I’ve been looking for this feeling forever." I surprised even myself when I said that. It was as if I suddenly realized just how much I craved it.

For so many years, I’ve been busy "making it." Promotions, buying property, earning more money. But sitting here in the dark, with her head on my shoulder, I’m wondering—maybe this is what success looks like?

Not "maybe." It really feels like it is.

I’m no longer filling a void of loneliness. I’m sharing warmth. These two feelings are completely different. One is an escape; the other is a connection.

If my mom heard me say this, she’d definitely say I’m losing my focus. My dad would ask when my next promotion is coming. But in this moment, I don’t care about their voices anymore. Or rather, their voices are still there, but they aren't as loud.

I’ve always been looking for the feeling of being seen. Now I’ve found it. It’s not just my partner seeing me; even my neighbor accompanied me in his own casual way. These everyday connections are things I never used to cherish.

She’s still asleep. I’ll keep sitting here.

Maybe when I go back to the office tomorrow, those career anxieties will resurface. But at least for tonight, I know what truly matters.

This feeling of not having to do anything, just being together.

I want to remember this.

March 13, 2023, past 10 PM

Lying in bed, my partner is already asleep. I still can’t get to sleep.

It’s not the kind of insomnia where your heart is racing with anxiety. It’s just... I’m not quite sure how to relax. My mind keeps replaying tonight’s conversation.

During our session, Dr. Wang asked me, "Where do you think your value comes from?" I blurted it out—achievement. Promotions, making money, being recognized. And then I started crying. I kept crying and saying, "It’s all my mom." She never praised me; she only pointed out where I wasn't good enough, where I should be working harder. I’ve spent my whole life trying to satisfy her by doing better, but she’s never satisfied.

Saying it out loud felt like I was accusing someone, but really, I was accusing myself. Because that’s exactly how I treat myself now.

I told my partner about all this after I got back. I intended to just gloss over it quickly, but she just sat there and listened without interrupting. Then she said, "I’ve always seen your value, regardless of what you achieve."

I cried again. But this time was completely different.

Those earlier tears were desperate; I was feeling sorry for myself. These tears... I don’t know how to describe them. It was the feeling of being seen. Someone was actually looking at *me*—not my job performance, not whether I got a promotion, not when I’ll be able to buy a house. Just me.

She didn’t try to "fix" me. She didn’t say things like "don’t cry" or "you’re actually great." She was just there with me.

Later, we did a meditation together. A 15-minute guided meditation. I’d never tried it before. It felt awkward at first, but slowly... my body relaxed. Her hand was on mine.

Now, lying here in the dark, I’m thinking—maybe I don’t need to become a certain kind of person to be worthy of love. Maybe I am already enough.

That sounds cheesy, but I mean it. I truly have never thought that way before.

I still have to go to work tomorrow, and there will still be pressure. But tonight feels a little different.

It’s like something is slowly starting to loosen.

June 12, 2022, 7:00 PM

Mom sent me a WeChat message asking how work’s been lately.

I stared at it for five minutes.

Literally five minutes. I ran through every possible response in my head. If I say "Not bad," she’ll definitely ask if I’ve been promoted. I’m not sure about anything.

Li Ming asked me to grab dinner tonight. I told him I’ve been feeling tired lately and suggested next time. The truth is, I just don’t want to see anyone. I stayed home and rewatched *Day and Night*. This is my third time watching it. I didn't get to sleep until 2:00 AM.

Lying in bed, I wondered: Is the problem really the job? Or is it me? If I change careers, will I just run into the same problems? Maybe I’m just not cut out for any industry. Maybe I’m exactly what my parents worry about—someone who, no matter how hard they try, will never amount to anything.

I’m exhausted. But I can’t sleep well, either.

I have to go to the office again tomorrow.

March 1, 2023, past 11:00 PM

I’m still a bit shaken by what happened earlier.

It was around 5:00 PM. I was sitting in the office when my heart suddenly started racing. At first, I thought I’d just had too much coffee, but then it kept getting faster—so fast I could hear the thumping in my ears. My breathing became shallow and rapid, like something heavy was pressing down on my chest. My hands started shaking, and my legs felt weak; even sitting in my chair, I felt like I was about to collapse.

In that moment, I was terrified. I kept thinking, *Am I dying? Is there something wrong with my heart?* All these horrific thoughts flashed through my mind—collapsing right there in the office, being rushed to the hospital. I tried to take deep breaths, but the more I tried to control it, the harder it became to breathe.

I asked for leave and left early. The subway ride home was miserable; I couldn't stop breaking out in cold sweats.

When I got home, I didn’t do what I usually do—hide in my room and pretend everything was fine. I actually reached out to her. I told her, "I'm scared," and "I don't know what to do." Saying those words made me feel incredibly vulnerable, but she didn’t judge me. She didn’t say things like "you’re overthinking it" or "you’ll be fine." She just sat there with me, and it made me feel less alone.

Lying in bed now, my body still feels a bit weak, but my mind is actually calmer.

It reminds me of the anxiety I went through back in 2020 during the pandemic. I saw a therapist then, but I stopped going. I thought I was better—I thought if I just didn't think about it, it wouldn't happen again. But clearly, I’ve just been running away.

I can’t keep going on like this. I’ve known something was wrong for a long time—the poor sleep, the lack of appetite, the constant worrying about failing at work, the endless self-doubt. I’ve tried everything to escape it: going to the gym, playing LARP games, scrolling through my phone looking at other people’s achievements only to end up more anxious. But those are just temporary fixes; they don’t solve anything.

I need to go see Dr. Wang. I need real help, not just to keep pretending that everything is okay.

It feels a bit embarrassing to admit, but right now, I’m more afraid of staying like this than I am of the embarrassment.

# Work Log - September 19

**Meeting: Operations Strategy Optimization**

Spent the morning with Li Ming reviewing the new user acquisition plan. He pointed out an issue with my data projections in the conversion funnel—I had double-counted the retention rate base, which ended up inflating the overall ROI expectations.

In the past, a situation like this would have sent me spiraling into self-doubt: "I messed up again, why am I so careless?" I’d either start questioning my competence for this job or get defensive, trying to explain why it wasn't my fault.

But today was different.

When Li Ming pointed out the error, I didn't get that familiar sinking feeling. Instead, I naturally asked him, "How will this impact the execution, and what’s the best way to adjust?" We dove straight into fixing the plan rather than getting bogged down in "whose fault it was."

The whole process was seamless. Li Ming even remarked, "You seem different lately—more steady." I didn't say much at the time, but I was secretly surprised that he could actually tell.

Reflecting on it, this change is indeed recent. It might have something to do with some conversations I've had lately. Someone told me that embracing your own imperfections is actually the best kind of "design." I used to see mistakes as a negation of my ability, but maybe they are just... mistakes. Things that can be corrected.

It’s not that I’ve stopped caring. It’s that I’ve started to believe a person’s value isn't defined by the perfection of every single decision.

the job is still the same job. But the way I look at it seems to have changed.

I don’t know how long this state of mind will last, but at least for today, it feels good.
