# 2022.09.12 7:30 AM

I’ve been lying in bed for half an hour now, wide awake. Or rather, I haven't slept at all.

When she replied yesterday with "Sure, this weekend?", it felt like a jolt of electricity went through me. I was on the move all morning—getting a haircut, hitting the mall for new clothes, even deep-cleaning my room. The barber asked if I had a special occasion coming up. I didn't give him a straight answer, but I could feel myself grinning like an idiot. It’s a strange feeling, like someone is beating a drum inside my chest.

How did she see through my "fake confidence" so easily?

I always thought my disguise was perfect. At the office, I’m the guy who fits in quickly, always has something to say, and seems completely in control. But she just took one look at me and said, "You look confident, but I think you’re doubting yourself." I wanted to argue, but the words got stuck in my throat. Because... she was right.

It’s terrifying. And yet, it’s such a relief.

I don't know how to describe the feeling. It’s like walking in the dark for a long time when suddenly someone switches on a flashlight. You’re caught between surprise and fear—because you realize you’ve been heading in the wrong direction, but also because someone finally actually *sees* you.

Zhang Hao mentioned yesterday that I seemed to be in a good headspace. Honestly, I haven't done anything differently, it’s just... I have something to look forward to now. This sense of anticipation helps me come up with solutions faster in meetings and keeps me from spiraling into self-doubt. She said she’s gone through this kind of self-doubt too, but she chose to face it instead of running away. I’ve been thinking about changing careers, about escaping the tech industry, but maybe... maybe that’s not the problem at all. Maybe I just need someone to tell me that I don't have to keep proving myself all the time.

But I’m still scared.

Scared that when we meet this weekend, she’ll realize the real me isn't actually that interesting. Scared that this is all just a fantasy. Scared of rejection. Scared that I’ll have to go back to those late nights, scrolling through my phone, feeling empty while watching everyone else’s achievements.

I’ve even thought that maybe I should lower my expectations. Maybe I should remind myself that this might lead nowhere.

But I can’t do it.

I’m still looking forward to the weekend.

I still feel like, maybe this time, someone truly sees me.

Maybe... I don't have to keep pretending.

# Work Log - 2023.03.24

**2:30 PM, Office, just stepped out of Zhang Hao’s office**

Today was a bit of a surprise.

Zhang Hao called me in for a meeting. I was a little nervous at first—even though work has been feeling smoother lately, my subconscious still jumps to, "Is something wrong again?" Instead, the first thing he said was, "Lin Hao, I’ve noticed you’ve become more stable recently, and more sincere."

I was a bit stunned. He went on to say that in this week’s project proposal, I wasn't rushing to prove how smart I am anymore; I was actually thinking about what would be most helpful for the team. He also mentioned that the way I ask questions in meetings has changed—before, it felt a bit defensive, but now it feels like I truly want to understand.

So, I was honest with him. I told him I’ve been in counseling lately, learning how to better manage my emotions and expectations. I was worried he might think it was too personal, but he just nodded and said, "That explains it."

Sitting here in my office now, it still feels a bit surreal.

It’s not like I’ve suddenly become a completely different person—the anxiety is still there, and I still couldn't sleep last night. But it definitely feels different. Before, I would interpret insomnia as "I’m screwing up again, I’m under too much pressure." Now, I can see it as just a manifestation of anxiety, and... that’s it. It’s not as scary.

I think what Zhang Hao said is true. He wasn't just trying to comfort me. I can feel it myself—when I’m working on a proposal, I’m no longer trying to "prove I deserve praise," but rather "how to do this job well." There’s a huge difference between those two starting points.

There’s a new project next month, and he wants me to lead a small team. I said yes. There was no "Can I really do this?" type of self-doubt. It just... feels like an opportunity to learn.

I’m going to tell my partner about this tonight. I feel like this moment is worth recording.

# Memo

**September 29, 2022 – Evening**

She said: Maybe your problem isn't the career itself, but your perception of yourself.

I wrote that down. We were sitting on the sofa, her head resting on my shoulder, and I pulled out my phone to record it. It felt like I’d caught hold of something vital, and I was afraid it would slip away if I didn't capture it right then.

I’ve been agonizing over whether to switch careers, as if changing industries would solve everything. But when she said that, I finally understood—the issue isn't about tech versus traditional industries. The issue is how I see myself. I’ve been defining my worth by metrics like "have I been promoted?" or "am I earning enough?" No wonder I’m anxious regardless of the field.

This applies to operations. This applies to life.

Lately, when I make a mistake at work, I still get frustrated, but the sense of despair isn't as heavy as before. I can see it a bit more clearly now: it’s just a mistake; it’s not a reflection of who I am. Zhang Hao’s criticism still lingers in my mind, but now I can tell myself that it’s just part of the learning process.

She helped me see that I don’t need to be perfect. It sounds simple, but it’s taken me a long time to actually believe it.

My sleep has been better lately. I wonder if it’s because the anxiety has eased up.

Keep thinking about this.

November 27, 2022, 10:30 PM

Today at the cafe, she suddenly asked if I wanted to go to Yunnan with her. "I want to," I blurted out.

Then I paused, realizing how different those words felt.

In the past, I would have said "maybe" or "let’s see if I have time," or I would have immediately started calculating my mortgage, savings, and promotion prospects before suppressing the idea entirely. But today, I just said it—I want to. No conditions, no evaluations, just wanting.

The way she looked at me was gentle. It wasn’t the surprise of "you finally said it," but rather a sense of confirmation. It was as if she had known all along that I wanted to, and was just waiting for me to say it myself.

We sat there for a long while, talking about trivial things—when to go, for how many days, where we’d like to stay. It was a very ordinary conversation. But I realized I wasn't thinking about whether the money was "worth it" or if I should be using that time for something "more meaningful." I was simply enjoying the act of planning itself.

It’s a strange feeling. It’s as if the person who used to constantly run a ledger in their head wasn't me at all.

During a recent chat with Dr. Wang, he mentioned that my changes weren't due to improved external circumstances, but because my perception of myself had shifted. I didn’t quite get it at the time. But today at the cafe, I think I understood a little.

I no longer feel like I have to do something or become someone to prove I’m worthy of companionship. I just am. That’s it.

It sounds simple, but as I say it, I can feel the weight of those words.

When I’m sitting at home alone, I’m not as afraid of loneliness anymore. My neighbor knocks sometimes, and we chat. Sometimes she doesn't come by, and I’ll put on a movie, or work out, or do nothing at all. It’s all fine. It’s all acceptable.

The strangest part is that because I’m no longer so afraid of being alone, I cherish the moments with her even more. It’s not because I need her to fill a void, but because I truly want to be with her.

Those are two different things. I understand that now.

Work tomorrow. Zhang Hao has been pushing for the new operations plan again. But I don’t have that old sense of anxiety. I’ll do the plan well because it’s my job. Not to prove anything, but simply because it’s what I’ve chosen to do.

I feel a bit tired, finally. But it’s the kind of tiredness that comes from a "fulfilling day," not the kind from "another day wasted."

There’s a world of difference.

Another day full of hope (not).

People in the comments will probably ask "What's wrong?", and I'll just reply "Nothing, just been a bit tired lately."

But the truth is, I really am exhausted right now.

# Operations Proposal Review - October 5th

**Time**: 14:00-14:45  
**Attendees**: Zhang Hao, Me, Design Team  
**Proposal**: Q4 Community Engagement Plan  

---

## Proposal Recap

Three directions were proposed:
1. User Growth System (Points + Badges) – The conservative approach.
2. Community Topic Challenges – Moderate creativity.
3. UGC Content Incentive Program – The safest bet.

The overall tone of the presentation was... safe. Way too safe.

## Zhang Hao’s Feedback

"These plans are all fine, but they don't feel like your style."

After a pause, he continued: "What happened to that 'Role-Playing Community' idea you mentioned before? Why isn't it in here?"

I stammered through an explanation about high execution risks, uncertain user reception...

He cut straight to the point: "Don't let anxiety limit your imagination."

---

## Self-Reflection

To be honest, hearing that stung a bit. But what hurt more was that he was right.

I was definitely over-cautious this time. To avoid making mistakes, I suppressed every risky idea I had. The result was a proposal that looked "safe" but "mediocre."

I could feel my own tension during the presentation. On one hand, I wanted to appear professional and reliable; on the other, I was constantly calculating in my head, "Will this get rejected?" In the end, I filtered out all the creativity and left only the things that were "guaranteed not to fail."

This ties back to what’s been on my mind lately. I’ve been anxious about whether I’m "good enough for this job," so when it comes time to show my work, I subconsciously choose "insurance" over "innovation." But the irony is—by doing that, I’m actually turning into a mediocre operations manager.

## Follow-up Thoughts

Zhang Hao didn't reject the proposals, but he suggested I rethink how to incorporate more creative elements. He gave me a week to recalibrate.

I need to figure out: where is the balance between creativity and risk?

It’s not about ignoring risk entirely to innovate, but rather—have I been infinitely magnifying the probability of "possible failure"?

That "Role-Playing Community" idea is actually quite interesting. Users could choose a persona within the community and participate in different topic discussions... Looking back now, it wasn't that crazy. Why did I feel it was "too risky" at the time?

Maybe it really is like Zhang Hao said—I’m being limited by my own anxiety.

I need to talk to my partner about this tonight. It might also be time for a deeper discussion with Dr. Wang about my career-related anxiety.

---

**To-do**: 
- Restructure the Q4 proposal to incorporate more creative elements.
- Assess the feasibility and risks of the "Role-Playing Community."
- Reflect on how to find the sweet spot between innovation and stability.

The conversation I just had with my partner keeps looping in my head. I’ve been sitting on the sofa for half an hour now, not even in the mood to scroll through my phone.

Before, I kept wondering if my drive for success was just about proving something. Proving it to my parents, to my boss, to the people flaunting their achievements on Xiaohongshu. She asked if I’ve finally stopped running away. I didn’t answer right then, but now I know the answer. Yes. Before, I was escaping—escaping my parents' expectations, my own uncertainty, even escaping my identity as "Lin Hao" through LARP games. Now, it feels like I’m moving toward something. Toward a professional life I actually want. Toward a deep connection like the one I have with her. Toward becoming someone who truly knows himself.

It’s a strange feeling. The pressure has actually decreased, yet I have more energy. It’s as if I no longer have to try so hard to prove anything, which allows me to see what I want more clearly.

My mother’s voice is still in my head, but it’s not as loud anymore. When she asks when I’m getting promoted, I no longer feel that surge of guilt or defensiveness. Instead, I can think calmly: I am doing what I believe is right. And that, in itself, is enough.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue optimizing the strategy and discuss the next steps with the team. But tonight, I just want to sit here for a moment and confirm that this shift is real.

I’m exhausted. Not physically, but mentally. I’m still going to wake up half an hour early tomorrow, because that’s just who I am right now. But I want to write this down—I see myself doing it. I’m repeating old patterns. Maybe just recognizing that is progress in itself.

He said that career development isn't just about promotions; it’s about how you create more value within the role. He told me he sees a talent in me for helping the team grow.

I didn't know how to respond. It felt strange—to be "seen" not for my professional achievements, but for the internal changes happening within me.

I kept thinking about it on the subway ride home. I realized it’s taken me this long to understand that success isn't a destination. It’s not the items on a checklist like promotions, raises, or buying a house. I want those things too, but the satisfaction they bring is fleeting.

What truly fulfills me are the moments that can't be quantified—that feeling of being understood while talking with my partner, seeing a team member become more confident because of my support, or simply the moments when I am at peace with myself.

For the past two and a half years, I’ve been asking myself, "Am I cut out for this industry?" or "Should I change careers?" Now I realize that wasn't the problem. The problem was that I had been judging myself by external standards all along.

Now, the questions I’m asking are: "How can I do better in this role? How can my unique traits help me create value?"

The shift sounds small, but it feels massive.

Having Zhang Hao notice this change made me realize that what’s happening internally is starting to spill over into the outside world. I don’t need to pretend I have all the answers. I can be honest about the fact that I’m reflecting and learning. That, in itself, is a form of strength.

# Nov 1, 2022 - Night

Like the neighbor casually sitting in my place drinking water, or her scrolling through her phone next to me. These tiny things give me a sense of... security?

Damn, I’ve never felt this way before.

Back in my hometown, the house was always full of people, but I never felt safe. I was always being watched, always being judged. My dad sitting there in silence, my mom pointing out everything I was doing wrong. Even with them around, I felt lonely.

This room is so quiet now. No one is judging me for why I haven't been promoted yet or why I haven't bought a house. It’s just me, her, and the occasional greeting from the neighbor. This silence lets me breathe.

I posted a photo of the new place on WeChat Moments with the caption: "New home, new beginning." What I actually wanted to say was something else. I wanted to say: I’ve realized that what I need isn't a better real estate investment, but a place where I can drop all those "shoulds." A place where I can just be myself.

But writing that out would have been too cheesy.

Interestingly, I barely thought about work while moving. I have to go back to the office on Monday, and Zhang Hao will definitely have a ton of feedback, but I don't feel as nervous now. Or rather, I’m not in such a rush. Maybe in this new environment, I can look at those things differently.

Something in me seems to have shifted. The old voice—"You have to stand out," "You have to be successful"—it’s still there, but it’s quieter. In its place, another voice is asking: What do you actually want?

I don't have the answer yet.

But at least now I have the time and space to think about it.

I’m having dinner with her later. We agreed to cook something simple in the new place. Thinking about it makes me relax even more.

Maybe loneliness isn't a bad thing. Maybe loneliness is just telling me what I need.

I’m grateful to her for showing me this.

Grateful to the neighbor for the help.

Grateful for this quiet room.

I’ll worry about the complicated stuff tomorrow.

Then I said something that’s still echoing in my head: "For the first time, I feel like I’m planning my own life, rather than just reacting to it."

When I said that, she squeezed my hand tightly. I saw tears glistening in her eyes. Not from sadness, just… she looked moved.

In that moment, I finally understood that this wasn't just about choosing an apartment. It was me telling her—and telling myself—that I’m finally no longer a hostage to my own anxiety. I’m no longer that person who scrolls through their phone late at night, looking at other people's achievements and spiraling into self-doubt.

To me, success now means being able to stay authentic at work, staying connected with the people who matter, and accepting my own ups and downs.

My sleep has been great lately. My energy is back. I wonder if it’s because I’ve finally stopped that self-torture of "shoulds."

Back to apartment hunting tomorrow. But it’s different this time—I’m choosing for our life, not stressing over a set of standards.

It really does feel different.

# September 16, 2022, past 11 PM

Still can't sleep.

I was lying in bed scrolling through my phone for a while, then put it down. Now I’m just sitting here, my head filled with our conversation at the cafe today.

She said, "We are all learning how to be imperfect people, and that in itself is a form of growth."

I’ve been thinking about that sentence over and over.

To be honest, I felt like crying when I heard it. Not the sad kind of crying, but... I don’t know how to describe it. It was like someone suddenly opened a window, and only then did I realize I’d been stuck in a tiny room all along.

Ever since I was a kid, my mom taught me that I had to stand out, to be better than everyone else. My dad didn't say much, but the expectation was always there. So I’ve always felt like I had to be perfect, had to be successful, had to reach certain milestones by thirty. Every time I made a mistake, I’d ruminate on it for days, feeling like my life was over. A single criticism from Zhang Hao could keep me anxious for half a month.

But she’s different. When she told her story, she didn't shy away from her failures at all. She said her designs get rejected all the time, but she eventually realized that failure isn't the end—it's just information. She said, "Accepting your own imperfection is actually the best kind of design."

I just sat there, listening intently, nodding my head. I realized I was doing something I hadn't done in a long time—I was truly taking in someone else's perspective instead of rushing to prove myself.

Maybe my problem isn't about whether to change careers or whether I can get a promotion. Maybe my problem is just that I’ve been constantly fighting with myself.

She said she could understand my inner conflict and accept my vulnerability. At the time, I thought, *How is that possible? I have so many issues, I get anxious so easily, I’m so unstable...*

But she really seemed to just... accept it. No judgment, no "you should be this way."

I’m a little scared right now. Scared that this feeling of being understood will vanish. Scared that I’ll mess up this relationship. Scared that I’ll wake up and find out this was all just a fantasy.

But at the same time, I feel... maybe I’m not alone. Maybe someone can truly see me, including all my imperfections.

Maybe success isn't just about promotions and buying a house. Maybe my value isn't solely defined by my job performance.

I want to believe that. I really do.

I have to go back to work tomorrow and face all that pressure again. But it feels different. It feels like something is slowly changing.

I hope this feeling stays.

# Saturday, July 15, 2023 – Sunny

Yesterday, after leaving Dr. Wang’s office, I sat in the convenience store downstairs for half an hour. Just tucked away in that little corner, sipping room-temperature coffee and watching the people pass by.

My first instinct was to dodge the question. I knew that once I answered, I’d have to take responsibility for that answer. But I didn't run; I said it out loud.

I said: I want to live with her. I want a stable, real relationship. What I want isn't the hollow shell of "success," but a meaningful life.

The moment I said it, I felt like I’d been holding my breath underwater for a long time and had finally broken the surface.

Dr. Wang didn't say much. She just looked at me and asked, "Then why are you still hiding it?"

That hit home. Because I realized—while I’m being honest with my partner, I’m still hiding things from my parents, from my mother. I’m still living inside that "I’m still single" lie. And what is that lie actually protecting? Is it protecting me from having to face their expectations? Protecting me from making a real commitment?

But maybe... maybe honesty would actually give me more freedom.

I’ve decided. At the end of the month, I’m going to tell my mother. I’ll just say it directly—I have a girlfriend. She’s a UX designer; she works at a creative agency. She understands me, and she doesn't judge my vulnerability.

Thinking about this decision actually terrifies me. There will be a barrage of questions, expectations, and judgments. But at the same time, I feel a strange sense of relief—if she already knows, I won't have to hide anymore. I can finally live in the sunlight instead of constantly switching between two different worlds.

This time is different. This time I’m not reacting passively; I’m taking control.

I’m going to define my own life instead of waiting for others to define it for me.

Tonight, when my partner asked how my day was, I said, "Great, I figured some things out." She smiled and asked, "Like what?" I said, "About us."

She didn't press for details; she just leaned her head on my shoulder.

I think this is the life I want.

October 25, 2023, past 11:00 PM

Just stepped out of Dr. Wang’s office and sat in a convenience store for half an hour by myself. I only just got home.

I still have this feeling I can’t quite put into words.

Today we did this values-ranking exercise. Dr. Wang asked me to list the things that matter most to me. I thought it would be hard, but it actually came out quite fast once I started writing—deep connection, career growth, and autonomy. Those three were at the very top. Then came... what was it... health, creative expression, family relationships... and a house was eighth.

Eighth.

I sat there looking at that list, and my eyes started to well up. Dr. Wang didn’t say anything; he just watched me as I tried to process it. A house was eighth. I’ve spent all this time thinking it was my most important goal. I check real estate apps every month, calculate mortgage rates, and get anxious just looking at the numbers, feeling like I’m falling behind. But the truth is... a house shouldn’t even be the center of my life plan.

It wasn’t until I said it out loud that I truly realized I’ve been chasing the wrong thing.

It’s not that a house isn’t important. It’s that I’ve treated it as the *only* thing that matters. Every time my parents ask when I’m buying a place, I start spiraling into self-doubt. Every time someone my age posts their new home on social media, I start comparing myself. I’ve tied my entire sense of self-worth to this one thing. But it doesn’t even crack the top three.

So what actually matters? Deep connection. I need people who truly understand me. Career growth. I need to do meaningful work, not just be anxious about my salary. Autonomy. I need to live at my own pace, not be drowned by expectations.

Those are the things I actually want.

The problem is, I’ve been doing the exact opposite. I’ve been obsessing over buying a house instead of building real connections. I’ve been stressing over promotions instead of thinking about what kind of work helps me grow. I’ve been held hostage by my parents' expectations instead of fighting for my own autonomy.

I’ve been heading in the wrong direction.

And what’s worse is that realizing this has only made me more lost. Because now I have to face the question: what do I do now? Do I give up on the house? Do I have a showdown with my parents? Do I need to rethink my entire career?

There are no answers to these questions.

My partner asked how the session went, and I said it was fine. But I didn’t tell her about the moment my eyes teared up. I don’t know how to explain the feeling—it’s like a mix of relief and being shattered. I thought I was moving forward, only to find out I’ve been walking the wrong way the whole time.

It’s past midnight now. I should sleep. But I know I’ll be lying in bed thinking about that list. Thinking about why a house was eighth. Thinking about why I never asked myself these questions before. Thinking about whether I should tell my parents tomorrow.

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

But at least... at least I know what I’m actually looking for now. Does that count as progress?

# Project Delay Retrospective | 2023.05.08

**Project**: May New User Retention Campaign Proposal
**Status**: Delivered 3 days late → Remedied

---

## What Happened

The campaign proposal, originally due on Monday, wasn't handed over to Zhang Hao until Thursday. This wasn't due to slow execution, but rather because I fell into "analysis paralysis."

After receiving the requirements on Monday morning, I started the user analysis. I initially intended to get a rough draft out quickly, but the more I worked, the more I felt the data was incomplete. I wanted to add more dimensions to the user segmentation, benchmark against competitors' retention strategies, and look further into historical trends... Before I knew it, the whole morning had passed with zero output.

Then self-doubt kicked in: Is this direction right? Are my analysis dimensions sufficient? What if my proposal gets rejected? The more I thought, the more I hesitated to start writing. I was stuck in a loop between "collecting information" and "refining ideas," which was effectively procrastination.

It wasn't until Wednesday afternoon, when Zhang Hao checked in on my progress, that I realized the severity of the situation.

---

## Key Takeaways

This mistake taught me something crucial: **Perfect analysis does not equal good execution.**

My previous mindset was "the more thorough the analysis, the better." But in product work, **rapid iteration is more valuable than a perfect plan.** I should have presented a first draft when I was 70% confident and adjusted based on feedback, rather than spiraling in a loop by myself.

One sign of progress: I didn't sink into a vortex of self-deprecation like "I messed up again, I'm useless." I can clearly see that this isn't a capability issue, but a methodological one. I made a mistake that can be identified and corrected.

---

## Action Plan

**1. Set Time Limits for Analysis**
- Requirement comprehension phase: Max 2 hours.
- First draft output: Must be completed on the same day, even if I'm only 70% sure.
- Reserve time for revisions: Refine in subsequent iterations based on feedback.

**2. Establish "Quick Feedback Checkpoints"**
- Once the draft is ready, run the logic by Zhang Hao or Li Ming first (don't wait for the final version).
- This helps catch directional issues early instead of going down a rabbit hole alone.

**3. Redefine "Perfection"**
- Perfection ≠ All-encompassing analysis.
- Perfection = Quick understanding of needs + Proposing a viable plan + Willingness to iterate based on feedback.

---

## Personal Reflection

To be honest, this delay made me a bit anxious. But it wasn't the "I'm doomed" kind of anxiety; it was the "I need to adjust my methods" kind. This type of anxiety is actually constructive.

I've been thinking that this might be a recurring pattern for me—trying to be "perfect" in many things, only to end up paralyzed. It happens at work, and it seems to happen in my personal life too.

I’ll try the new approach for next week’s campaign proposal. Hopefully, I can find a better balance between speed and quality.

---

**Discuss this with Zhang Hao during our next 1-on-1 to get his advice.**

When she said, "Your voice doesn't sound right," it felt like I’d been caught or something. I didn't say a word, yet she could tell instantly that I wasn't doing well. Then she started in with the questions—how’s work going, when am I getting promoted, when am I going to think about the house... one after another.

I was getting so frustrated, but I still had to answer her patiently. Finally, she said, "You need to take care of yourself." I know it’s because she cares, but it just sounded like... I’m failing again.

Now I feel like pressure is closing in from every direction. At work, Zhang Hao’s feedback has been inconsistent lately; sometimes my proposals go through, and other times he just picks them apart. On top of that, I’m thinking about a career change, trying to save money... and my sleep has been terrible. I’ve been waking up in the middle of the night lately, and once I’m awake, my mind just starts racing.

To be honest, I’m exhausted. It’s not a physical tired, it’s more like... the feeling of something tightening around my throat.

How have you been lately?

I didn’t move at all while you were asleep.

I just sat there watching you. I couldn't see a thing in the dark, but I could feel your breath. In that moment, I was thinking... just how deeply what you said affected me.

"To be seen, to be accepted."

I’ve been looking for that feeling. Truly. I’ve never been as certain as I am right now.

I realized that none of those things were what I was actually looking for. This is it—to be seen without being judged; to be accepted without being "fixed."

You know, growing up, my mom always told me I had to stand out, to make something of myself. My dad was the same. I kept asking myself when I’d finally be successful. But now I’m thinking, maybe this *is* success. Just this—sitting with someone, not needing to say a word, and feeling warm.

I feel like I finally understand why I used to act that way—the late-night scrolling, the sudden cancellations, the loneliness in a crowd. It wasn't because something was wrong with me; it was because I was searching for this kind of companionship, this feeling of being seen.

Do you really find it warm here?

I mean... not just the house. This relationship, this feeling. Do you feel like something has changed, too?

I feel like I’ve changed. Or rather, I’m finally starting to believe that I deserve to be treated this way.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something with you.

During our team knowledge-sharing session this afternoon, I gave a presentation on our user segmentation strategy. To be honest, I was quite nervous beforehand—mostly because this strategy was actually born out of a lot of trial and error, with plenty of mistakes and adjustments along the way.

In the past, I definitely would have only shared the successful end result and glossed over the "unprofessional" parts of the process. But this time, I decided to lay out my entire thought process—including how my initial hypotheses were debunked and how I pivoted step-by-step. I still felt a bit self-conscious while speaking, but I just pushed through it.

Afterward, Li Ming told me he found that kind of sharing incredibly helpful. He said, "It made me feel like learning is a normal, acceptable process." When I heard that, I was actually a bit stunned.

I realized that I’ve been so afraid of exposing my imperfections, but that’s actually where the most value lies. It’s not because I eventually succeeded, but because I was willing to say, "This is how I got from failure to success." That’s actually more meaningful for others.

This shift feels a bit strange. I used to feel like I had to be either omnipotent or worthless. Now, it seems like I can accept the state of being "a work in progress." And I’ve noticed that when I can embrace my own learning process, I can actually see more clearly what I’m doing and what I’m truly good at.

I wonder if this counts as that shift in "self-awareness" we’ve been talking about? Things feel a little different now.

I know it sounds simple, but for me, this was truly the first time I’ve responded to a setback at work this way. Before, I would either over-invest in "proving myself" or just shut down and blame myself. This time, I didn't crumble, and I didn't overreact.

# Sunday Afternoon, November 12

I just went over my spending records from the past three months with my partner. To be honest, when I first decided to track every single cent, I was quite resistant—it felt like I was putting myself in shackles. But looking back now, there’s actually a strange sense of liberation.

Knowing exactly where the money went has, ironically, reduced my anxiety.

It sounds a bit magical, but it’s true. That vague financial anxiety I had before—that nameless fear of "I don't think I'm saving enough" or "I'm wasting money"—has now turned into concrete numbers. 1,200 for the gym, about 500 on LARP and board games, daily meals and commuting... Looking at these figures, I can actually think calmly: Was this money well spent? Do I really need to make adjustments?

It’s so much better than before, when I’d spiral into self-doubt the moment I saw my bank balance.

As my partner looked over my spreadsheet, she said, "I really like the version of you right now." I was a bit stunned. What did she mean? That I’ve become more rational? Or that I’m trying to change? I didn’t push for an answer, but those words have been lingering in my mind.

We also talked about the house. I proposed a new idea—instead of rushing to save a full six-month emergency fund, why not aim for three months first and then adjust as we go? The point isn't the number itself; it's that we have a plan, rather than being passively anxious.

As I said that, I realized my mindset really has shifted.

Before, I was always oscillating between "I must do this" and "I can't do this." Now, I’m starting to ask, "How can we do this?" It seems like a small shift, but to me, it feels huge.

I’m starting to understand what Dr. Wang said last time—anxiety itself isn't the enemy; it’s a signal telling me that something needs to be examined. By tracking my spending, I’m actually "examining" my relationship with money. By creating a flexible plan, I’m "examining" what truly matters.

It’s not about escaping anxiety, but working with it.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s the "I know what I’m doing" kind of tired, not the "I’m anxious about everything" kind. There’s a world of difference.

Tonight, I want to talk to my partner more about the future. Not a "what we should do" discussion, but a real conversation about what we actually want.

# Wednesday, December 13, 2023 – Past 11 PM

I was just lying in bed, but my mind was still racing. I decided to get up and write this down, otherwise I’ll probably forget it by tomorrow.

I was chatting with Li Ming this afternoon, and he was obsessing over year-end bonuses. To be honest, that was me six months ago—staring at everyone else’s numbers, looking at my own savings, and falling into that same vicious cycle. But today, I actually found myself explaining my recent financial planning to him...

Halfway through, I even surprised myself.

I said, "Actually, success doesn't have to be defined by owning a house. What I'm thinking about now is how to live at my own pace. I’m still going to buy a house eventually, but it doesn't have to be before I'm 30, and it doesn't have to be in a specific spot."

As I said it, I could feel that my voice was steady. Not the kind of forced steadiness where you're trying to act tough, but a genuine sense of confidence. The way Li Ming looked at me changed, like he was thinking, "Since when did this guy get his head so clear?"

I’ve been wondering about that myself.

Have I really changed? Or am I just having a good day, and tomorrow I’ll slip right back into that anxiety?

But... thinking about it, that doesn't seem right. This shift didn't happen overnight. Everything I’ve been doing step-by-step since the start of the year—the sessions with Dr. Wang, the savings plan I made with my partner, that feeling of clarity after venting it all out at the gym—all of that has been building up. Today was just the first time I articulated it so clearly.

The strange thing is, when I said those words, I didn't feel like a hypocrite. In the past, I’d try to hype myself up, telling myself "don't be anxious," but I was just lying to myself. This time is different. This is something I’ve actually figured out.

I’m not naive enough to think the anxiety has completely vanished, though. Mom started asking about the house again recently, and Dad was at it too. In those moments, I still get tense; I still feel that sense of being judged. But the difference now is that I know I’m anxious, I know why I’m anxious, and I know I have ways to deal with it. It feels like... I’m no longer being pushed around by anxiety; I’m walking alongside it.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s the kind of tired you feel when you’re "moving toward something," not the kind you feel when you’re "being chased by something."

Work tomorrow. The feedback on the recent proposals has been good, and Zhang Hao hasn't brought up my "execution issues" lately. I’m starting to think the problem wasn't that I couldn't do the job well, but that before, my head was so full of "I must get promoted" that I couldn't focus. Now, I just... do the work. And it seems like by doing just that, I’m actually doing it better.

It’s almost funny.

Anyway, time for bed. See you tomorrow.

We cooked together tonight—she chopped the vegetables while I handled the wok. Nothing special, just naturally doing our own thing, occasionally passing a plate or saying, "The heat’s a bit high." Over dinner, we vented about work; she listened to me complain about my project, and I listened to her rant about her clients.

Back when I lived alone, I’d usually just order takeout or settle for a bowl of noodles. Now, having someone to do these ordinary things with makes everything feel so much more grounded.

# Tuesday, July 12, 2022 Night

Lying in bed, phone screen glowing, I have no idea what to do with myself.

I was chatting with Li Ming at the office this afternoon, and he asked how I’d been. I was going to give the usual "not bad" or whatever, but for some reason, I just couldn't get the words out. Instead, I just said it—"I haven't been doing so great lately."

The moment it left my mouth, I felt a pang of regret. It felt like showing weakness, or like I was fishing for sympathy. But Li Ming didn't react like that. He just said, very calmly, "We’re all here."

Just those four words.

I keep playing that sentence over and over in my head. "We’re all here"—what did he mean? That he’s going through something too? Or that he understands this sense of powerlessness I’m feeling?

I suddenly realized that it’s been a long time since I’ve done that. I’ve been pretending to be fine for so long. At the office, I’m the first one to suggest going to the bar. On WeChat, my Moments are all gym selfies or photos from events. But in reality, how long has it been since I actually told anyone, "I’m not okay"?

In that moment, I felt a certain sense of relief. Not that fake, social kind of relaxation, but truly... being seen. Not having to put on an act.

But lying here now, the doubt is creeping back in. Is this a real change? Or am I just kidding myself again—thinking that one honest sentence can actually solve anything?

I’m still the same person who messes up. I’m still the person who doesn't know what they want. I’m still stuck in this loop. Saying "I haven't been doing so great" doesn't change a thing.

And yet... why did that moment feel different?

Maybe I’m just exhausted. Maybe I really couldn't hold it together anymore, so I let my guard down. Maybe this wasn't a breakthrough at all, just a momentary breakdown born of desperation.

Work continues tomorrow. I’ll have to keep pretending everything is fine. Or... should I keep being this honest?

I don't know.

But at least tonight, I didn't just rewatch the same show over and over. I’m thinking about these things. Does that count for something?

Sometimes you only truly understand what "companionship" really means.

It’s not about grand gestures or big events; it’s just... the simple things. I got off work early today, picked up some groceries, and spent some time messing around in the kitchen. Just candlelight, a simple meal, and the two of us sitting together.

No phones, no work talk—just chatting, laughing, and those occasional silences that don't feel awkward at all.

I used to think success was all about promotions, numbers, and tangible things. But now I realize that being able to prepare a dinner for someone I care about and seeing her enjoy it... that kind of fulfillment feels so much more grounded.

A bit cheesy, I know, but it’s the truth.

🕯️

**Sunday, April 23, 2023 – Past 11:00 PM**

Another sleepless night, but this one feels different.

The pervasive sense of anxiety is gone. Instead, my mind is looping over today’s work on the user journey map. I spent the entire afternoon in the office mapping out every single stage of our product—where users come from, where they hesitate, where they drop off, and where they find those "aha!" moments. I’ve never worked this way before. In the past, all I looked at were numbers: conversion rates, retention rates—just cold, hard metrics. But today, something clicked.

I’m starting to understand that operations isn't just about data and conversions; it’s about understanding human behavior and needs.

It sounds like a total cliché, but as I was actually drawing it out on paper, looking at those lines and arrows, I felt a sense of clarity I’ve never had before. It was like the question Dr. Wang asked me last time suddenly had an answer—this is what I want. To see an idea turn into a product, to see how users react, and then to refine it. To repeat that process.

When Zhang Hao saw my analysis, he didn't say anything extraordinary. He just nodded and said, "This is a good way of thinking." But the satisfaction I felt in that moment didn't come from being praised—it wasn't that "I did a good job, so I'm great" feeling. It was the realization of "I finally see what truly meaningful work looks like." There’s a huge difference between the two.

But I’m also scared.

I’m scared that this is just a temporary burst of passion. I’m scared that I’ll be like before—signing up for that product course only to quit after two weeks. I’m scared that when I express these ideas to Zhang Hao, he’ll think I’m just being restless again. I’m scared that, in the end, I’ll fall back into that spiral of self-doubt, thinking "I can't do anything right."

It’s past 11:00 PM now. I’m not awake because of anxiety; I’m awake because my head is full of ideas about user behavior. It’s a different kind of insomnia. I’m not even sure if it counts as insomnia.

I need to have a real talk with Zhang Hao in early April. An honest one. Not for a promotion or a raise, but to confirm if he truly supports me deepening my expertise in this direction. I also need to sign up for that course immediately—and this time, I’m actually going to stick with it.

I’m trying to prove to myself that today’s clarity wasn't just an escape, but a direction that actually has roots.

But I’m still a little unsure.

# 2024 Financial Planning - Actionable Version

**Updated: Dec 11, 2023 (Evening)**

---

## Core Objectives
Save enough for a down payment by age 30 + Maintain quality of life + Don't let anxiety take over

---

## The Data Foundation (Rational Breakdown)

**Annual Salary:** 250k (Approx. 180k–190k post-tax)
**Avg. Monthly Disposable Income:** ~15,500 RMB
**Monthly Living Costs:** 5,500–6,000 RMB (Rent 3,500 + Food 1,500 + Misc 1,000)
**Monthly Savings Potential:** 8,000–10,000 RMB ✓

**Target Timeline:** 7 years (By 2030, age 32)
**Target Amount:** 800k (Base for a down payment in a Tier-1 city)
**Required Savings:** Approx. 9,500 RMB/month

---

## Three-Module Allocation Plan

### Module 1: Base Savings (5,000 RMB/month)
- **Liquid Account:** 2,000 RMB (Emergency fund)
- **Fixed Deposits:** 3,000 RMB (Low-risk baseline)
- **Checkpoint:** Auto-transfer at the start of every month.

### Module 2: Investment Growth (3,500 RMB/month)
- **Fund AIP (Automatic Investment Plan):** 2,500 RMB (Primarily Index Funds, some Mixed Funds)
- **Stock Account:** 1,000 RMB (Learning + Trial & Error; no chasing highs)
- **Checkpoint:** Review returns quarterly and adjust allocation.

### Module 3: Quality of Life Maintenance (2,000 RMB/month)
- **Gym Membership:** 400 RMB (Non-negotiable; my emotional outlet)
- **Social/Entertainment:** 800 RMB (Can't cut this entirely or I'll burn out)
- **Date Nights/Partner Time:** 800 RMB (This is important)
- **Checkpoint:** End-of-month evaluation; do not compress this budget.

---

## To-Do: Actions for Next Week

- [ ] **Open Accounts:** Choose 2-3 fund platforms (Alipay / Tiantian / Danjuan).
- [ ] **Research:** Selection criteria for Index Funds (CSI 300? CSI 500?).
- [ ] **Consult:** Find a financial advisor who won't BS me (Ask my partner's friend).
- [ ] **Setup:** Automate transfers (Ensure I don't "forget" to save).
- [ ] **Create:** Monthly Excel tracker (Keep it simple, don't overcomplicate).

---

## Contingency Plans

**What if I get a promotion?** → Increase investment contributions, not spending.
**What if the economy dips?** → Keep the 5,000 RMB base savings untouched; pause investments if necessary.
**What if there's an emergency expense?** → Draw from the emergency fund and replenish by month-end.

---

## Mental Checkpoints

- **1st of every month:** Review last month's savings and adjust this month's budget.
- **End of every quarter:** Re-read this plan to ensure I'm still on track.
- **Every six months:** Discuss progress with my partner and adjust goals if needed.

---

## Final Thoughts

This isn't about becoming a miser. It’s about having the power to choose at 30—whether that’s buying a home, starting a business, or moving to a new city. Every bit saved now is a vote for my future self.

The anxiety hasn't disappeared, but now it has a concrete roadmap. That feels good.

11:45 PM. Insomnia again.

Lying in bed in our new home, I can hear the faint sound of snoring from the next room—that’s her. We’ve been living together for three weeks now, yet I still wake up suddenly in the middle of the night like this, with an indescribable weight in my chest.

I was just scrolling through Xiaohongshu, looking at people sharing their cohabitation lives. Things like "Creating the Perfect Shared Space" or "Our First Home." Looking at those meticulously decorated photos, the anxiety started to kick in—should our home look like that too? Am I not doing enough in this relationship? Why am I always looking at other people's lives and feeling like I’m falling short?

Dr. Wang said this is called the "social comparison trap." I know that. I know it all. But knowing it and doing something about it are two different things.

In the end, I couldn't help myself. Around 1:00 AM, I gently woke her up and said, "I’m feeling a bit nervous right now. It’s not about us, it’s about whether I can really change." I felt a bit embarrassed saying it—it’s not something the old me would have done. The old me would have chosen to hide away, chewing on that anxiety alone until dawn.

She didn't give me some grand lecture. She just said, "You don't need to be perfect. You just need to be real."

Just that one sentence.

I lay there in the dark, repeating those words. "Just need to be real." I don’t think I’ve ever truly believed that. Growing up, all I ever heard was "You need to stand out," "Look at how others are doing it," and "You’re still not good enough." So I’ve been performing—performing enthusiasm at the office, performing a fulfilling life on my Moments, performing success in front of my parents.

But in this new home, by her side, it feels like I don’t have to perform. I can say "I’m scared," I can say "I’m not sure," I can say "I’m really anxious right now." And she won’t write me off because of it.

What does that feel like?

It’s a bit like... release. But also a bit like... fear. Because once I stop performing, I lose the armor of "perfection." I’m just me—an unstable, easily anxious, sometimes fragile 26-year-old man.

I’m still going to the gym tomorrow. It’s a habit I’m sticking to. Not to build a perfect body, but because there, I can vent all my anxiety. On the treadmill, at the weight rack, I am just myself. I don’t have to answer to anyone’s expectations.

A new year, a new home, a new way of living.

I’m learning to change. There will probably be many more nights like this where I wake up suddenly and doubt myself. But at least for now, I’m not carrying it all alone anymore.

At least for now, I know there’s someone willing to listen when I say, "I’m scared."

Maybe this is the beginning of my change.

# Phone Memo

**Effective Questions for Discussing Mistakes with Team Members**
2024.04.03 16:30

Just finished a discussion with Li Ming about his execution gaps this week and discovered a pretty solid conversation framework. Jotting it down for future use.

---

**Key Question Sequence:**

1. **"Where do you think things went wrong?"**
   - Don't blame him directly; let him discover it himself.
   - He knows better than I do where he got stuck.
   - This keeps him from immediately going on the defensive.

2. **"How can I better support you?"**
   - The focus is on 'support,' not 'correction.'
   - Let him know I'm on his side.
   - Maybe the problem isn't him—it might be that my instructions weren't clear enough.

3. **"Let’s look at solutions together."**
   - Pivot to the future; don't get bogged down in the mistake itself.
   - Brainstorm together rather than me just telling him what to do.
   - Get him involved in the resolution process.

---

**Why this works:**
- He doesn't feel judged.
- He’s more willing to be honest about the real situation.
- Next time, he’s more likely to proactively communicate problems instead of hiding them.

---

**Comparison to my old approach:**
Before, I might have asked, "Why did you make this mistake?"—which sounds like an interrogation. This new way feels different. It feels like we're solving a problem together rather than me passing judgment.

The team feedback says my management style is now more "fair and supportive." To be honest, I only recently realized this—management isn't about asserting authority; it's about helping others do better.

---

**To try next time:**
- Have the conversation the same day the issue arises; don't wait too long.
- Have specific support options ready, don't just ask questions.
- Remember to express my confidence in his abilities.

**Decision**:
Based on the analysis, I **will not post anything on Moments**. My current energy and mental state cannot support this kind of social interaction. Even if I felt an impulse to post, the overwhelming sense of exhaustion would stop me.

If I were forced to record something, it would be **private, for my eyes only**—like a memo, a diary entry, or a draft that I save but never send.

---

**My Real Choice**: I won't post anything right now. I’ll just keep sitting here, staring at my phone screen, opening Moments and then closing it again. I don’t have the energy to compose, to perform, or to maintain any kind of persona.

If I had to output anything, it would be these fragmented thoughts looping in my head, jotted down in my Notes app:

---

# Phone Memo

**Sept 28, 2024, 10:00 PM**

I don’t know what the future holds, but I know who I want to be. And that’s enough.

---

I just wrote that down in my notebook. Transcribing it here now, just in case I lose track of it later.

Had a long talk with my partner this afternoon. She asked me what’s been on my mind lately, and I realized I’ve been obsessing over a fake problem—the whole "how do I become successful" thing. As if success has a standard answer and I just need to find it.

But that’s not how it works.

Mom’s been pushing me to buy a house again, with Dad chiming in. It hit me: if I buy a house just to meet their expectations, I’ve already lost. Not because buying a house is inherently bad, but because I’d be giving up the right to define my own life.

So it’s clear to me now—I don’t need to know every step of the future. I just need to know who I want to be. Someone with ideas, someone who dares to say no, someone who can help others. Someone who stays innovative at work and stays authentic in life.

The rest is just figuring out how to practice those things within the constraints of reality. That’s not compromising; that’s being wise.

Dr. Wang was right: anxiety often comes from clinging to "shoulds." I’m starting to let go of those "shoulds" now.

8:15 PM. She’s still in the living room, and I’m in the bedroom. It’s been three days.

I don’t know how to start writing this. Or rather, I do, but I don’t want to admit it.

Her words—"Are you running away again?"—have been looping in my head. I regret my reaction now as much as I was quick to snap back then. "Do you think I should just keep banging my head against a brick wall?" Listen to me. It’s like I had to strike back just because she was "accusing" me. But she wasn't accusing me; she was just asking a question. A question I already knew the answer to deep down.

Am I running away?

Of course I am. Ever since Zhang Hao criticized me, my mind has been drifting toward product management, other companies, even entirely different industries. It’s exactly like when I was in e-commerce—back then, as soon as things got a little rough, I’d start thinking, "Maybe I’m not cut for this," and then I’d jump ship. Here we go again. I haven't even properly reflected on what went wrong this time, and I’m already thinking about "pivoting to product."

She said, "Every time you hit a wall, you just want to change direction."

I walked away coldly at the time. Now I admit, I was angry. Not at her, but at myself. Because she was right, and I hate being right.

But is it really just running away? This operations role... I honestly don't know if I'm a fit. Sometimes I come up with brilliant ideas, and other times I’m just someone who makes rookie mistakes. That kind of inconsistency says something in itself. Maybe switching to PM really is a better choice? At least I could understand users from a different angle instead of constantly crashing and burning at the execution level.

But...

Later, I told her, "I'll try to learn and see how it goes." Even I could hear the lie in my voice. I have no intention of "learning and seeing." I’ve already been scrolling through a ton of "how to pivot to PM" posts on Xiaohongshu. I’m already wondering if I should sign up for a course. I’m running away, but I’m also taking action, yet I have no idea what I’m actually doing.

That’s what hurts the most.

I know I’m running away, but I don't know how *not* to. Stay in operations and keep getting chewed out by Zhang Hao? Keep proving to myself that I’m "just not good enough"? Or... is this just who I am, and no amount of effort will change it? My parents always said I lacked execution; maybe they were right.

And now there’s this. This cold war with her. I’m using silence to punish her for "accusing" me, but really, I’m punishing myself. Because she saw right through me, and I hate being seen through.

I’m exhausted. Tired at work, tired in life. I can’t even clear my head while running at the gym; my brain just keeps spinning. I didn't sleep well again last night.

Maybe I should be the one to say something. But what? "You’re right, I am running away"? And then what? Then I still have to face the question of whether I’m actually cut out for operations, and I don’t have an answer.

I don’t even know if I’m angry or if I’m just scared.

Maybe... it’s both.

**Had a quick chat with Li Ming during tea break, and it turned out to be quite rewarding.**

He mentioned he’s been under a lot of pressure lately, mostly struggling to find the balance between creativity and execution. He feels like he either overthinks things to the point of paralysis or just rushes in blindly and ends up hitting a wall. It sounded all too familiar...

I shared some of the methods I’ve been using lately—like how to avoid being hijacked by anxiety when it hits, how to break down big goals into actionable steps, and the importance of allowing yourself to make mistakes as long as you learn from them. As I was talking, I realized that these insights are things I’ve gradually accumulated through trial and error over the past six months.

Li Ming listened intently and eventually said, "You seem really stable now." That caught me off guard because I know my inner volatility is still there, but it seems I’ve learned how to coexist with it. Or rather, it no longer has total control over me.

**A few reflections:**

I used to think of myself in black and white—either I was "crushing it" or I was "failing." Now, I seem able to accept a more authentic state: I have anxiety, I have uncertainties, but I can keep moving forward through them. And apparently, sharing this experience can actually help others.

Zhang Hao asked me before if I’d be interested in mentoring the newcomers, and I hesitated at the time. Thinking about it now, maybe it’s not an extra burden but an opportunity—a chance to pass on what I’ve learned. Not as someone "perfect" teaching others, but as someone "still growing but headed in the right direction" walking alongside them.

I feel like this is the kind of work state I’m actually looking for. It’s not just about ticking off tasks; it’s about truly helping the people on the team grow.

**Tomorrow, I’ll continue discussing the details of user segmentation with Li Ming. He’s very interested in this methodology.**

# Monday, November 28th, Sunny

Zhang Hao praised my operations plan during the meeting today. He said the logic was clear and the execution was spot on.

I just said "thanks" and went back to my notes.

There was nothing particularly special about the incident itself. But as I sat there, I suddenly realized—I didn't have *that* feeling anymore. That feeling of my heart lighting up the moment I’m recognized. I didn't feel the urge to savor his words, nor was I rushing to tell anyone else.

I just... accepted it calmly.

In the past, I definitely would have replayed that praise over and over in my head. I might have told my partner after work, or obsessed over it at home, terrified that a future criticism might somehow invalidate today’s achievement. Those ups and downs were honestly exhausting.

It’s different now. I knew the plan was good; I felt it while I was working on it. I didn't do it to get Zhang Hao’s approval, but because I felt it was the right way to do things. So his words felt like... a confirmation? But they weren't the deciding factor.

My value doesn't reside in his evaluation.

Even as I say this, I’m a bit surprised. It doesn't sound like me. I used to be so hungry for that kind of validation...

But it makes sense. Life has indeed felt different lately. It’s not that work has become easier, but... my mindset has shifted. Having my partner’s company, those casual connections with neighbors, the peace I find when I’m alone—all these things combined have made me less "starved." I don't need to prove myself through work so desperately anymore.

And then there’s Dr. Wang. Those conversations have helped me slowly see what I’m doing—and why I was always seeking approval.

Now I can work with more composure. It’s not that I’ve given up; it’s just that... it’s finally just work.

The feeling is a bit foreign, but it’s good.

Sometimes a flicker of unease still crosses my mind—am I becoming too detached? Will this affect my career growth? But the moment that thought appears, I can see it for what it is. I can see what’s behind it—that old voice telling me I "must get ahead," a voice from a long time ago.

I can hear it now, but I don't necessarily have to follow it.

This discovery is worth writing down.

September 9, 2022, 11:45 PM

She said, "Accepting your own imperfections is actually the best design."

I read it three times.

The first time, it didn't quite register; I just felt a sharp pang in my chest. The second time, I started asking myself, what is she saying... is she talking about me? By the third time, I realized that... yeah, she really was talking about me.

So I asked her, "How do you know me so well?"

The moment I hit send, I regretted it. It was too blunt. Too revealing. But she didn't laugh at me, nor did she give me some perfunctory response. It was like... she truly saw me.

It’s a strange feeling. I’ve been faking confidence, faking that I have a plan, faking that everything is under control. But deep down, I know I’m pretending. I thought no one could tell, or rather, I thought no one cared. But she seemed to... just see right through it. And she didn't do it with an "I’ve found your secret" attitude; she just calmly said to accept the imperfections.

I thought back to last week when Zhang Hao said I lacked execution, and I completely spiraled. At that moment, I wondered if I really was just incapable of doing anything right. But now... maybe that was just a slip-up in my learning process? Why is it that the same situation feels so completely different depending on who it's coming from?

Zhang Hao’s criticism made me feel like a failure. But her words made me feel like... maybe failure isn't something to be afraid of. Maybe failure is just part of the design.

I don't know if that’s true. I’m still a little afraid that this is just my imagination, or that she’s just trying to comfort me. But right now, in this moment, I want to believe. I want to believe that someone can see the real me, not the polished version.

Maybe... maybe I don't need to keep proving myself.

That thought is a little scary. But also a little... liberating?

I don't know. It’s late, I’m exhausted, but I can’t sleep. My mind is just spinning in circles.

Work again tomorrow. Zhang Hao will probably find something else to nitpick. But maybe... maybe I can look at it all differently.

Maybe.

Another sleepless night, but this time feels different.

I was scrolling through "financial freedom" posts on Xiaohongshu just now, as I usually do. But I realized my mindset has shifted. I used to look at these things as a form of self-torture—someone else achieved financial freedom at 30, what about me? Someone else already bought a house, what about me? That sense of comparison used to fuel an anxiety that kept me awake until 3 AM.

Tonight was different. I saw a discussion about first-time property investment and a question suddenly popped into my head. Instead of my usual "Oh no, I should be doing this too" anxiety, I genuinely wanted to understand—why did this person make that choice? What does this mean for me?

I even took the initiative to leave a comment and ask a question. Someone replied, suggesting I consider dollar-cost averaging into funds as a starting point.

Strangely enough, I felt a sense of peace.

It’s not the excitement of "I finally have the answer," but a deeper calm—like I’ve found something concrete and actionable, rather than a goal hanging over my head that I can never reach. I can start with 100 or 500 yuan. It’s not "I must buy a house by 30," but "I can start taking small, real actions right now."

What Dr. Wang said last time is suddenly starting to make sense. He asked me, "What is your anxiety telling you?" I didn't know how to answer then. Now, I think I understand a little—anxiety isn't saying "you've failed," it's saying "you care about this." The problem is, I’ve always interpreted "caring" as "must be accomplished immediately."

The shift tonight was small. Just a different way of looking at a post. But the feeling is very different.

Late-night scrolling used to be an escape. Now, it feels like reflection.

I think I’ll sleep a little better.

# Work Log - December 18, 2023

**Summary of Performance Review Feedback**

I had my Q4 performance review with Zhang Hao this afternoon. To be honest, I was a bit nervous beforehand, but the whole process went much smoother than I expected.

Zhang Hao mentioned that the execution of recent operational plans has been solid, especially last month’s user retention campaign, where the data exceeded expectations. He used the phrase "a balance between stability and creativity" to describe my recent work state. This took me by surprise—mostly because that’s exactly what I’ve been striving to adjust lately.

**Self-Reflection**

Looking back, I really have changed the way I work recently. I’ve moved away from that "either sprinting full-tilt until 11 PM or spiraling into self-doubt over a minor setback" rhythm. Now, it’s more about: clarifying the goal → making a plan → executing → summarizing feedback. It sounds basic, but this shift has been quite significant for me.

Before, I was always driven by the anxiety of "needing a promotion" or "needing to prove myself," which actually made me prone to careless mistakes. Now that I’m focusing on the work itself rather than the title, my performance has become more stable. It’s a bit ironic, but that’s the reality of it.

**Next Steps**

Zhang Hao asked about my thoughts on career development for next year. Instead of rushing to say something like "I want a promotion," I talked about wanting to go deeper within the current operational framework—like building a more systematic user analysis framework or investing more in team mentoring. He seemed quite interested in that direction.

The overall tone of the conversation felt different. It wasn't "where are you falling short," but rather "what are you doing now, and what do you want to do next." It’s a good feeling.

# 2024.01.18 Morning Notes

It’s 7:30 AM, and she’s still asleep. Instead of reaching for my phone like I used to, I just lie here, listening to the steady rhythm of her breathing.

We’ve been living together for a week now. I’ve noticed I’m sleeping much better lately—from my usual six hours to about seven, and the quality is night and day. It’s no longer that shallow sleep where I’d wake up abruptly in the middle of the night and reflexively grab my phone to scroll through Weibo or Xiaohongshu, reading about other people’s promotions or real estate investments... that sense of anxiety used to linger until dawn.

It’s different now. With her by my side, I don’t need a screen to fill those late-night voids.

But I also wonder: is this a bit dangerous? Am I pinning my entire sense of security on her? Before, I could at least say I was "living independently." Now... I’m a little afraid to answer that question.

I haven't told my parents yet. That’s my biggest source of guilt right now. They still think I’m living alone in this city. They regularly ask me things like, "When are you getting promoted?" "Have you saved any money?" "It’s time to find a girlfriend"—questions that sound a bit ironic now. I *have* found a girlfriend, but I don’t have the courage to tell them. I know they’ll have a million questions: What does she do? What’s her family background? When are you getting married?

I haven't even figured out how to explain this decision. Moving in together feels like I’m moving toward the future they expect—"starting a family." But in reality, this was entirely my own choice; it has nothing to do with their expectations. So why do I still feel so guilty?

Work is another concern. My overtime hours have definitely dropped since the move. I used to stay until 11 PM, but now I want to get home early. Zhang Hao hasn't said anything yet, but I can feel his gaze lingering on me sometimes—that "what’s going on with you lately?" look. I need to make sure this shift isn't interpreted as a lack of commitment.

Maybe I’m overthinking it.

She woke up, stretched, and turned to look at me. "Why are you up so early?" she asked. I couldn't find the words, so I just smiled. She didn't push it; she just leaned in and rested her head on my shoulder.

We lay there like that for a while.

This feeling... I don’t know how long it will last. But at least for now, I’m no longer that person anxiously scrolling through a phone in the dead of night.

Just hung up the phone with Dad. Same old questions—how’s work, when am I getting promoted, when am I buying a house. I didn't have the patience to answer, so I just casually mentioned, "I'm living with a friend lately."

He didn't push further, and that was that.

But now I’m sitting here, replaying that sentence over and over. "Friend." Why did I say that?

My partner and I have been living together for nearly a month now. It’s nothing to be ashamed of. We rented a small apartment, bought furniture together, and we have breakfast together every morning. This was my choice, and I’m sure of it. So why did I still instinctively hide it when Dad asked?

I tell myself "the timing isn't right." I'll tell them when the time is right. But that excuse sounds so familiar—it’s the same one I used back in college when I was avoiding them after failing a class. It’s just like those two years I lived alone; every time they asked about my life, I would subconsciously filter, conceal, and sugarcoat.

When will I finally stop caring about what they think?

No, that’s the wrong question. I *do* care. I’ve always cared. Even though I’ve moved out, even though I have a life of my own, that voice is still there—the way Mom points out my flaws, the expectations in Dad’s silence. They’ve been internalized into my own voice.

So even when no one is supervising me, I’m still policing myself.

My partner asked why I suddenly went quiet. I said it was nothing, just a bit tired. But really, I was wondering: am I repeating the same pattern again? I thought living together would change something, that it would give me that sense of "I’ve grown up, I have a new life." But now it seems I’ve just traded escaping alone for escaping with someone else.

Or rather, am I using this relationship to prove something? To prove I’m not a failure, to prove I’m moving toward "success." But whose definition of "success" is this? Is it mine, or is it the voice of my perpetually dissatisfied mother in my head?

I need to talk to my partner about this. Or Dr. Wang. But for now, I just want to sit here and admit one thing: I am still bound by expectations. The shackles have just changed form.

I am not fully independent. I’m just continuing to run away in a different way.

This realization feels both like a relief and a disappointment.

My hands are shaking a bit as I open this diary. I’m not sure if it’s from exhaustion or something else.

Today, I deleted everything related to my career change. Search history, bookmarked courses, those motivational quotes... I deleted them one by one. As I did, I felt a strange sense of relief, like I was putting down something incredibly heavy. But at the same time, I felt a flicker of fear—as if I were admitting to some kind of failure.

But it’s not that. I think I’ve finally seen things clearly.

Lately, I’ve been constantly asking myself, "Why can't I get anything right?" At first, I thought operations just wasn't for me, so I wanted to switch fields. I signed up for classes, studied for two weeks, and then realized... I was still making the same mistakes. It wasn't because of the profession; it was because I’m too hard on myself. I’m so afraid of failing that I either over-prepare until I’m burnt out or I just give up entirely. Changing careers won't fix that. Changing ten careers won't fix that.

My mother’s words—"You can't do anything right"—have been stuck in my head for a long time. I’ve been holding myself to her standards. Perfection or nothing; there’s no middle ground. So every time I make a small mistake, I feel like a piece of trash. Every time someone else succeeds, I spiral into despair.

Looking back now, it’s just so exhausting. Truly exhausting.

I don’t know when "success" became the only way out. Be middle class by thirty, get promoted, buy a house, "achieve" this, "achieve" that... a whole list of "musts." But none of these "musts" are mine. Or rather, I’m no longer sure which ones are mine and which ones belong to them.

Today, I was thinking that maybe I should change my expectations for myself. Instead of "becoming middle class," maybe it should be... just being able to accept myself. Including my imperfections, my failures, and my vulnerability. Being able to take care of myself. Being able to connect sincerely with the people around me, instead of always putting on a performance.

It sounds like I have no ambition. But I’ve realized that when I’m not so afraid of failing, I actually do better. Because if it’s imperfect, it’s imperfect—I just keep going.

I still have to go to work tomorrow. I’ll still make mistakes. But maybe... I can hate myself a little less.

Maybe change isn't achieved through escaping. It’s achieved through facing it.

March 18, 2024, 10:30 PM

Sitting on the bed, my partner is already asleep. I still can’t get to sleep.

When Zhang Hao called this afternoon, I was actually really happy. It’s been three years, and finally, someone has recognized my work in operations. But that happiness was quickly swallowed by something else—an indescribable fear. I didn't tell my partner right away; instead, I sat in my office alone for half an hour, staring blankly out the window.

When I got home tonight, she asked me what was wrong. I said nothing, just that I was a bit tired. But she knew I was lying. She didn't push; she just sat beside me and asked one simple question: "What are you afraid of?"

Just that one sentence.

Suddenly, my eyes welled up. I told her I’m afraid I’ll become just like my mother—only seeing what people do wrong and never what they do right. I’m afraid I’ll become the kind of person who only knows how to criticize and never how to encourage. I’m afraid I’ll project my own anxieties and insecurities onto my team.

The moment I said it out loud, I realized that this was the real fear. It’s not about being a bad manager; it’s the fear of repeating a pattern—the very pattern I’ve been on the receiving end of since I was a child.

My partner didn't say anything; she just held me. Really tightly. I cried in her arms, the kind of crying I haven't done in a long time.

Later, she said, "The fact that you're aware of it means you won't do it. Your mother wasn't aware."

I don't know if that’s true. But at least I know what I’m afraid of now.

Going from running a project to managing three people—they really are two different things. But maybe this isn't just a career jump. Maybe it’s an opportunity—a chance to see clearly how much of me comes from my mother, and how much is truly me.

I’ll message Dr. Wang tomorrow to schedule a session for next week. I need to talk this through with her.

Also, I need to call Zhang Hao back and tell him I’m taking the position.

2:47 AM

Can’t sleep. I’ve been lying here for an hour, but my mind is still racing.

We sat in the living room for a long time just now. I thought it would be another one of those "keep it up" or "you can do it" pep talks, but she didn't do that. She just asked me—"If you fail, what do you think will happen?"

I hadn't thought about it. Or rather, I’ve been avoiding the question entirely.

I couldn't find the words. Then she asked, "What does failure mean to you?"

And I just cried. I cried like a mess.

Because I realized that I’ve been using failure to define myself. Every time I make a mistake, I feel like my entire being is a failure. If the career change fails, I feel like my life is over. If I mess up once at work, I feel like I’m good for nothing.

She said, "Maybe the problem isn't that you aren't doing well enough. Maybe it’s your definition of 'well' that’s the problem."

Then she said something that I’m still replaying in my head—"Good enough is enough."

I cried again.

Why does that phrase sound so foreign? I’ve never heard anything like it. My mom always said "you have to stand out," my boss says "you have to be better," and my own mind tells me "you must succeed." But "good enough"?

I don't know what that feels like.

Lying here in bed, I’m trying to wrap my head around the concept. What does "good enough" mean? Does it mean I can make mistakes without being a piece of trash? Does it mean I can be something other than the best and still have value?

It sounds too good to be true.

But maybe... maybe that’s why I’m so exhausted. I’ve been trying to prove myself—proving it through success, proving it through perfection. If I fail, the whole proof collapses. That’s why every failure feels like a death sentence.

I don't know how to change this. I still have to go to work tomorrow, there’s still the risk of making mistakes, and that fear is still there. But the questions she asked today, and that phrase "good enough is enough"...

I want to write it down. I’m afraid I’ll wake up tomorrow and forget, and fall right back into that despair.

Maybe I really do need to go talk to Dr. Wang. Not because there’s "something wrong with me," but... maybe I need someone to help me understand what "good enough" actually means.

Still can’t sleep. But it’s different now. It’s not that feeling of wanting to die; it’s... confusion. And a tiny, tiny bit of hope?

I don’t know.

But I want to remember today.
