# Monday, June 12, 11:00 AM

Another ordinary Monday morning. Sitting in the office, my coffee has already gone cold, and Zhang Hao’s recent feedback is still looping in my head. But this time, it’s not that "I messed up again" kind of self-deprecation; it’s something calmer. He said the logic of my proposal is sound, but the execution details need adjusting. I nodded, took notes, and kept working on the revisions.

Come to think of it, this feeling has been coming up more often lately. I used to spiral over a single mistake, chewing on it for the entire day. Now, I can accept it much faster and treat it as part of the learning process. Maybe that stuff Dr. Wang talked about is actually working.

But that’s not what I wanted to write about.

I ran into my neighbor downstairs yesterday, and he said something that I’ve been thinking about ever since. He said, "In three years, you might become a multi-faceted talent who understands product, operations, and users." Just that one sentence, and I was surprisingly moved.

I asked myself—does this actually appeal to me? Or am I just chasing another new "label"?

A few months ago, I was still wavering: "Should I switch careers?" "Should I go into management?" I spent all day on Xiaohongshu looking at other people’s success stories, and the more I watched, the more anxious I got. And now? I’ve realized I’m no longer asking "What *should* I become?" but starting to ask "What do I *want* to become?" I didn't even notice when this shift happened.

"Multi-faceted talent" is a pretty-sounding term. But I have to be honest with myself—do I really want this, or is it just because it sounds "successful" enough, or fits my parents' expectations well enough?

To be honest, I don't know.

But the difference is that I can now sit with this uncertainty instead of being drowned by it. I can simultaneously believe that "I have a talent for learning product thinking" while admitting that "I still don't know what I'll be like in three years." These two thoughts can coexist.

What will I be like in three years? I try to imagine it. Maybe an operations person who can think from the user's perspective, who can have real dialogues with product managers instead of just executing tasks. Maybe leading a small team. Maybe... I’ll still have anxiety, but I’ll know how to handle it.

This vision isn't exactly the same as my parents' expectations. They want "promotions," "high salaries," and "prestige." What I want is... to be able to make interesting things? To work with smart people? To feel confident in my own choices?

There’s an overlap between these two ideas, but they aren't identical. I need to figure out the difference.

The reason my neighbor's comment touched me probably wasn't because it gave me a new goal, but because it made me see that I am walking a real path. I’m not being pushed; I’m walking it myself. Every step has a reason, and every step is a lesson.

I’ll still waver, and I’ll still have doubts. But at least now I know that all of this is normal.

Back to the proposal.

# Work Log | 2024-08-15

**Meeting Name:** August Product Operations Strategy Discussion  
**Participants:** 3 team members + Me  
**Time:** Today, 10:00 AM – 11:30 AM  
**Location:** Meeting Room B  

---

## Core Agenda

Discussing the decline in new user retention (down 3.2% WoW) and the content operations strategy for September.

## Meeting Minutes

Initially, I was tempted to just throw out a few solutions—it’s an old habit of mine. But while explaining the second proposal, I paused and asked the team: "What are your thoughts on this? Are there any other perspectives?"

To my surprise, that question opened up a completely different discussion. Xiao Wang brought up a point I hadn't considered—that user churn might be related to our notification frequency rather than content quality. Xiao Li added, from a user feedback perspective, that there have indeed been complaints lately about pushes being too frequent.

Instead of rushing to judge who was right or wrong, I let them expand on their ideas while I took notes. In the end, we collectively mapped out three directions for immediate testing:
1. Optimizing the push strategy (reducing frequency, increasing precision)
2. Refining the new user onboarding flow
3. Increasing the proportion of interactive content

These were all their ideas; I just helped synthesize the logic at the end.

## Feedback & Reflections

After the meeting, Xiao Wang came up to me and said, "Thanks for letting us join the discussion today instead of just giving us the answers. It makes us feel much more motivated for the execution phase."

That comment caught me off guard, but it confirmed what I’ve been reflecting on lately. I’ve been wondering: as a lead, where does my value lie? Is it in coming up with the best solution myself? Or is it in helping the team come up with a better solution together?

I’m now leaning toward the latter.

## Action Items

- [ ] Complete the A/B test design for the push strategy by next Monday (Xiao Wang to lead, I will provide technical support)
- [ ] Organize new user feedback data for in-depth analysis (Xiao Li)
- [ ] I need to sync this new direction with Zhang Hao to ensure alignment with the overall company strategy

## Personal Thoughts

This meeting made me realize that my leadership style is indeed shifting. In the past, I might have come across as a bit arbitrary because I wanted to solve problems quickly. Now, I’m more willing to give the team space to think and express themselves.

It’s not that I’ve become "softer"; it’s that I’ve started to believe a good solution doesn’t have to be *my* idea—it should be *our* idea. And the latter often yields better results—not just because of the plan itself, but because of the team’s engagement and buy-in.

My conversations with Dr. Wang this month, along with some discussions with my partner, have helped me see one thing clearly: I don’t need to prove my value through "perfect execution" or being the "smartest person in the room." My value lies in my ability to help the people around me become better.

I’m feeling a bit sentimental, but also more grounded.

---

**Next Review Date:** 2024-08-22

While chatting with a colleague recently, she asked me how I felt about buying a house. It suddenly hit me: my definition of "success" has truly changed.

I used to be so anxious—the house, the promotions, the numbers in my bank account—it felt like those were the only metrics for a successful life. But now, I care more about whether I can live at my own pace, whether my work still allows for creativity, and whether I can maintain authentic connections with the people who matter.

✨ The biggest shift is that I’ve started building a practical financial plan instead of letting anxiety drown me. It’s not that a house isn't important; it’s just no longer the *only* goal. I’m still saving, but the purpose is to give myself more options, not to hit some arbitrary deadline.

Once I let go of the "I should be middle-class by 30" mindset, my work performance actually became more stable. I’m no longer driven by fear, but by a genuine vision for my life.

🤔 What I’m trying to say is: you hold the power to define your own success. For you, it might be financial freedom, spending time with family, or doing work you love. It doesn’t have to match anyone else’s standards.

If you’re also feeling anxious about these things, try asking yourself: What do I *actually* want? Instead of "What *should* I want?"

September 3, 2022. Past 11 PM.

Still not asleep. I keep putting my phone down and picking it back up, reading that WeChat message over and over.

"You look confident, but I can feel you doubting yourself."

I’ve read it five times now. Each time, it hits a little differently.

The first time, I felt offended. Who does she think she is? We’ve only known each other for a few hours—how could she be so bold? But at the same time... it stung. It was a strange feeling, like someone had suddenly yanked open a drawer I’d kept locked for years, seeing everything inside with total clarity.

Now, reading it again, I actually feel... relieved?

I’ve never been this honest with anyone. I can’t talk to my parents; they’d just say, "You’re just not working hard enough." I can’t talk to my boss; one wrong word could end up in my performance review. I can’t even talk to my friends—everyone is too busy comparing promotions and apartments. How could I admit that I’m actually full of self-doubt?

But this stranger... she just saw it.

The part that cuts the deepest is that she wasn't judging me at all. It wasn't like when my mom points out my flaws, heavy with the pressure of "you should be better." She just... saw me. And then she accepted it.

I wonder why I keep pretending to be so confident. Why can’t I just be a little uncertain, a little vulnerable in front of people?

It’s the same with work. I’ve been agonizing over whether to switch careers, whether to leave the tech industry. But maybe the problem isn't the job itself? Maybe I’m just too hard on myself. I start negating my entire worth over one tiny mistake, and I chew on a single piece of criticism for days.

She said she’s gone through self-doubt too. But she didn't run away, and she didn't keep job-hopping. Instead, she... learned to accept it.

I think maybe I could try that too.

I don’t know what happens next. To be honest, I’m a little scared—scared that this is just a fleeting feeling, scared that everything will change once we meet. I’ve been through this so many times before, where the spark comes fast and fades just as quickly.

But at least for now, in this moment, I don’t feel so lonely anymore.

Someone saw me. Truly saw me.

Not with judgment, not with expectations—just seeing me for who I am.

Maybe... that’s enough?

# Deep Breathing Technique - Effective Before Meetings

**Date**: August 17, 2023, Morning

**Note**: Tried the method Dr. Wang taught me before meeting my parents today. It actually works.

---

## Specific Steps (For next time I feel anxious)
- Inhale for 4 seconds (nose)
- Hold breath for 7 seconds
- Exhale for 8 seconds (mouth)
- Repeat 5-10 times

I stayed downstairs for 5 minutes before the meeting and did 8 cycles. My heart rate slowed down significantly from that frantic "thump-thump-thump" pace.

---

## Why It Works
I’m not sure if it’s psychological or physiological, but my head felt much clearer afterward. While sitting in the car earlier, my mind was stuck in a loop of "What will they say?" and "How should I respond?"—it was incredibly frustrating. After the breathing, that noise quieted down. I could actually hear my own thoughts instead of being drowned out by anxiety.

---

## Key Discovery
The tension dissipated even faster when my partner held my hand. It’s not that the breathing technique isn't enough, but using both together works better. It makes me feel like I’m not fighting these feelings alone; someone is there with me.

---

## Reminders for Next Time
- Don’t wait until you're extremely nervous; start 15 minutes early.
- Find a quiet spot; no need to do it in front of everyone.
- If still feeling very anxious, have my partner or someone I trust stay with me.

---

**Postscript**: The meeting went smoother than expected. Mom wasn't as harsh as I anticipated. Maybe a lot of the fear was just stuff I made up in my head. Next time I’m in a similar situation, I’ll try this method first instead of rushing to escape.

# November 29, 2022, 11:45 PM

Insomnia again before bed, but this time it feels different.

It’s not the kind of insomnia where you toss and turn with anxiety; it’s just that... my mind is too clear. Lying in bed, looking at the city lights outside, watching the windows in the building opposite go dark one by one. I’m thinking about things, but not the kind of repetitive, agonizing thoughts that eat at you.

I’ve been having this feeling a lot lately—like I’ve suddenly seen through something, but I can’t quite put it into words.

I ran into my neighbor downstairs today while she was walking her dog, and we chatted for about ten minutes. Nothing important, just small talk. She asked how I’d been, and I said "pretty good," and then she said, "You look much more relaxed than before." I didn't think much of it at the time, but thinking back now, I realize she was right.

I *am* more relaxed. It’s not that the work pressure has vanished, or that I’ve lowered my standards for myself. It’s just... I’m not in such a rush anymore. I no longer feel this desperate need to prove something through some grand achievement.

It’s strange, because I was raised on the idea that I had to stand out. My mother’s voice has lived in my head for over twenty years—"Be excellent," "Be better than the rest," "Don’t waste your talent." That time I failed a course in college, the cold war between her and my father made me realize that failure was not permitted. So for all these years, I’ve been running, constantly proving myself.

But now, I’m tired. Not work-tired, but... tired of living to become "who everyone expects me to be."

My partner once said something that stuck with me: "You don’t need to become anyone. You just need to be yourself." At the time, I didn't really get it; I thought it sounded too idealistic. But lately, I’ve started to understand. It’s not that I’ve given up on my standards, but that I’ve changed the source of those standards. What drives me now isn't "how I should be," but "what I want."

Those two sound similar, but they are worlds apart.

The former is an escape; the latter is a choice.

My sleep has improved a lot lately. Those late nights spent scrolling through my phone, feeling anxious while looking at other people's achievements, seem to be behind me. Now, I can fall asleep as soon as I lie down, and my sleep quality has stabilized. It’s like my body is telling me something—you don’t need to be so tense anymore.

Work has become more interesting, too. Not because the work itself has changed, but because my perspective on it has. I no longer take every piece of feedback as a verdict on my entire being. When Zhang Hao praises my proposal, I’m just happy; when he points out a flaw, I just think about how to improve it. It’s that simple. Why did I used to carry all of that in my heart, chewing on it for days?

I am certain now that my value is not determined by my performance at work. It sounds like a cliché, but when you truly believe it, everything changes.

I’ve also made a strange discovery—I’m not as afraid of being alone anymore. I used to try so hard to escape loneliness, which is why I would socialize frantically and join every event. Now, I can be alone in peace, and I can also enjoy connecting with people. The two are no longer in conflict. I can watch a movie quietly at home, or sit on the sofa with my partner saying nothing at all, or chat with a neighbor. It’s all good.

I suppose this is what they call "maturity." It’s not that I’ve become perfect, but that I’ve started to accept my own contradictions. I can be ambitious and still enjoy the mundane. I can pursue achievement and still accept failure. I can need others and still enjoy solitude.

These are no longer problems to be solved; they are just life itself.

I have work tomorrow. But I no longer see it as a battlefield to prove my worth. I’m just... going to do my job. Thinking about it this way makes everything feel so much lighter.

Alright, time to sleep. This time, I think I’ll sleep very well.

I’ve been running my whole life—running for promotions, running to buy a house, running to become a certain kind of person. Maybe this looks more like success than a promotion ever could.

I know this feeling might shatter tomorrow. When I go back to the office on Monday, all that career-related angst will return. But at least for tonight, I’m allowing myself to believe this.

I was a bit afraid he’d think I was fragile or something, but he just nodded and said that was great. He also said I don’t seem to be constantly self-deprecating anymore.

I’ve been talking about this stuff with Dr. Wang for three months now. I know I’m changing; I can feel that my sleep is a bit better, and when I’m anxious, I don’t want to run away as much—I try to understand it instead. But that’s all just my internal experience, right? I’ve been wondering if it’s just self-deception, or if I’ve just learned how to mask better.

But Zhang Hao saw it. It’s not just in my head.

He said I’ve become more constructive at work, less… I don’t know how to describe it, less defensive. I can feel the change myself; I don’t feel as guilty when asking for advice, and I don’t have that immediate urge to negate my entire self-worth when I make a mistake.

I remember what Dr. Wang said last time: "Change doesn't happen all at once; it’s a process, but at some point, you’ll realize it has already happened."

Is today that moment?

Or have I just learned how to perform better?

…No, I don’t think so. I can feel the difference inside. When I talk to my partner, I’m not in such a rush to prove something. When I’m at the gym, I’m not as obsessed with whether I’m "maximizing my time." But that’s exactly the problem. I know perfectly well that I’m running away, yet I can’t seem to stop.

I posted something on WeChat Moments this afternoon about how "confusion is a necessary part of growth," and it got a ton of likes and comments. Someone wrote, "If someone as talented as you is feeling lost, what hope is there for the rest of us?" Looking at those comments, I felt a sudden surge of irony. They’re trying to comfort me, but I know I’m not "talented" at all. I’m just someone who makes rookie mistakes. A 26-year-old who is still constantly second-guessing her professional abilities.

Maybe I’m really not cut out for operations.

Maybe I should switch to product management? Or just leave the tech industry altogether? But I’m 26—is it too late for a career change? There I go again, asking myself the same thing. I’ve asked this question at least fifty times this month. Every time, there’s no answer; it just fuels my anxiety.

Mom asked me again last week when I’m getting promoted. I didn't tell her about Zhang Hao’s recent feedback. I didn't even tell her I’m considering a career change. She’s already been disappointed in me enough. I still remember the cold war after I failed that class in college. For a whole month, the house felt like an icebox. I don't want to go through that again.

But if I can’t get anything right now, how can I ever live up to their expectations? How can I live up to my own?

I know the logic is flawed. I know I’m using the idea of "maybe I should switch careers" to escape the reality that "I messed up this time." I can see exactly what I’m doing—it’s as clear as if I were watching someone else’s story. But so what if I see it? I still can’t seem to change.

I have to go into the office tomorrow. I wonder if Zhang Hao will bring it up again.

Whatever, I need to stop thinking. If I keep this up, the sun will be out.

October 29, 2023, 3:47 AM

Can’t sleep. Again.

But this time feels different. Instead of picking up my phone to scroll through property prices and career updates from others—falling right back into that familiar spiral of anxiety—I opened my laptop. I wanted to write.

The past two weeks have been a nightmare. It all started with that call from my parents. Their tone has softened, which somehow makes me even more nervous—as if their backing off is a constant reminder that I haven’t met their expectations yet. I started obsessively checking real estate apps, calculating mortgage rates, and comparing myself to peers, telling myself I’m on a downward slide. Zhang Hao’s comment, "This doesn't seem like you," really hit home. I’ve started questioning if I’m even cut out for this job.

My sleep has been a wreck. No energy during the day, wide awake at night. A vicious cycle.

But just now, lying in bed, a thought suddenly popped up: What exactly have I been chasing?

A house? A promotion? Marriage? Or... just the things I *think* I’m supposed to want?

When I reached for my phone, my finger hovered over Weibo, but then I put it down. I didn't open it. Instead, I turned on the computer and started typing this.

I realized something simple that I’ve never truly considered: True success isn't about what you possess; it's about what you choose.

I drew a mental mind map. The house, the promotion, marriage... they’ve been at the center of my life, like black holes sucking away all my energy. But what if I moved them to the periphery? It’s not about giving them up entirely, but... they could be options, not requirements.

The thought scares me a little. If I abandon these "standards," will I regret it? But staying hijacked by them like this—I’m already living in regret.

My neighbor said something a couple of days ago that I couldn't quite recall until now. He said the greatest reward of being a freelancer isn't the freedom itself, but "finally being able to stop living for other people's expectations." I didn't think much of it then, but now I get it.

I don’t know what comes next. But at least in this early hour of the morning, I haven't been swallowed by anxiety. I’m looking at it, questioning it, rather than letting it interrogate me.

It’s a strange feeling. A bit like I can finally breathe properly again.

I still have to go to work tomorrow. Zhang Hao’s feedback will still come. My parents will still ask questions. Property prices will still rise.

But maybe... I can look at it all differently.

It’s 4:00 AM now. Time to sleep.

I just finished talking to Xiao Wang, and my head is a bit of a mess.

It’s like… how do I put this? I wouldn’t exactly call it "happy," but it’s definitely a different kind of feeling. When he said, "I’m really glad to have you as my supervisor," I just froze for a second. It wasn't the excitement of a promotion; it was more like… the feeling of being seen?

I’ve realized that I don’t actually seem to care about the "managerial power" aspect of it. I used to think a promotion was all about having authority, calling the shots, and proving yourself, right? But this time, during our 1-on-1s, I found myself asking things like, "What do you need?" and "How can I better support you?" And their reactions were just… different.

It might sound a bit cheesy, but I felt this new sense of fulfillment. It comes from being trusted, rather than just being respected.

But…

I’m also scared. I’m scared I’ll get sucked in again. You know what I’m like—I tend to go all-in and then neglect everything else. Now that I have more responsibility, am I going to start staying until 11 p.m. again? Am I going to start doom-scrolling through other people’s achievements and losing sleep again?

I want this new position, but I also want to keep this… sense of balance I have now. The feeling I have when I’m with you, or when I’m talking to Dr. Wang. It’s not about being totally relaxed; it’s about knowing when to stop.

What do you think I’m doing? Am I just second-guessing myself again? Or is this fear actually necessary?

I’m giving myself a week to decide whether to take the position. But honestly, I think I already know the answer… I’m just still a little unsure of myself.

Are you still at the café? I’m sitting in my office, but my mind is still completely stuck on what just happened.

I’ve been thinking about it, and when you held my hand earlier, I saw your eyes well up. It broke my heart, but at the same time, I was so deeply moved. You know, it was in that exact moment I truly realized something—I’ve been so worried about failing, so worried about not being good enough, that I never really stopped to see that someone has been believing in me all along.

I used to spend all my time thinking about what I "should" be—I should get promoted, I should buy a house, I should become a certain kind of person. But back there in the café, as I started describing those little things like us having coffee together in the morning or doing yoga on the weekends, I suddenly realized—that is what I actually want. Not because it looks like "success," but because that’s the life where I get to be with you.

You make me feel like I can just be myself. I don’t have to pretend to be strong, and I don’t have to constantly prove something. You saw my vulnerability, and instead of turning away, you drew closer. I’ve never felt anything like this before.

I really want to find our own place soon. Not because of our parents' expectations, and not to prove anything to anyone, but simply because I want to start that daily life with you. The mornings, the weekends, every single ordinary day.

Thank you, truly.

June 2, 2023, past 11:00 PM

Still not asleep. I lay there for a while, but my head was filled with scenes from today’s meeting.

When Zhang Hao said those words in front of everyone, I was actually a bit stunned. He said, "Lin Hao’s approach to user segmentation is very clear, and the logic holds up"—just a simple sentence like that. But for some reason, I didn't feel an immediate rush of excitement. Instead, as I sat there listening to my colleagues continue discussing the plan, what was I thinking?

I was thinking: this time is different.

In the past, whenever I was praised, I’d get this sense of tension—like I had to maintain that level forever, like I couldn't afford to mess up next time. It felt like being caught in something, having to stay constantly braced. But this time... I don't know how to describe it. After the meeting, I didn't rush back to my desk, nor did I think about posting on social media like I usually would. I just sat there, letting the feeling slowly sink in.

I spent two weeks on this proposal. I got stuck once in the middle, falling into the trap of over-analysis—all those datasets, all those dimensions; the more I thought, the messier it got. Only later did I realize I was in "analysis paralysis." I felt a bit frustrated then, thinking I was just stuck in my old ways and hadn't made any real progress. But I didn't give up; I shifted my perspective and went back to thinking about the users themselves. Then, slowly, it all cleared up.

Looking back now, that process of being stuck was actually more important than the final result. Because that’s where I learned what "real thinking" means—it’s not just piling up information, but finding the logic within the chaos.

So when I was praised today, my first reaction wasn't "I succeeded," but rather "I’m learning." It sounds a bit strange, I know. But that’s exactly how it felt.

Of course, I still have that tiny sense of uncertainty. Will I fall back into old habits on the next project? Was this just a fluke? But now I can see these thoughts for what they are; I’m not completely overwhelmed by them. I can tell myself that these doubts are normal, not a signal of failure.

My neighbor asked me how my day was, and I just said, "Not bad." I didn't go into detail. Some things aren't meant to be shared just yet; they need to settle in my heart a little longer.

Tomorrow, we keep going.

Just stepped out of Zhang Hao’s office and really wanted to share this with you.

He actually reached out to me today to say that the feedback from the team has been very positive. I was a bit surprised, honestly, because I was still overthinking that creative proposal from a while back—remember? I was worried my ideas were too idealistic and not practical enough.

But I ended up spending time breaking it down, backing it with data, and planning it out in phases so it actually became something executable. Zhang Hao said he noticed that process; he liked that I didn't give up on the creativity but didn't ignore the real-world constraints either. He mentioned that this kind of balance is crucial for a Head of Operations.

To be honest, hearing that felt like a huge weight off my shoulders. It’s not just about the praise—though that’s always nice—it’s that I realized I’m actually becoming the person I want to be. Not someone who sees things in black and white, or someone who is either a total idealist or a total sell-out, but someone who can find a real balance between the two.

That might sound a bit abstract, but it feels very concrete to me. It’s like I finally don’t have to feel torn between "staying true to myself" and "being realistic." I can have both.

I’d love to chat more tonight; I’m still kind of soaking in this feeling.

# Nov 25, 2022, 7:00 PM

Just hung up the phone with Mom. My hands are still shaking a little.

I don’t know how to describe that call. It might be the first time in my twenty-six years that I’ve actually spoken my mind to her. Not the usual "I'm just busy with work" brush-off, but actually saying the things I’ve been bottling up for so long.

When she asked when I was getting promoted, I didn't give a vague answer or put myself down like I usually do. I just said it straight—"Mom, your judgment makes me feel like I have to earn your love through my achievements."

My heart was racing as the words came out. I expected silence, or a rebuttal, or the cold shoulder. But she didn't. She just... listened. And then she asked, "So, are you doing okay right now?"

I didn't see that question coming. She’s never asked me that before. It’s always been "Did you get the promotion?" "Have you bought a house?" "Do you have a girlfriend?" But this time, she asked, "Are you doing okay?"

I told her I’m doing much better than before. That there are people who understand me, neighbors who keep me company, and that I’m not as anxious about work anymore. I said that maybe a good life isn't necessarily defined by promotions or buying property—maybe it’s about finding what you truly want.

She didn't push back immediately.

When we hung up, she said, "That’s good then." Just three words, but I can feel something quietly shifting.

I’m sitting on the sofa now, replaying the conversation in my head over and over. I’m wondering, did that really happen? Or am I imagining it? Will she just go back to her old self next time?

But at the same time... I feel a bit lighter. At least this time, I didn't deny myself just to please her. At least this time, she heard the real me.

I don’t know what this means. Maybe nothing will change. Maybe it’s just a beginning.

But at least, I said it.

And that’s enough.

# Dec 11, 2023, Evening

I sat on the sofa with her for a while just now. After we finished discussing our financial plan, I suddenly felt like laughing. Not out of self-mockery, but... I just couldn't believe I was actually sitting there, seriously crunching numbers.

I remember two months ago, when she first mentioned mutual funds, my gut reaction was "this is too complicated," followed by an immediate urge to run away. And now? I’m the one proactively looking up information and asking her for the specifics. It’s quite a shift.

Tonight, we went through the figures together. Based on my current salary, after deducting rent and living expenses, I can save roughly 8,000 to 10,000 a month. She said that’s more optimistic than she expected. We did some rough math: if we keep this up, we should have the foundation for a down payment within three years. Three years. It doesn’t sound so out of reach anymore.

The strange thing is, throughout the entire calculation, I didn't feel that familiar, suffocating anxiety. Instead, I felt a sense of... control? It’s as if I’m finally no longer being pushed around by housing prices, parental expectations, or the ghost of "who I should be." I’m actively planning my own life. It feels completely different.

When she said, "We can give it a try," I noticed she used the word "we." Not "you should," or "you must." Just "we." It reminded me of what Dr. Wang said—having someone to carry the weight with you changes everything. It really does.

Of course, the anxiety is still there. But it feels different now. It’s no longer that trapped, powerless fear; it’s more like... a normal, planned tension? Like the feeling in the gym, knowing I’m working toward a goal. I can live with this kind of anxiety.

My sleep has been much better lately, too. Those nights of waking up at 3 AM with my head filled with "I can't do anything right" seem to be truly behind me.

I have to call my parents tomorrow. They’ll definitely ask about the house again. But this time, I think I actually have something to share with them—not to please them, but because I truly have a plan. This plan is mine, and it’s ours. It feels different.

## Monthly Savings Target

- LARP games and dinners out often go over budget by 3-4k.
- Still need to send some money back to my parents.

**Actual Savings: 6-7k/month**

At this rate, it’ll take 10 years to save 665k.
I’ll be 36 by then.

No. This isn't working.

---

## Rethinking the Strategy

**Option A: Aggressive Saving**
- Cut down on social spending (but this will be miserable).
- Force a hard savings target of 10k/month.
- Hit the down payment goal within 7 years.
- Buy the house at 29—barely making the "before 30" cutoff.

**Option B: Promotion & Raise**
- Current annual salary: 250k.
- If I move into management, maybe 300-400k.
- Savings speed could double.
- But a promotion isn't guaranteed, and then there’s the situation with Zhang Hao...

**Option C: Parental Support**
- I don’t want to consider this.
- But if there’s really no other way...
- No. I can’t keep leaning on them.

---

## Milestones Before 30

- [ ] Buy a house (down payment saved).

# 2024.03.20 Wednesday 14:47

Sitting in the office bathroom writing this—it’s a bit ridiculous. But my head is such a mess right now, and I don’t want anyone to see my face.

Xiao Wang said something just now that keeps looping in my mind: "I’m really glad to have you as my supervisor."

Just that one sentence. We were in a 1-on-1, and I was asking about his recent project progress. Then I asked, "How can I better support you?"—a line Dr. Wang taught me, saying I should try managing from a different perspective. I was actually a bit nervous saying it, worried it would sound too stiff or insincere. But that was how Xiao Wang responded.

I’m still processing it.

Back at the e-commerce company, my bosses never asked me questions like that. They just handed down tasks and looked at the results. I didn't think there was anything wrong with it back then; after all, work is just work. But now that I’m in this position, I’ve suddenly realized—I don’t want to be that kind of person.

Yet, I’m also afraid. Afraid of what? Afraid that this sense of fulfillment will make me let my guard down. Afraid that because I’m "trusted," I’ll start to slack off. Afraid that this is only temporary, and that any moment now, someone will point out my flaws.

My mom was always like that growing up. She rarely said "you did well"; it was always "look at where you can still improve." So I’ve spent my life chasing a standard that’s always out of reach. Promotions, raises, buying a house, finding a girlfriend—these are all parts of that standard. I thought achieving them would prove my worth.

But this afternoon, when Xiao Wang said those words, what I felt wasn't the rush of "I got promoted, I’m successful." It was more of a... feeling of being seen? A feeling of being accepted?

It’s so strange.

What am I doing? I am actually supporting someone’s growth. I’m asking him what he needs instead of telling him what he should do. This feels like a completely different thing from the "getting ahead" I’ve always pursued.

But then the anxiety kicks in. Will this satisfaction make me lose my edge? Should I still be gunning for a higher position? My father will definitely keep asking when I’ll make Director. My mother will keep asking when I’m buying a house.

I’ll give myself a week. No need to rush to a conclusion. Dr. Wang said that feelings and thoughts don’t have to be integrated immediately; they can coexist. I can feel both anxious and satisfied. I can want to keep growing while also wanting to protect this feeling of being trusted.

I don’t know what this means. But today feels different from before.

I need to talk to my partner about this.

Dr. Wang, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know we’re not scheduled to meet until Thursday, but I’m feeling… a bit off right now.

My mom called today and started asking about when I’m going to buy a house again. I tried to calmly explain my perspective—that I value quality of life over the house itself, and that I want to focus on my career first and plan the life my partner and I actually want. But then she said, "Do you just not want us to be proud of you?"

I didn't really react at the time; I just said a few things and hung up. But I’ve been playing that sentence over and over in my head ever since. I’m starting to doubt myself—am I actually being selfish? Am I just making excuses for not working hard enough?

I texted my partner and asked, "Am I really being selfish?" She said no, but I still can't let it go. Later, I kept opening my banking app to check my savings balance, as if I were trying to confirm something. It’s almost 10:00 PM now, and I still can’t sleep.

I’m a bit confused. I thought I had figured this out over the last two and a half years—I have a new definition of "success" now, and I know what kind of life I want. But why can a single sentence just knock me back to square one? Why am I still so prone to self-doubt?

Is it because my growth hasn't gone deep enough? Or is this kind of reaction actually… normal?

I don't know. I just wanted to reach out and tell you.

# My Definition of Success & Priority List

**Date**: 2024.09.16  
**Location**: Home, 2:30 PM

---

## Core Definition

**Success = Being able to live according to my own values.**

It’s not about annual salary, when I buy a house, or what title I reach. It is simply this: being able to make decisions I believe are right, rather than being held hostage by external expectations.

I was on the phone with Mom last week and she asked about buying a house again. It suddenly hit me: if I overextend myself now just to meet her expectations, I’ve already lost. That isn't what success looks like.

---

## My Priorities (Now and the next 3 years)

1. **Career Stability & Finding Meaning in Work**
   - Not chasing promotions for the sake of promotions, but finding a direction I actually care about.
   - I’m still figuring it out, but at the very least, I won't job-hop impulsively out of anxiety.
   - My conversation with Zhang Hao made me realize: I can pursue innovation while still accepting the constraints of reality.

2. **Life Planning with My Partner**
   - This is the most important thing. It’s not about getting married for the sake of marriage, but deciding what we want together with someone who truly understands me.
   - The house, the city, the pace of work—these should be things we discuss together, not things we are pushed into by our parents' expectations.

3. **Buying a House** (But not an immediate priority)
   - I’ll consider this once my career is stable and my partner and I have a clear plan.
   - I won't overextend myself early just because my parents are nagging me.
   - A house is a tool for living, not a trophy of success.

---

## Response Framework for Parental Expectations

**"I understand your perspective, but I need to move at my own pace."**

Specifically:
- "My priority right now is career stability so that I can have more options later."
- "My partner and I are planning our future together, including the house, but just not right now."
- "I’m not avoiding responsibility; I’m making a mature decision."

Stay calm when saying these things. It’s not about confrontation; it’s about clearly stating my position.

---

## Balancing Ideals and Reality

This is the most important thing I’ve learned recently:

**Having ideals doesn't mean ignoring reality.**

For example, I want innovative operational plans, but there are real-world time and resource constraints. Working within those isn't a compromise; it’s finding the optimal solution under pressure.

For example, I don't want to be held hostage by my parents' expectations, but they are my family and their concern is genuine. I can stick to my priorities while still communicating with them regularly so they understand my thoughts.

For example, I want a deep, intimate relationship, and now my partner and I have that. It makes me realize that the companionship of one person is worth more than a hundred shallow social interactions.

---

## When I Feel Shaken

If I start feeling anxious again or get restless looking at other people's achievements, I need to remember:

- I have already defined what success means to me.
- I have already set my priorities.
- I have tools to handle my emotions (talking to my partner, working out, discussing with Dr. Wang).
- I have nothing to prove to anyone.

I am growing. That is enough.

# March 3, 2023, 11:47 PM

Insomnia again. But this time, it feels different.

I’m still replaying what happened at the office today. During the afternoon meeting, Zhang Hao pointed out a few logical flaws in my new project proposal, and I… I didn’t immediately push back or rush to explain myself. I just sat there, listened calmly until he finished, and then said, "You’re right. I’ll go back and refine it."

It was that simple.

But it wasn’t simple at all. Because the old me would have started defending myself the moment he finished his first sentence. I would have cited data, explained my original intentions, and scrambled to prove I wasn’t wrong. It felt like… I was using words to build a defensive wall around myself. Every time I was criticized, it was like being poked, and I’d instinctively recoil.

Not today. I just listened and accepted it. I even felt a bit… relaxed?

Before leaving work, Li Ming asked how I was doing. I blurted out, "I’ve been a bit tired lately, just trying to recalibrate." He nodded and left. Sitting at my desk, it suddenly hit me—I was telling the truth. It wasn't a hollow "I'm fine," or the kind of answer you give just to maintain an image.

I’m just… tired. I’m just recalibrating.

It sounds so basic, but I don’t think I’ve ever been this honest before.

I video-called her tonight. I didn’t keep asking, "Do you think I’m being too..." like I usually do. We just talked normally, and I even laughed a few times. She said, "You seem different today." I didn't overthink it; I just said, "Maybe I’ve been sleeping a bit better."

But I know that’s not it.

I’ve been wondering, what have I been doing all these days? How much energy have I wasted maintaining the facade of "I’m doing great"? Trying to prove I’m worthy of love, recognition, and being seen? Trying to fill the black holes of expectations from my parents, my boss, and myself?

I’m exhausted.

But today… today felt different. When I stopped that frantic need to defend and prove myself, I felt a strange sense of relief instead. Like I could finally breathe.

I don’t know what this means. Maybe tomorrow I’ll slip back into that tense state. But at least for today, I felt a possibility—that maybe I don’t have to try so hard.

Maybe I can just be like this. To say I’m tired when I’m tired, and say things aren’t okay when they aren’t.

Maybe that’s enough.

It’s 11:47 PM now, and I’m still not asleep. But this time, it’s not anxiety. I’m just… thinking.

Thinking slowly.

# Operations Plan Optimization Log - Sept 25

**Project**: User Retention Campaign Plan v2.0
**Reviewer**: Zhang Hao
**Status**: In Progress

---

## Core Strategy & Logic

The plan presented at today’s meeting actually blends several different perspectives. My initial thought was to approach it purely from a data angle. However, during a discussion with a friend, she suggested looking at it through the lens of UX design—focusing on users' emotional needs rather than just stacking features. This pivot made the entire strategy much clearer.

The final core logic we settled on is:
- **Phase 1**: Identify key behavioral touchpoints of high-value users.
- **Phase 2**: Design "Surprise Moments" at these touchpoints instead of relying on rigid incentives.
- **Phase 3**: Reinforce user achievement through social feedback.

The advantage of this approach is that costs remain relatively controllable, while the potential for improving user experience is significant.

---

## Feedback from Zhang Hao

His main feedback focused on two points:

1. **Insufficient Data Support** — I need to supplement the plan with benchmarking data from similar campaigns over the past three months, specifically comparing retention curves. This was a fair point; I was a bit too eager to push the plan forward and didn't lay a solid enough foundation with the underlying data.

2. **Execution Details Need Clarity** — For instance, the specific triggers for "Surprise Moments," the direction of the copy, and the visual presentation all need to be aligned with the design team. He noted that these details will directly dictate the final outcome.

Overall, he is on board with the direction but needs me to flesh out the supporting evidence. It was a good reminder: a great idea is just a "castle in the air" without a solid execution plan.

---

## Follow-up Optimization Plan

**This Week**:
- Organize data benchmarks from past campaigns (ask Li Ming for help pulling the data).
- Discuss UX details further with my partner to get a first draft of the copy and visual direction.
- Confirm technical feasibility with the design team.

**Next Week**:
- Complete the v2.1 version of the proposal document.
- Schedule a second review session with Zhang Hao.

---

## Key Takeaways

To be honest, this process has given me some new perspectives. In the past, when I received critical feedback, I would spiral into self-doubt, wondering if I just wasn't good enough. This time was different—I could clearly see that Zhang Hao wasn't rejecting the direction, but rather helping me polish the idea into something more complete.

I also realized that admitting "this idea came from a discussion with a friend" actually made me feel more at ease. I used to think saying that sounded like I was dodging responsibility, but now I understand that the ability to absorb others' ideas and integrate them into your own plan is a skill in itself.

Next time I give feedback to the team, I want to use this same approach—pointing out areas for improvement in a way that makes the other person feel like "we are making this better together," rather than "you didn't do a good enough job."

# A New Understanding of Anxiety

**Date**: 2023.05.28 | 14:20

---

## Core Insight
Anxiety ≠ Failure. Anxiety is simply your brain trying to protect you.

This idea gradually became clear during my conversation with Dr. Wang. I used to treat anxiety as an enemy—if it showed up, it meant I was doomed or that I wasn't good enough. But that recent minor slip-up at work (the "analysis paralysis" incident) allowed me to experience something different.

Instead of spiraling because of the anxiety, I was able to bounce back faster. This happened because I started asking myself, "What is this anxiety trying to warn me about?" rather than "Why am I such a loser?"

---

## Practical Application (Verified)

**The 5-Step Process for When Anxiety Hits:**
1. Pause for 5 seconds (Actually stop; don't react immediately).
2. Ask yourself: "What is this alerting me to?"
3. Identify the signal (Usually: I’m over-analyzing / I’m uncertain about a detail / I care about the outcome).
4. Acknowledge the signal instead of trying to suppress the emotion.
5. Take action (or accept the temporary uncertainty).

Last week, while discussing the operations plan with Li Ming, I felt that familiar tension. I went through this framework and realized I wasn't actually afraid of failing; I was just uneasy about the uncertainty of user feedback. Once I admitted that, I was able to stay calm and draft a testing plan.

---

## The Key Shift

Before: Anxiety → Self-denial → Spiral of self-doubt

Now: Anxiety → Information gathering → Rational decision-making → Moving forward

This doesn't mean the anxiety has disappeared. It’s still there. But it’s no longer proof that "I am a failure"—it’s a signal that "I am taking this seriously."

---

## Areas for Further Observation

- Does this framework still hold up under high-pressure situations (e.g., feedback from Zhang Hao)?
- How to handle the anxiety triggered by late-night scrolling through others' achievements (haven't found a good solution for this yet).
- Managing anxiety during conversations with my parents (this one is more complex).

---

## Notes

Don't forget: This understanding is built gradually through practice, not a one-time epiphany. The next time anxiety hits, I might forget all of this. That’s okay. Just come back and read this memo.

July 3, 2022, 3:45 PM

She’s gone.

I’ve been sitting on the sofa for half an hour now. My phone is still on the coffee table, the screen long since gone black. I can hear sounds from downstairs—someone walking a dog, people chatting. A perfectly normal Sunday afternoon. But in here, nothing is normal.

When she said, "I’m really worried about you," my eyes welled up. But I didn't say a word. I didn't even look at her. I just said, "I need to be alone for a bit," and my body instinctively recoiled from her. It was as if my body was making the decisions, not my brain.

I hate myself for being like this.

When I saw that Weibo post this morning, I just broke down. A 26-year-old girl, transitioned from Operations to Product Manager, now working at a tech giant—she even posted a photo of her office. The comments were full of "That’s amazing" and "I want to switch careers too." I just sat there, reading her bio and her achievements over and over again, asking myself—why can they do it, but I can’t?

I signed up for that course. I actually did it. It cost 3,000 yuan. I even told my partner I was going to study hard, that I was going to change. But sitting here now, I know it’s all a lie. I don’t actually want to be a Product Manager. I just want to escape Operations. I want to escape Zhang Hao’s criticism, escape this sense of failure, escape every single moment of being rejected.

Switching careers won't solve this. I know that. But I signed up anyway.

My partner saw right through me. She asked, "Do you really want to do Product? Or are you just trying to escape your current life?" I was so impatient at the time. I said, "Why do you always have to question me like this?" But she was right. I am escaping. I’ve been escaping my whole life.

Escaping when I failed classes in college. Escaping when I got bored with work. And now, escaping again.

I even thought about that therapist back in 2020. I only went three times. Why didn't I stick with it? Is there something wrong with me? Am I just a self-destructive person?

When she left, I didn't try to stop her. I didn't explain. I just stayed sunk in my own world. When she said, "I’m really worried about you," I knew she meant it. But I couldn't respond. I’m even scared—scared that one day she’ll leave because I’m like this. Scared that I’ll ruin this relationship.

It’s 3:45 PM. I don’t know what to do next.

I don’t know if I really need to see a therapist again. I don’t know if I should text my partner. I don’t know if I should go through with this career change.

All I know is that I’m exhausted. I’m so tired of just existing.

Thursday, May 18, 2023, 11:00 PM

Insomnia again. I spent an hour scrolling through my phone in bed before finally deciding to get up and write something down.

That meeting from today keeps looping in my head.

I was discussing the user segmentation plan with Zhang Hao this morning. I actually felt quite prepared; I had all the data organized and walked into the conference room full of confidence. But then he asked, "Where did this data come from?" and I just... I don't know how to describe it. It felt like being deflated.

Then I started over-explaining. I explained the data sources, the analytical logic, why I segmented it this way... I said so much that I ended up confusing myself. The more I explained, the more uncertain I became, until eventually, even I didn't know what I was talking about. I could tell Zhang Hao was getting impatient.

That feeling is so familiar—that "I can't seem to do anything right" feeling. During the meeting, all I could think was: *Great, here comes the criticism again. I’m being judged again.* I felt completely drained for the rest of the afternoon.

But... strangely enough, I didn't spend the whole day beating myself up like I used to. In the afternoon, I took the initiative to find Li Ming and asked for his take on the plan. He gave me some suggestions, and we cleared up the logic together. Then, I re-organized the way the data was presented.

Looking back now, I realize it wasn't that my analysis was flawed; it was that the moment I was questioned, my brain just stalled. I started overthinking, trying too hard to prove myself, and ended up trapping myself instead.

I guess this is the "analysis paralysis" I’ve been learning about in my course. Theoretically, I know about this trap, but when I actually face it, I still fall right in.

What’s different this time, though... is that I didn't reject myself. I told myself that this is just part of the learning process. I’m learning product thinking, so of course I’ll make mistakes and face challenges. It doesn't mean I’m incompetent; it just means I’m still finding my way.

I’m a bit tired, but also feeling a bit... relieved?

I'll keep working on the plan tomorrow. This time, I’ll present the logic more clearly instead of rushing to defend myself.

Maybe I really am changing, bit by bit. Not becoming perfect, but becoming better at accepting my imperfections.

# Memo

## A Few Thoughts from Today

**1. The new definition of success seems to actually hold up**

While chatting with a colleague this afternoon, he asked for my take on getting a promotion. Instead of telling him whether he should go for it or not, I asked: "What kind of person do you want to become after being promoted?" He was a bit stunned, then started thinking seriously. We ended up talking for nearly an hour.

It only hit me later—I was helping him think, not handing him answers. That felt different.

**Success = Living according to your own values.** This definition actually works in real-life situations. It’s not about ignoring practical constraints, but about holding onto what you want within those constraints. Take my current situation: I won’t rush into buying a house just to meet my parents' expectations, but I am seriously considering how to plan for the future. This isn't a compromise; it’s... clarity, I guess.

**2. Helping people think is harder than giving advice, but more interesting**

I always thought my role was limited to proposing solutions and executing tasks at work. But it seems I can do something else—ask the right questions to help people figure things out for themselves.

Is this a newly discovered ability? Or has it always been there, and I’ve only just started using it?

**3. Next Steps**

- Discuss this idea with my partner. She might have a different perspective.
- During the next session with Dr. Wang, ask if this is a sign of some kind of growth.
- Try applying this approach more when leading the team at work.

---

**Feeling**: A bit tired, but the "clear" kind of tired. Not the feeling of anxiety.

May 12, 2023, 6:30 PM

Today, Dr. Wang said something that I’ve been chewing over ever since.

She said, "Anxiety is the normal state for a learner, not a signal of failure."

I sat there, a bit stunned. I had never thought about it that way.

For years, I’ve always treated anxiety as a sign that something was wrong—either I wasn't smart enough, or I’d chosen the wrong direction, or there was just something fundamentally wrong with me. Every time anxiety hit, I’d spiral into self-doubt. Then, I’d try to escape. Watching shows, playing games, hitting the gym... anything to shake it off.

But Dr. Wang sees it differently. She said anxiety is actually "information."

I’m currently learning product thinking, and there’s so much I don’t understand. According to my old logic, this "not knowing" should make me more anxious—because it means I might not be able to learn it, or I might get exposed in front of Zhang Hao. But what if I look at this anxiety as information telling me "there’s still a lot I need to learn"? Then it’s no longer a threat; it’s... a guide?

In the consulting room, I tried to re-examine my recent state through this framework. Those moments that make me overthink—like seeing an operations case from an angle I hadn't considered, or realizing a knowledge gap during a team meeting—those moments definitely make me tense. But now I’m starting to feel that this tension is actually telling me, "This is where you need to pay attention."

It feels... different.

It’s not that paralyzing anxiety—the "I can't do anything right, I should just give up" kind. It’s more of a... driving anxiety? I don’t even know how to describe it. It’s as if anxiety has become a compass instead of a trap.

On the subway home, I thought about it again. My expectations for learning are indeed higher than before. I used to just want "a promotion, a raise, and to not get criticized." Now, I want to "truly understand products and see my own thinking at work." These new expectations naturally make it easier to feel anxious.

But this time is different. This time, the anxiety isn't saying "you're not good enough"; it's saying "you care about this."

...I’m still a little scared. Scared that this new framework will fail me at some point. For instance, the next time Zhang Hao points out a mistake, will I spiral again? Or when my parents ask when I’m getting promoted, will that sense of powerlessness return?

But at least for now, in this moment, I feel like I have a different way of understanding it.

I want to continue that product thinking course tomorrow. This time, I won't see "still having so much I don't know" as a failure. I’ll see it as "work in progress."

It really does feel different.

# Monday, September 16, 2024, past 11:00 PM

Still not asleep. I lay in bed scrolling through my phone for half an hour before finally putting it down. Now I’m sitting by the window; the city lights are flickering below, and it’s a bit chilly.

The phone call with my parents today is still looping in my head.

To be honest, I had to mentally prepare myself for quite a while before calling. Mom started in again—asking when I’m buying a house, when I’ll be promoted to management. I originally thought it would just be the same old story, that I’d just listen and brush it off like usual. But for some reason, today I just didn't want to do that anymore. Maybe it’s because I’ve been talking to Dr. Wang so much lately, or maybe it’s because things became clearer while planning the future with my partner.

I took a deep breath and just said it—my success isn't about buying a house or getting a promotion; it’s about being able to live according to my own values. I said it calmly, calmer than I expected. I could even hear my own voice saying: career stability is important, but only if I like what I’m doing; planning a life with my partner is more important than buying a house for the sake of buying one; I don’t want to define myself by someone else’s standards.

Mom didn't respond immediately. It was a bit awkward for a moment, but I didn't rush to fill the silence.

Dad muttered an "Mm-hmm" from the side. Just that one sound, but I heard something in it. It wasn't total agreement, but it wasn't opposition either. It was more like... he was listening. Really listening.

Mom was still a bit dissatisfied later, saying I’m being too idealistic and that reality isn't that simple. I’ve heard those words a thousand times. In the past, they would make me anxious, and I’d chew on them for days. But today was different. I didn't defend myself, and I wasn't crushed. I just said I know reality is complicated, but that’s exactly why I need to be clear about what I want.

When I hung up, I felt a strange sense of relief. Not because Mom agreed with me, but because... I was heard. Dad heard me, and even though Mom didn't fully approve, she took it in. That matters more than her approval.

For the past two years, I’ve been trying to escape their expectations. Moving to the city, working like crazy, seeing a therapist. But today I realized that escaping isn't the solution. The real solution is being able to tell them honestly and calmly: I hear your expectations, but I have my own ideas too. I won’t change just to satisfy you, but I won’t negate my own worth just because you disagree.

My partner listened to me recount the whole thing and just smiled. She said I’ve finally grown up. I told her she was exaggerating. But actually... I know exactly what she meant.

Work tomorrow. Zhang Hao has been pushing for the proposal on the new project. But I’m not that afraid anymore. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become super confident, but rather that I know what I want. If the proposal gets rejected, I’ll feel bad, but I won’t doubt my own value.

Alright, I can sleep now.

# The First Conflict After the Move - A Record

**Time**: Last night | **Location**: New living room

---

## What Happened

It was about the placement of the living room sofa. A small thing, but I overreacted.

My partner suggested moving the sofa closer to the window for better light. My immediate reaction was: "You’re always trying to change my mind." Then I started rattling off a list of reasons why the current spot was better—even though I knew those reasons were weak.

She didn’t argue back; she just looked at me quietly. That silence made me even angrier because I could feel her pulling away.

---

## The Turning Point

She asked one question: "Are you angry at me, or is it something else?"

I wanted to say, "It’s just about the sofa," but the words didn't come out. There was a pause for a few seconds.

Then I told the truth: "I’m scared. Since moving here, it feels like everything is changing. I’m afraid that if I agree with you, I’ll slowly lose control over my own life. Like... I’m back to that feeling of being controlled by expectations again."

---

## Her Reaction

She didn’t try to convince me, and she didn’t say, "You’re overthinking it."

She said: "I understand. But I’m not trying to control you. I just thought the light by the window was nice. If you don’t like it, we won’t move it. Or... should we try it together?"

That was it. No confrontation, no winners or losers.

---

## Aftermath

We moved the sofa together. The light really is better.

More importantly, I realized that when I was being defensive, I was actually projecting my fears about my parents onto her. Instead of hitting back at my bad attitude, she asked the real question.

I guess this is what Dr. Wang calls "honestly expressing vulnerability."

---

## Lessons Learned

- Defensive reaction = deep-seated fear. Need to identify it faster next time.
- Not every disagreement is an attempt at "control." Sometimes, it’s just two people having different ideas.
- A partner’s gentleness isn’t a sign of withdrawal; it might be them giving me space.
- I can say "I’m scared" instead of saying "You’re wrong."

---

**Note**: Discuss this with Dr. Wang during the next session. It feels like I actually put those techniques to use this time.

# New Perspectives on Anxiety

**Date**: 2023-06-25 | **Tags**: #MentalManagement #WorkReflections

---

## Core Idea
I’ve only recently truly understood that anxiety isn't an enemy to be eliminated, but something we must learn to coexist with.

I used to focus entirely on how to "stop being anxious," which ironically allowed the anxiety to control me. Now, I’ve shifted my perspective: when anxiety arrives, I ask myself—what is it trying to tell me?

## The Difference
- **Eliminating Anxiety** = Constantly avoiding, suppressing, or waiting for it to disappear on its own (which it never does).
- **Coexisting with Anxiety** = Acknowledging its presence, listening to its signals, and then deciding how to act.

For example, while presenting the user segmentation strategy to the team last week, I could feel the anxiety—but this time, I didn't let it intimidate me. Instead, I used that "nervous energy" to stay focused. Anxiety said, "You might mess this up"; I replied, "That’s exactly why I need to be even clearer."

## Practical Application
- **At Work**: Anxiety = A reminder of which areas require more attention to detail.
- **In Life**: Anxiety = A signal that I genuinely care about the matter at hand.
- **The Key**: **Don't fight against the anxiety, but don't let it drag you down either.**

This shift feels significant. It’s not that the anxiety has vanished, but rather that I am no longer held hostage by it.

---

*It reminds me of what Dr. Wang said: "Anxiety itself isn't the problem; your reaction to it is." I think I’m finally starting to get it.*

Honey, I’m still at the office, but I really wanted to share what just happened with you right away.

During the team meeting this afternoon, I made a decision—I laid out the entire process behind the user segmentation strategy, not just the final results. I talked about how messy my initial thoughts were, how many times I changed direction, and even all the failed attempts. To be honest, I was still a bit nervous while speaking because I’ve always been used to only showing the "perfect" side of things.

But the most amazing thing happened. Li Ming came up to me afterward and said, "This made me feel like learning is a normal, acceptable process."

When I heard that, my eyes almost welled up. It wasn't because I was being praised, but because I suddenly realized—I’m not just doing my job well; I’m influencing others. My vulnerability and my trial-and-error process actually gave someone else courage. That’s a feeling I’ve never experienced before.

You know? I’ve always been chasing promotions, higher pay, and recognition. But today I realized that those things might not be what matters most. What matters most is that I can see myself growing, and I can help others see that they can grow too. This feeling is real and grounding; it’s completely different from that hollow sense of anxiety.

My mood shifted so quickly from being nervous to the moment I heard that feedback. It wasn't just relief, but a deep sense of fulfillment. It’s like I finally found something that has been inside me all along, but I had never really looked at it.

My head is a bit of a mess right now, yet everything feels so clear. I want to keep thinking about this, but more than that, I want to talk to you. Are you off work yet?

September 12, 2024, 10:30 PM

My fingers are still shaking.

I just sent a message to my parents. Just one. It wasn't long, but I typed it out three times, deleting and restarting every time.

The first draft was too blunt, like I was making some kind of grand declaration. The second was too soft, like I was trying to please them. The third... for the third one, I just sat on my bed, staring at the screen for a long time. Finally, I hit send.

The message just asked if we could find a time to have a serious talk about "my thoughts on success." That’s it.

My mind is a bit of a mess right now. I feel relieved, but also nervous.

For the past two and a half years, it feels like I’ve just been waiting—waiting for them to ask, and then either answering passively or avoiding the question entirely. Every time they called to ask, "When are you getting promoted?" or "When are you buying a house?", I’d either give a perfunctory answer or get irritated. But I never took the initiative to tell them how I actually felt.

Dr. Wang said last time, "You can choose to express yourself proactively, rather than always reacting defensively." It sounded right at the time, but actually doing it... it’s still scary. I’m afraid they won’t understand, afraid they’ll be disappointed, afraid of falling back into that feeling of being judged.

But I’ve really figured it out now. I no longer believe that success has to be about promotions, buying a house, or making a fortune. What I want is to live according to my own ideas, to have genuine connections with the people who matter, and to find meaning in my work. This isn't an escape; this is what I truly want.

At the office, I’m now able to work with the team more openly—not because I’ve become perfect, but because I’ve accepted my imperfections. In recent projects, I’ve stopped obsessing over "Am I doing enough?" and started focusing on "How can we make this more meaningful together?" It feels completely different.

My partner asked how I was doing, and I told her I was trying to have a different kind of conversation with my parents. She said, "That’s brave." I didn’t say anything then, but I thought to myself: it’s not about being brave, it’s about... finally being able to be myself.

I don’t know how they’ll respond. Maybe they’ll still have their expectations; maybe they still won’t quite understand. But at least this time, I’m not hiding, and I’m not passively waiting to be judged. I am proactively saying what I want.

This feels different.

It feels kind of good.

# 2023.08.11, past 11 PM

Insomnia again. Tossing and turning in bed, my mind is looping through everything I discussed with Dr. Wang today.

My partner said she wanted to meet my parents, and I just blurted out "Sure" without thinking. But from that moment on, my brain hasn't stopped. What if... what if she’s disappointed in me after meeting them? What if they don’t like her? What if this meeting goes south and ruins the great relationship we have now?

During our session today, Dr. Wang asked me, "Lin Hao, what specifically are you most afraid of?"

I gave her a bunch of "what-ifs." She didn't buy it. Instead, she pushed further: "But what are you *really* afraid of?"

I was silent for a long time before I could finally say it—I’m afraid that once she sees my family, once she sees the way my parents... the way they expect and judge things, she’ll be disappointed in me. I’m afraid she’ll realize I’m not actually as... "whole" as I’ve portrayed myself to be. I’m afraid this meeting will expose the real me, and then she won’t love me anymore.

My eyes stung a bit as I said it.

Dr. Wang asked, "When you’re with her now, are you being your real self?"

I said, "Yes."

"Then, is the person she loves right now the real you?"

"...Yes."

"So, if she already loves the real you, why would her love change just because she meets your parents?"

I didn't have an answer.

But my mind is still racing. I know Dr. Wang is right, but I just can’t control these "what-ifs." These hypotheticals are multiplying in my head like a virus. I’m even starting to think about making up an excuse to postpone the meeting... but I know that’s just my old habit kicking in—avoidance.

I’ve already been honest with my mother. I’ve already gone through that cycle of fear and release. Why is it still so hard this time?

Maybe it’s because this isn't just about my relationship with my parents anymore; it involves my partner. That makes everything feel more complicated, higher stakes. If it were just me, I could handle the failure. But if I drag her down with me...

My phone screen lit up. A message from her asking if I’m still awake. I didn’t reply. I don’t want her to see me like this—anxious again, catastrophizing again.

She deserves someone more stable.

But maybe... this is just who I am. Imperfect, prone to anxiety, sometimes getting stuck inside my own head.

I need to sleep. Or... I need to talk to Dr. Wang about this again tomorrow.

# Pre-session Memo

**Date**: June 20, Evening
**Points to discuss before the next session**

---

## 1. Today’s Conversation with My Father

I spoke with my dad on the phone yesterday. This time was different—instead of getting irritated by his questions as I usually do, I took the initiative to explain my recent user segmentation strategy and my understanding of product thinking. I went into great detail; it wasn't just a dismissive "yeah, okay."

After listening, he said, "You have a clear direction now."

That sentence made me pause for a long time. It wasn't that I was happy to be praised, but rather... he saw me. He truly saw me. Before, when he asked questions, I could feel the weight of expectation and judgment. This time was different; it felt like he was listening to me rather than evaluating me.

**Question**: Why was I able to share proactively this time? In the past, I genuinely didn't want to talk; why do I want to tell him these things now? Is it because I actually have something to say, or because my perception of myself has changed?

---

## 2. The Feeling of Being Seen

I’ve been thinking about this—growing up, the way my parents looked at me was always "you’re not good enough." My mother pointed out flaws; my father was silent but expectant. I got used to self-negation under their gaze.

But this was different. When he said, "You have a clear direction," he wasn't saying how well I was doing, but rather... that I know who I am and where I’m going.

This feeling is unfamiliar. It’s also very necessary.

**Question**: How much does this "being seen" impact my self-concept? Is my current understanding of myself authentic, or is it just temporary confidence fueled by recent successes?

---

## 3. The Anxiety is Still There, but It’s Different

I haven't had any late-night anxiety attacks these past few days. My sleep has improved too. But the anxiety hasn't disappeared; it’s just... existing in a different way?

For example, when I think about my career direction, I still feel that tension of "what should I do to succeed?" But now, I can ask myself "what is meaningful to me" instead of being overwhelmed by the anxiety.

It feels like my relationship with anxiety has changed. Before, it was "I am anxious"; now, it’s "I have anxiety, but I can handle it."

**Question**: Is this a real change, or is it just because things have been going smoothly lately? If I fail next time, will this sense of stability collapse again?

---

## 4. Regarding the Next Steps

I’m wondering if I should continue down this path. Not because of a promotion or making more money, but because... I see my own growth here. I can help the team understand product thinking, I can see the results of my strategies, and I can learn from failures instead of spiraling into self-negation.

But there’s also a voice saying, "Are you putting pressure on yourself again? Are you chasing some standard again?"

I don’t know if this voice is a rational reminder or just the old anxiety patterns acting up.

- Progress in anxiety management and how to maintain this stability during future failures.

What is success?

I used to think it was a promotion, a high salary, or buying a house before thirty. Now I realize those might just be the surface. Real success is perhaps that moment when you suddenly see clearly what you’re doing and why you’re doing it, and you feel a sense of inner peace—not because you’re trying to prove something to anyone, but because you truly believe the path is worth it.

I’ve had some small breakthroughs at work recently, but the bigger gain is that I’ve started to trust my own judgment. I’m no longer so easily discouraged by a single comment, nor am I in a rush to seek external validation. It feels a bit like finally hearing my own inner voice.

Let’s keep growing together.

## Personal Reflections
