When I jumped into Operations in the tech sector, I was excited at first, but I also went through a lot of self-doubt. There was a time when I seriously considered a career change—maybe moving into Product or something else entirely.

Now I realize the problem was never the industry itself; it was that I hadn't found the answer to "Who do I want to become?"

After today’s conversation with Zhang Hao, I feel I finally have a piece of that answer: I want to be an Operations Lead who understands users, creates value, and helps the team grow. Not because the title is prestigious, but because this direction aligns with my values.

This feeling is completely different from the old "What *should* I be doing?" mindset.

---

**Status Log**
- Energy: 8/10 (Feeling a sense of relief after the meeting)
- Stress: 3/10 (Having a clear direction actually lowered my stress)
- Work Stability: Execution quality has been solid this week; no careless mistakes.

Starting the new project next week—really looking forward to it.

March 9, 2023 Afternoon

I just stepped out of Dr. Wang’s clinic. Walking down the street, my steps feel inexplicably lighter. It’s strange.

When I went in, my whole body was tense. Once I sat down, I started talking and didn't stop for about thirty minutes. The work anxiety, my parents' expectations, why I keep denying that anything is wrong with me, that episode the other night... I heard my own voice shifting between anger, despair, and confusion, like someone repeatedly asking, "What is actually wrong with me?"

Dr. Wang didn't interrupt. She just sat there and listened. That only made me pour everything out even more recklessly.

Then she asked a question. A simple one, but one I had never truly considered.

"If you stripped away your mother’s expectations, the benchmarks of your peers, and all those 'shoulds'—what kind of professional life do you actually want?"

I... just went silent.

My eyes started to sting. I didn't even realize I was crying until she handed me a tissue. I have never asked myself that question. Never.

I’ve been running constantly, constantly trying to prove something, constantly measuring myself against others. But I never stopped to ask—what do I want?

I keep replaying what she said afterward: "Your worth is not determined by your achievements, but by your essence as a human being."

It’s a simple sentence. But it felt like someone was breaking something I had believed in my whole life.

Leaving the clinic, I didn't feel immediate relief or closure. But there was this strange sensation—like something I’d been suppressing was finally seen. At least the problem isn't hiding in the shadows anymore. At least now I can start to actually face it.

I don’t know what this means yet, but... I feel a little different.

Maybe next time I should ask myself—without all those expectations, what do I want?

Not what I *should* want. What I *do* want.

I was just chatting with Li Ming, and something interesting happened.

He was struggling with a user operations problem—specifically, how to treat different types of users differently. I walked him through the tiered strategy we used before, explaining how to tailor operation plans based on user activity and spending power. As I was talking, he suddenly asked, "How did you come up with this approach?" So, I started breaking down my entire thought process for him—from problem definition and data analysis to the final execution plan.

After I finished, he said, "Oh my god, you’re explaining this so clearly. I’ve never heard you talk about things so systematically before."

I was a bit stunned for a moment. I suddenly realized that I wasn't just "explaining a plan" to him; I was actually helping him understand a way of thinking. It felt different.

You know, I used to deal with a lot of anxiety and self-doubt—it was really bad for a while. Back then, I kept wondering when I’d finally become one of those people who are always stable and self-assured. But now, I think I’ve understood something: I don’t need to become that person. What I need is to learn how to coexist with my anxiety and use my experiences to help others.

My conversation with Li Ming today made me see that. I’m not running away from anything, and I’m not trying to prove anything. I’m just sharing the path I’ve walked. And that, in itself, is meaningful.

It feels like so much has changed over the past few months. From that feeling in May of "Did I make the wrong choice?" to now being able to clearly say, "I am an operations person who understands product thinking"—this transformation seems deeper than I imagined.

Just wanted to share this with you.

# Career Decision Compass

**Date: 2024.07.15**

---

While chatting with my partner recently, I had a sudden realization. I felt the need to write it down so I can revisit it every time I face a career crossroads.

My most fulfilling moments have never been about promotions or raises.

Truly. Looking back at my work over the past two years, the moments that kept me thinking late into the night and made me eager to get to the office the next morning were always:

- Seeing an operational strategy actually help users and watching the data reflect that success.
- Seeing a team member complete a project they previously thought was impossible because of my feedback and support.
- Mapping out a new workflow with Li Ming and the team, where everyone had that "Aha! So it *can* be done this way" moment.

As for those promotions? Honestly, once the initial excitement faded, there was nothing left. Same with the raises. I’ll mention them when my parents call to ask, but they aren't what I truly care about.

So, I’ve decided—this is my career philosophy. From now on, whenever I make a choice—whether to take on a new project, switch jobs, or change myself for a certain position—I will ask myself this:

**"Will this decision bring me closer to 'creating user value and helping the team grow'?"**

If the answer is yes, it’s worth it. If it’s just for a title or a number, I’ll pass.

I’m not 22 anymore. Back then, I wanted everything, terrified of falling behind. Now, I only want what’s right.

---

*P.S. This doesn’t mean I don’t care about career progression. I still want to be a better Head of Operations; I still want a bigger stage. It’s just that my definitions of "better" and "bigger" have changed.*

I’m sitting in the office restroom, my phone screen glowing, but I have no idea how to start this.

My hands were shaking when I walked out of Zhang Hao’s office just now. They’re still shaking.

I need to tell you what happened, but I don’t know where to begin. Maybe I should just say it—he offered me the position of Head of Operations. A management role.

I know this is supposed to be a good thing. When he said it, I could feel my eyes light up; for a split second, I was genuinely happy. But then, it was like someone hit the pause button, and all the fear came rushing in.

I told him I needed a week to think about it. He looked a bit surprised, but he didn't push.

Now I’m sitting here, and my head is a mess. On one hand, this proves I’m valued at this company, right? It’s what I’ve always wanted. But on the other hand… I don’t know. I really don’t know if I can handle management. I’ve done okay in operations, but that was just me doing my own work. Now I’m being asked to lead a team, to be responsible for other people’s work, to… I don’t know.

What am I most afraid of? I’m afraid of falling back into that same trap. Do you remember last year when I was pushing myself so hard? I was burning out every single day just to prove how hard I was working. I’m scared I’ll get sucked in again—that I’ll break myself just to prove I can be a good manager.

And honestly, I’m still struggling with something else—this opportunity came so fast. I’ve only been with this company for a little over two years. My professional identity hasn't even stabilized yet, and now I’m supposed to add "manager" to it. I feel like I’m constantly switching between different roles without ever really figuring out what I actually want.

I need to hear your thoughts. Do you think I should take it? Do you think I’m ready?

I know this is a heavy question to put on you, but I really need your perspective right now. Not the kind of perspective that just encourages me to go for it, but… something real, something that helps me see things clearly.

I want to talk to Dr. Wang about this, too. But before that, I wanted to hear from you.

I’m still in the office restroom. Wait for me to get back?

March 4, 2023, Afternoon

I just hung up the phone, and my hands are still shaking a little.

Dr. Wang’s voice was gentler than I remembered. It’s been three years; I never thought I’d dial that number again. 2020 was different—back then, my mom forced me to go. I went two or three times and then stopped, feeling like it was useless. But this time... this time, I was the one who dug out that business card and dialed the number from the office restroom.

Why did I suddenly decide to do it? I can’t really say. Maybe it was because I couldn’t sleep again in the early hours of yesterday morning, lying in bed scrolling through my phone for two hours, looking at other people’s promotions, other people’s houses, other people’s lives, and then looking at myself—a 26-year-old still obsessively imagining failure scenarios for a single project. Or maybe it’s because the conversations between her and me have been getting fewer and fewer this week; I can feel her looking at me, but I don’t know how to talk to her. Or maybe... I’m just tired. Truly tired.

I said something really stupid to Dr. Wang: "I want to actually fix this, this time." I laughed at myself after saying it because I don't even know what that means. Fix what? I can’t even clearly articulate what the problem is. So I added that I had written a list.

I wrote that list last night. I listed everything that keeps looping in my head: why I always feel like I’m not good enough, why a single piece of criticism can make me miserable for three days, why I can laugh and joke with colleagues during the day but want to hide away alone at night, why I’m so dependent on her, why my sleep is so poor, why I’m always running away... It felt a bit manic while I was writing it, but I felt a little clearer afterward. At least by taking these things out of my head and putting them on paper, they seemed less terrifying.

Dr. Wang said we could meet next Monday. I booked the appointment.

The moment I put the phone down, I had a strange feeling. It wasn't relief, and it wasn't hope—I’m still very scared. But there was a sense of... release? Like I’ve been holding my breath this whole time and I can finally let it out. I don’t have to pretend everything is getting better anymore, I don’t have to tell her "I’m fine," and I don’t have to give myself pep talks in the mirror. At least in the consulting room, I don’t have to keep up this act.

But sitting here in the office now, looking at the project documents on my computer screen, that sense of release is starting to fade again. I’m wondering, what am I going to say next Monday? Can I really change? Or is this just another form of escape, and I’ll give up just like I did three years ago?

I don’t know. I don’t know yet.

But at least... I did something. Something I should have done a long time ago.

Had a sudden realization at the office today and decided to re-examine our user feedback data through a layered lens. After seeing the proposal, Zhang Hao said, "This time, you didn't just think of it—you actually understood it."

It sounds like a compliment, but what really stuck with me was that word: "understood." In the past, I could write polished proposals that were even convincing enough to fool people. But today felt different—I could feel myself truly thinking through the problem instead of just applying concepts.

Maybe this is what growth feels like. It’s not about how many buzzwords you’ve mastered, but about starting to see things for what they really are.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s a different kind of exhaustion. It’s not the feeling of being crushed; it’s the feeling of being put to good use.

# Project Progress Log | 2024.09.03

**Project**: Autumn User Growth Operations Plan v2.0
**Participants**: Zhang Hao, Operations Team (3 people)
**Time**: Yesterday, 2:00 PM – 3:30 PM

---

## Key Discussion Points

During the weekly meeting, Zhang Hao raised a concern: the original execution cycle was too long (6 weeks) and the resource investment was too heavy. He wanted to see it completed within 4 weeks while keeping costs under control.

My gut reaction was actually a bit defensive—we had polished this plan repeatedly, and every step was logically sound. Cutting the timeline felt like cutting quality. However, instead of a flat "no," I proposed we conduct a **cost-benefit analysis** together.

Specifically, we:
- Listed every stage of the original plan and its corresponding resource investment.
- Tagged which parts were "core conversion drivers" and which were "nice-to-haves."
- Estimated the ROI for each stage.
- Identified areas for optimization or adjustment based on these findings.

---

## Final Plan (Compromise Version)

After the analysis, we decided to:
- **Keep**: User segmented operations + core conversion path optimization (these had the highest ROI).
- **Simplify**: Changed the content asset library from "daily updates" to "weekly updates," without lowering quality standards.
- **Postpone**: The deep-dive community operations were pushed to October (originally planned for September).

New execution cycle: **4.5 weeks**, with resource investment reduced by approximately 25%.

---

## Follow-up Actions

1. The team will re-review the details of the simplified plan this Wednesday.
2. I will handle coordination with the design team regarding the adjusted asset schedule.
3. Set aside buffer time to handle any contingencies.

---

## Reflections

To be honest, what surprised me about yesterday’s conversation was how "cool-headedly" I handled the conflict. In the past, I would have either pushed back immediately (appearing uncooperative) or agreed to everything (and regretted it later).

This time was different. I didn't give up my insistence on "quality," nor did I ignore Zhang Hao’s practical constraints. Instead, we found a "visible, logical" solution in the middle.

I feel like this aligns with what I’ve been thinking about lately—it’s not about choosing between "idealism" and "reality," but finding a way that makes sense for both sides.

The team's feedback was also positive. They didn't seem to resist the simplified version; in fact, they felt it was "well-reasoned." This makes me feel like I’ve genuinely made some progress in "how to make decisions."

Let’s see how the results look next week.

# Work Reflection Log | 2023.05.22

11:45 PM. Just got back from the gym. I’m a bit exhausted, but my mind feels surprisingly sharp. I decided to capture some thoughts while in this state—things I want to bring up with Dr. Wang during our next session.

---

## What Happened Today

This afternoon, while working on the user segmentation plan, I fell back into that state of "analysis paralysis." Once I had the data, I started obsessively verifying and tweaking the dimensions, terrified of missing some crucial factor. It wasn't until Zhang Hao asked for a progress update that I realized I’d spent over two hours on something that should have been wrapped up in two.

In that moment, the familiar self-doubt crept back in. I thought: **Am I just not cut out for product management? I can’t even handle a basic segmentation without making it this complicated.**

But this time was different.

---

## What Changed This Time

In the past, a situation like this would have sent me spiraling into self-negation for the rest of the afternoon, thinking things like, "Maybe I should just change careers." Today, I made a different choice—I stopped and asked myself: **"What does this mistake actually tell me?"**

I realized that my "caution" during the analysis actually stems from a fear of making mistakes. I’m afraid of submitting a plan that isn't perfect and then being judged for it. But in reality, when Zhang Hao saw how long I was taking, his reaction wasn't to criticize my work; he just asked, "Are you running into any roadblocks?"

He even said, **"Sometimes, an 80-point solution delivered on time is more valuable than a 100-point solution that arrives late."**

That really hit home.

---

## Notes in My Journal

Today, I wrote down two different "definitions of success" in my notebook:

**The Old Definition:**
- Promotions
- High salary
- Flawless execution
- Never making mistakes

**The New Definition (The one I’m trying out):**
- Being able to learn from errors
- Accepting my own fluctuations
- Continuing to act despite uncertainty
- Knowing when "good enough" is actually good

I almost laughed as I wrote this, because I realized the difference between these two is like... I’m using a completely new yardstick to measure myself.

The old yardstick says: **You aren't perfect, therefore you failed.**

The new yardstick says: **You are learning, therefore you succeeded.**

---

## Things I’m Still Unsure About

To be honest, this new definition isn't my "instinctive reaction" yet. When my mom asks me next time if I’ve been promoted, I’ll still feel that pressure. When I see people on social media getting promoted or buying houses, I’ll still have that split second of comparison.

This isn't a "one-time transformation." It’s something I need to remind myself of over and over again.

Sometimes I wonder: Am I just making excuses for myself? Is this "learning process" just a story I’ve made up to escape high expectations?

But while I was running 3km at the gym tonight, thinking about these questions, I realized that this "uncertainty" isn't actually that scary. Uncertainty itself is evidence of learning.

---

## Next Steps

Finish the user segmentation plan tomorrow. I’ve set a hard deadline for myself—submit the first draft by noon. Even if it’s not perfect, I’m sending it.

Then, continue with Module 4 of the Product Thinking course. I used to try to understand everything perfectly in one go; now, I’m trying to accept the process of "learning while doing."

Also, I want to ask Dr. Wang a question in our next session: **"If I can never fully get rid of the fear of failure, how do I live with it?"**

Because I’m starting to understand that maybe the goal isn't "how to eliminate fear," but "how to keep moving forward while the fear is still there."

---

Goodnight. Tomorrow is another day.

Stop asking if I’m good enough, and start asking how I can be better.

As I wrote this in my journal, I suddenly realized that something really is changing. It’s not an overnight transformation, but more like slowly adjusting a lens—the things that used to be a blur are finally coming into focus.

My sleep is still a bit hit-or-miss, but at least my mind isn't racing the moment my head hits the pillow anymore. Walking down the street tonight after finishing my workout, those "did I do something wrong?" thoughts didn't crowd my head. I was just... walking, breathing, a bit tired but clear-headed.

I guess this is just the beginning.

August 7, 2024, 9:00 PM

Lying in bed, she’s already asleep. I’m not tired; I just want to replay today’s conversation in my head one more time.

At the cafe this afternoon, I told her about the thoughts I’ve had while organizing my diaries lately—those ideas about success and about myself. I was a bit nervous, not knowing how to put it into words, afraid she’d think I was just talking about abstract nonsense. But after she finished listening, she just looked at me and said, "That’s the person I’ve always seen."

I was stunned for a moment.

I don’t know why, but that sentence made my eyes sting. Not the sting of sadness, but... the feeling of being seen. I’ve been doubting whether these changes in me are real, or if they’re just my own delusions. But she said that’s who she’s always seen, which means it’s not something I just made up. It’s real.

Then we started talking about what kind of life we actually want. She said she doesn’t want to do design work she hates just for a high salary, and I said I don’t want to compromise my own ideas just for a promotion. Neither of us cares if the apartment is downtown or if our annual income hits a certain number. What we want is to be able to do interesting things together, to support each other, and to live without being consumed by anxiety every single day.

As the words came out, I realized—we’re actually on the same page. It sounds simple, but it was a bit of a shock to me. Because I’ve always been thinking about "I need to succeed," rather than "What kind of life do we want?"

Lying here now with her asleep beside me, I’m thinking—maybe this is what I’ve been looking for over the last two years. Not an answer, but a person who can see all of me, including my contradictions, my vulnerabilities, and my uncertainties, and still say, "That’s the person I see."

I’m a little tired, but it’s a satisfying kind of tired.

Work continues tomorrow, and I still have to deal with my parents' expectations and so many other things that need adjusting. But today’s conversation makes me feel like—at the very least—I’m not fumbling through all of this alone.

The life we want together. The idea is a bit new, but it feels right.

I just got off the phone with my dad, and I really wanted to share something with you.

He asked how work was going, and instead of brushing him off like I usually do, I actually opened up. I told him about the user segmentation project—how I came up with the idea, and how I went about validating and optimizing it step by step. I even shared what I’m thinking about for the next phase and how I want to dive deeper into the product logic.

And you know what? He listened for a long time, and then he just said one thing: "You have a clear sense of direction now."

Just that one sentence. But as I listened, my eyes started to sting.

I realized I don’t need him to say things like "Great job" or "I’m proud of you" anymore. In that moment, I just needed to be seen—to be truly seen for who I am right now, what I’m doing, and what I want. Not to be judged on whether I’m "successful enough" or when I’m getting promoted.

Is it strange? It feels like our relationship shifted quietly at some point. I’m no longer running away from his expectations; I’m actively sharing my thoughts with him. And he’s no longer just pushing me; he’s actually listening.

I’ve been wanting to ask you—do you think this means I’ve really changed? Not just on the surface, but a real change from within?

Because I feel so different now. I’m not as anxious anymore. Even though the pressure is still there, I feel like I finally know where I’m going.

Are you there?

# August 2nd, 9:30 PM

When she asked me, I didn’t answer right away. I turned toward the window, looking at the night view below, my eyes stinging a little.

"How are we supposed to move forward?"

I’ve actually been avoiding this question. It’s not that I didn’t want to answer; it’s just that... answering it meant admitting something. Admitting that I can no longer brush things off with phrases like "I'm not sure yet" or "Let’s just see."

There was a long silence. She didn’t rush me; she just sat there. That made it even harder.

Finally, I said, "I want to keep going with you."

My voice was quiet, but as the words came out, I felt something. It wasn’t a sudden burst of certainty, but more like... a sense of relief? Like I finally gave myself permission to want something.

Then she asked me what I was afraid of.

I said, "I’m afraid you’ll think I’m not good enough. Or... I’m afraid you’ll have too many expectations, and then I’ll let you down."

The moment the words left my mouth, I knew—this wasn't about her. This was about my mother.

I’ve been using my fear of her to explain away my hesitation in this relationship. It’s as if committing to her means having to face my mother’s expectations and judgments. As if the moment I say "I have a girlfriend," everything will spiral out of control.

She said, "Maybe you should try trusting her."

I didn’t react immediately. But that sentence kept circling in my head, over and over.

...Maybe my fear of my mother doesn’t come from her at all. Maybe it’s something I’ve woven myself. I’m imagining how she’ll judge me, pre-empting what her expectations will be, and then, to escape these scenarios I’ve created, I refuse to face reality.

It’s so ironic. I’m hiding from something that might not even exist.

Dr. Wang said before that I tend to confuse "others' opinions" with "self-worth." I think I finally understand what that means now.

I’ve decided. At the end of this month, I’m going to call my mother. Not to ask for her permission, not to hear her judgment, but just to... tell her the facts. I have a girlfriend. I’m taking this relationship seriously.

Maybe she’ll have expectations. Maybe she’ll ask a ton of questions. But that’s her reaction; it’s not my responsibility to predict or control it.

I can feel what this decision brings—it’s not total peace, but a sense of... taking charge of the situation.

Instead of being trapped in my own imagination.

July 2, 2023  10:15 PM

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying here for half an hour, but my mind is still racing. Might as well get up and write something.

She asked me yesterday, "Have you thought about our future?"

It’s a simple enough question, but my first instinct was to brush it off. To say something about being exhausted from work or being under a lot of pressure lately. I could feel myself going through the same old motions—using fatigue as a shield. But it didn't work this time. She looked at me and said, "I’m not just anyone. I just want to know what you’re really thinking."

In that moment, I felt a sudden pang of discomfort. Not the kind you feel when you’re being blamed, but more like... being seen through? No, more like being truly seen.

I sat there for a while, and then I just said it—I’ve never been this blunt before: "I want to build a life with you. But at the same time, I keep asking myself: is this what I really want, or am I just repeating old patterns? How do I know I’m not just making this decision to meet someone else’s expectations?"

I was a bit scared as the words came out. Scared she’d think I wasn't sure enough, scared that being this honest would disappoint her. But she didn't. She just said, "Then let’s figure it out together. Not because I’m rushing you, and not because you’re doing it for me. Just... together."

I’m still replaying those words in my head.

It’s strange. My relationship with my parents, with my boss, even with myself—it feels like at some point, they all became defined by "whose expectations I need to meet." I’ve become so used to that pattern. But what she said wasn't like that. She said "together."

It made me realize something—I have no idea where my own desires end and other people’s expectations begin. Or rather... I’ve never actually asked myself "What do I want?" I’ve always asked "What should I want?"

Dr. Wang said in last week’s session that anxiety itself isn't the problem; the problem is my reaction to it—either avoiding it or trying to over-control it. But this time, in front of her, I feel like I did a third thing. I just... stayed in the anxiety. Acknowledged it. Spoke it out loud.

The anxiety is still there, but it feels different now. It’s not that suffocating urge to run away, but more of a... lucid restlessness? I can feel it, but I’m not drowning in it.

I should call my mother tomorrow. Tell her I have a girlfriend. I know it’ll trigger a whole chain of questions and expectations. But now I’m thinking that maybe I can face it differently. Not by hiding, not by being reactive, but by... proactively stating my truth.

Even if I haven't fully figured it out yet.

At least this time, I’m trying to be real.

# Project Summary | B-Side Operations Platform Revamp Completed

**Project Duration**: Mid-April – Mid-June 2024 (8 weeks)
**Project Scale**: 3-person team + cross-departmental collaboration
**Status**: ✓ Launched on schedule

---

## Core Metrics

- User Retention: 28% → 34%
- Platform DAU: Increased from 21k to 32k
- Feature Adoption Rate: 72% (Target: 60%)
- Team Efficiency: ~40% improvement compared to the previous project (based on task completion cycles)

The numbers look decent. But to be honest, the numbers aren't what I care about most.

---

## Team Performance

**Wang Tao (Data Analysis)**: I was actually a bit worried about him at the start. In the early stages, his analysis reports were always slow, and I considered taking over several times. Later, I realized the issue wasn't his ability, but my failure to communicate priorities clearly. Once I adjusted my communication style, his efficiency spiked, and the quality of his data insights far exceeded my expectations. His final user behavior analysis directly shaped our decisions on feature optimization.

**Li Xin (Operations Execution)**: Her performance caught me by surprise. There was a phase in the middle where user feedback was overwhelming, and we were momentarily in chaos. She took the initiative to organize all feedback, categorize it by priority, and even drafted the initial summaries for user interviews. This was supposed to be my job, but she handled it with more detail than I imagined.

**Zhang Ming (Content Operations)**: He single-handedly built up the content reserve in the two weeks leading up to the launch. Honestly, I probably didn't give him feedback quickly enough during that period, but he never complained. Instead, he proactively sought me out to discuss the content direction. The final content library was remarkably consistent in quality.

---

## Management Reflections

This project taught me a few things.

For the first two months, I was still stuck in my old ways—micromanaging everything and insisting on the final say on every detail. The result was a team constantly waiting for my feedback and me being exhausted. By mid-May, I realized this wasn't sustainable. I started experimenting with a different approach:

1.  **Define goals and constraints clearly, then let go.** It’s not about being hands-off; it’s about pushing decision-making power down to them.
2.  **Regular 1-on-1 feedback instead of centralized criticism in project meetings.** This makes feedback more specific and less like a public trial.
3.  **Proactively acknowledge their work.** It sounds simple, but I realized I rarely did this before.

During the project wrap-up, HR gave me some feedback: "Your team says you are a fair and supportive manager." To be honest, I was a bit stunned. I know I still have many flaws, but that feedback made me realize that maybe I’m finding a different way to do this job.

---

## The "Honest Truth" Part

Midway through this project, I hit a real low point. Every day I was questioning whether I was truly fit for this role, and my sleep suffered. I even felt like giving up a few times.

But it was during this time that I started thinking seriously about one question: **What kind of manager do I want to be?** Not "what kind of manager gets promoted," but who do I actually want to be as a person?

The answer was surprisingly simple: I want to be someone who makes the team feel supported rather than judged. I want to be someone who knows their limits and won't drag the team down just to prove themselves.

This project was high-pressure, but it gave me a chance to test if this philosophy actually works. It seems it does.

---

## Takeaways for the Next Project

- Empowerment > Micromanagement.
- Clear goals + regular feedback > Massive post-hoc adjustments.
- Recognize specific work > Vague praise.
- A manager’s stability directly impacts the team’s stability (I felt this one deeply).

One more thing: **Setting boundaries is vital.** During this project, I learned to stop replying to work messages after 10 PM. It was hard at first, but I’ve found it actually makes me more efficient during the day.

---

**Logged on**: June 12, 2024, Evening
**Mindset**: Exhausted but grounded. Still a lot to learn, but at least I know I’m heading in the right direction.

Later, he asked me what was up lately, saying I seemed different. I just naturally said, "I'm learning to accept myself." I was actually a bit shocked the moment those words left my mouth—how did that even come out of me? But saying it didn't feel awkward or defensive at all. It just felt... real.

It reminded me of what my partner said before: "Accepting your imperfections is actually the best design." Back then, I thought it sounded healing but a bit hollow. But now... I think I’m finally starting to get it. It’s not that I don’t care about mistakes; it’s that I don’t need to negate my entire self because of one.

The strangest part is that when I stop the self-criticism, I can actually see the problem more clearly. Without all that emotional noise, I can focus on "what to do" instead of "why am I so bad."

It sounds like such a simple concept, but for me... this feels like the first time I’ve actually done it.

The old me would have spent days overanalyzing Li Ming’s words, asking myself why I always mess up. The current me is just thinking about how to do better next time.

I feel like I’m slowly turning into a different person. Not a perfect person, but... someone who can accept being imperfect.

The feeling is still a bit foreign. But I like it.

I sat on the balcony alone for a long time after dinner.

Your words—"we have a choice"—have been looping in my head. I realized I’ve never actually thought about it that way. I’ve always just assumed life is what it is: the house, the promotion, the marriage. Like a checklist where you just have to tick off every box. But when you asked me, "Do you actually want it?" I suddenly realized... I hadn't even considered that.

It sounds silly, but I feel a sense of release. Not a light, airy feeling, but more like... I realized I can have my own answers, instead of always just answering everyone else's questions.

I’ve decided. I’m going back to see Dr. Wang. Not because I have all the answers now, but because I need to figure out if those anxieties are my own genuine concerns, or just things I’ve been carrying for others. The fact that you’re willing to walk through these questions with me has already changed so much.

Thank you for that question tonight. Truly.

I feel a bit tired now, but it’s not that hopeless kind of tired. It’s... the kind of tired that comes with a bit of hope?

# 2024.09.03

Zhang Hao came to talk to me this morning about that operations proposal. My initial reaction was the same as always—the moment he said, "The plan is too complex, can we simplify it?" a thought flashed through my mind: *Here we go again. I messed up again.* It was that familiar, sinking feeling.

But this time, I paused. It was only for a few seconds, but it felt much longer.

I didn't immediately agree with him, nor did I defensively explain why the plan had to be designed that way. Instead, I asked him a question: "Do you think the complexity itself is the issue, or do you feel the ROI isn't worth it?"

Zhang Hao was taken aback for a moment, and then we walked through a cost-benefit analysis together. The conclusion was that the plan did need adjustments—not because "I did it wrong," but because "at this stage, that level of investment is simply unnecessary." Those are two completely different things.

Why was I always like that before? Hearing a rejection and automatically translating it into "I’m not good enough." Now I can tell the difference—someone else's suggestion is just a suggestion; it’s not a judgment of who I am as a person.

Sitting in the office, I remembered what Dr. Wang said last week: "You’re becoming capable of staying rational under pressure." I didn't quite believe it at the time. But today, I really felt it. It’s not that I didn't have that moment of self-doubt—I did, and it was very clear—but I chose not to let it control me.

This feels... different.

When I mentioned this to my partner tonight, she said, "You know, the way you handle things has really changed." I asked her what she meant, and she said that before, I would either completely cave to others' opinions or defensively dig in my heels. Now, I’m actually thinking and engaging in a dialogue.

Listening to her, I suddenly realized—I’m no longer running away from anything. I’m not trying to prove anything, either. I’m just doing my job, using the abilities I have right now.

It feels like something has truly shifted over these past two years. Not just on the surface, but something deeper.

I’ll keep adjusting the proposal tomorrow. No nerves.

It made me think of what you said before about "the life we want." When you mentioned it, I remember thinking, *Exactly.* We don’t want a life of just mindlessly accumulating things. We want to do what we love, be with the people we care about, and have enough space to think and create.

That conversation with my mom made this realization even clearer. I realized I’ve been measuring myself by my parents' standards all this time, but I actually have my own now. And those standards align with yours.

It feels like we’re moving in the same direction, and that’s a really good feeling.

November 24, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Li Ming said something today that I’ve been turning over in my mind ever since.

We were discussing an operations plan this afternoon, and he brought up an idea. I just naturally helped him flesh out his thinking—nothing major, just pointing out a few areas that could be optimized. But he suddenly stopped, looked at me, and said, "You seem different lately. You're more... genuinely willing to help people."

I was a bit stunned. My reflex was to brush it off with some polite cliché, but I swallowed the words. Because I knew he was right.

In the past, whenever I helped someone, my head was full of thoughts like, "Will this get the boss's attention?" or "Will this performance earn me extra points?" I could even feel myself performing—playing the role of the "helpful colleague." Looking back, it’s a little embarrassing.

But things really seem to have changed lately. When I was helping Li Ming, I didn't think for a second about what was in it for me. It was just... I simply felt that if my ideas could help him, that would be good. That was it.

Helping others is meaningful in itself; it doesn't need to be for some kind of return.

When did I start thinking this way? It seems to have started with being with my partner, and those casual chats with the neighbors. The way they look at me isn't about judging my achievements; they just... see me for who I am. That feeling made me realize that being seen is enough. I don't need to prove anything through my performance.

So now, when I help others, my motives have become pure.

The fact that Li Ming could see this change—what does that mean? It means it’s not just my own illusion. I really am changing.

I found it a bit hard to believe while sitting in the office. But now, alone at home, I feel a sense of peace instead. It’s like... I finally don't have to work so hard. I don't have to constantly think about how to present myself or how to make others think I'm capable.

I can just be me.

It’s a strange feeling, but it feels right.

September 29, 2022, 11:35 PM

It’s 11:30 PM again. I’ve noticed this pattern for a while now—I used to stay up until 1 or 2 in the morning, but lately, I naturally start feeling sleepy around 11. It’s not a forced kind of tiredness, but a comfortable sensation, like my body is gently telling me it’s time to rest.

I had a long talk with my partner today. I started spiraling again over that same old dilemma—whether to switch careers, tech vs. traditional industries, operations vs. management. To be honest, I’ve been overthinking this for nearly a year. Every time I get stuck in it, I end up feeling like I’ve made all the wrong choices.

But my partner said something today that suddenly made things click. She said, "Maybe your problem isn't the career itself, but your own self-perception."

I was stunned.

Later, I jotted that sentence down in my phone's notes and thought about it some more. It seems to apply to both operations and life in general. I’ve been constantly asking "What should I do?" without ever figuring out "Who am I?" Whenever my boss praises me, I’m on cloud nine; whenever I’m criticized, I fall into a pit of self-doubt. It’s as if my entire value is dictated by other people’s opinions.

If I look at it that way, what difference does switching industries make? I’d just repeat the same pattern in a new place.

I’m only just realizing this now.

The strangest thing is that this realization has made me relax a lot. Before, I was always trying to prove something, as if not getting a promotion, not buying a house, or not being "successful" meant I was a failure. Now... I don’t know how to put it, but it’s like those things suddenly aren't that important anymore? It’s not that I’ve given up; it’s just that the urgency and the fear have faded.

When my partner is with me, I don’t feel the need to perform. She sees my imperfections, my indecisiveness, my anxiety, and she just... accepts it. No judgment, no "you should be this way." It’s such a foreign feeling—so foreign that it actually felt a bit uncomfortable at first.

I think my improved sleep has something to do with this, too. I used to lie in bed with my head full of tomorrow’s work, housing prices, and my parents' expectations, scrolling through my phone until 2 or 3 AM. Now, after talking with her, I feel a sense of companionship, and sleep just comes naturally. My body is relaxed, and my mind is at peace.

I still have to go to work tomorrow. I’ll still make mistakes, and I’ll still get criticized. But it doesn’t seem so scary anymore. Maybe this is what I really needed—not a change of job, but a change in how I see myself.

I’m getting tired. Time to sleep.

# August 26, 2024, past 11 PM

Lying in bed. She’s already asleep, but I can’t seem to drift off.

Something she said earlier on the living room sofa is still playing on a loop in my head.

"I always felt like you were running away from something. But now, I see you facing something."

I didn't respond right away. I just looked at her, my eyes welling up a bit. Then I said something stupid: "It took me a long time to realize that."

She smiled—not mockingly, but with that look of pure understanding.

I think this might be the first time someone has truly seen me this way. Not looking at my achievements, not judging whether I’m doing things right or wrong, but actually seeing what I’m going through. Seeing how I’ve shifted from—how do I put this?—from that state of constant escape and self-denial into... something else.

I showed her those notes. The ones about a new definition of "success," about "the kind of person I want to be." I was actually quite nervous when I wrote them, afraid they’d come across as self-indulgent or naive. But she read them all carefully and said, "These are real. I see them in you."

Two and a half years. From moving out at the end of 2021 until now. That period of frantic anxiety in the middle, seeing Dr. Wang, the insomnia, the endless miles on the treadmill, the late nights scrolling through other people's achievements and feeling utter despair. She saw all of it.

But she didn't say, "You went through this." She said, "I see you facing it."

There’s a huge difference.

Running away versus facing it. I’d never thought about those two words that way. Before, I was just running—running from my parents' expectations, from the uncertainty of work, from my own self-doubt. Sometimes I even ran from her, hiding in my own world.

And now? I don’t know if I’ve stopped running entirely, but something is definitely changing. I’m starting to be able to look at those anxieties instead of being swallowed by them. I’m starting to be able to tell my parents, "I have my own ideas," instead of just rebelling or staying silent. I’m starting to be okay with being imperfect at work instead of spiraling into self-loathing.

It sounds like no big deal. But to me, it’s everything.

She said we should imagine together what "the life we want" looks like. Not the life we "should" have, not the life our parents expect, and not the "successful" life that looks good on social media. Just what *we* want.

I haven't figured it all out yet. But at least now I can actually think about the question, rather than being drowned by the anxiety of "what I should become."

She’s sleeping now. I’m staring at the ceiling, feeling a strange sense of peace. Not a fake, suppressed peace, but the real kind—the kind that comes after you finally let go of something.

Maybe I’ll be anxious again tomorrow. But at least for tonight, I know someone saw me changing.

And that’s enough.

My partner said something today that I’ve been turning over in my mind ever since.

She said, "Lately, it doesn't feel like you're trying to prove something anymore; it feels like you're actually enjoying yourself." I didn't respond right away—I just smiled. But honestly, those words hit home.

I’ve been wondering: when did this shift start? There wasn't a single defining moment, but something is definitely different. That constant tension—the feeling that I *had* to be the best just to prove my worth—seems to be slowly loosening its grip. It’s not that I’ve stopped caring about work, it’s just… I don’t know how to describe it. It feels less suffocating?

Before, I was always running. Running fast, running tired, running until I forgot why I was even running in the first place. All I knew was that I couldn't stop. If I stopped, I’d be judged; I’d be looked down upon. By my parents, by my boss, by myself. So I had to keep running, keep proving.

But lately, I’ve started asking myself—what is success, really? Is it a promotion? A six-figure salary? Buying a house before thirty?

My answer now is: not entirely.

Success feels more like a process than a destination. It’s about whether I can be fully immersed in what I’m doing without being swallowed whole by it. It’s about finding a balance between work and life, rather than using life just to fill the gaps left by work. It’s about staying authentic instead of distorting myself to fit some hollow "successful persona."

It sounds a bit abstract, but that’s truly how I feel right now.

Last week, my partner and I planned a trip for next month. It seems like a small thing, but it meant a lot to me. The old me wouldn't have had the mental energy to plan something like that. My head was too full of work, promotions, and the need to prove myself. Now, I actually have the space to think about things like, "Where do I want to go, and who do I want to be with?"

I still get anxious. I still wake up in the middle of the night obsessing over some work detail. But it feels less desperate now. Like Dr. Wang said, anxiety itself isn't the problem; the problem is how I handle it.

I’m starting to recognize my own patterns. It’s strange—it’s like I’m observing myself from the outside. "Oh, there goes Lin Hao overworking again." "Oh, there’s Lin Hao trying to fill an inner void with achievements again." Being able to see it means there's a chance to change it.

She hugged me. I could feel that she wasn't just comforting me for losing my temper, but rather, she understood *why* I reacted that way. That feeling of being truly seen... I don’t know how to describe it. The irritation just evaporated.

But I want to remember how this feels tonight—the feeling of being understood.

I’m wondering, is this a real change? Or is it just a surface-level adjustment? Because I can still feel that underlying anxiety—especially with work being so stressful lately. But the difference this time is that I can see it, rather than being overwhelmed by it.

This is what you meant by "identifying the pattern," right? I just wanted to check if I’m understanding it correctly.

Dr. Wang, I’ve been thinking a lot since our last session—for several hours now, actually, haha.

First of all, I really want to thank you for all your help over the past few months. To be honest, since I didn't stick with the treatment during my period of anxiety last year, I was a bit nervous when I reached out to you again. I wasn't sure if I could truly change. But after these three months, I can feel a distinct difference. It’s not just that my sleep has improved; more importantly, I’m now able to identify my recurring thought traps—like spiraling into self-doubt over a minor mistake or getting anxious when I see others' achievements. The techniques you taught me might sound simple, but they really do work.

I’d like to discuss the schedule for our future sessions. My thought right now is that I don’t need to come in every week like before, but I don’t want to stop entirely either. I was wondering if we could switch to once every two weeks? That way, I can maintain the progress I’ve made while giving myself some space to practice and consolidate things on my own. I feel like I’m currently in a phase of "maintenance and deepening" rather than "crisis management."

Also, I’ve had some new thoughts regarding work that I’d like to discuss with you—specifically about how to balance career development with quality of life. It’s not super urgent, so once every two weeks should be plenty.

Do you think this arrangement is feasible? If so, we can confirm the specific timing when we next meet.

Thank you.

July 28, 2023. Night.

I just hung up the phone with Mom. My hands are still shaking a little.

I don’t know how to describe this feeling. It’s just… I finally did it. I called her of my own accord and said, in the most casual tone, "Mom, I have something to tell you. I have a girlfriend now." No testing the waters, no waiting for her to ask first, and none of that people-pleasing tone. Just a statement of fact.

She was silent for about three seconds. Then she said, "Alright. Since you’ve already decided, just make sure you treat her well."

That was it. No interrogation, no blame, and none of the "Why didn't you tell me sooner?" accusations I had imagined. Instead, it was… acceptance?

I’m sitting on the sofa now, my mind a bit of a blank. This feeling is very unfamiliar.

In the past, every time I faced my mother, I would subconsciously enter a kind of "coping mode"—either defensive (explaining why I couldn't meet her expectations) or people-pleasing (pretending to agree with her views). This time was different. I didn't care how she would react; I just… spoke the truth. My choice.

After the call ended, I sat for a long time. It suddenly hit me—this wasn't a "request for approval." I wasn't asking her "Is it okay?" I was telling her "This is my life."

It sounds simple, but for me… it feels like something has been unlocked.

What Dr. Wang said last time about "redefining self-worth"—I think I understand it a little now. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become super confident or anything. It’s that I’ve started to believe that my choices are valid, and even if Mom doesn't agree, that’s okay. But this time, she did agree. More importantly, I’m no longer so afraid of her judgment.

I’m still a little scared, but it’s different. The fear has turned into… something I can coexist with?

I want to tell her (my partner) about this. But not yet. I want to let this feeling settle in my heart for a while.

It’s very peaceful. A very unfamiliar kind of peace.

I just got off the phone with my mom, and I really wanted to share something with you.

She said, "I can see how happy you are now, and I’m so glad for you."

You know, that’s the first time she’s ever said anything like that. Before, it was always "When are you getting promoted?" or "When are you buying a house?" But this time, she was actually seeing me, not judging me.

I was a bit stunned. I just said, "Thank you for understanding," and my eyes got a little misty as I said it.

Then it suddenly hit me—I wanted to invite them over to see the new place. Our home. The thought even surprised me, because when I first moved out, it was really about escaping that sense of pressure, right?

But it’s different now. I want them to see the place I’ve built with you. Not to prove anything, just... to share it with them.

Mom sounded really happy when she heard the invitation. She said, "Your dad and I would both love to go."

I feel like your support has allowed me to see my relationship with my family in a different light. It doesn't feel like a confrontation anymore; it feels like we can actually just talk. It’s a strange feeling, but a good one.

Are you there?

11:45 PM. Insomnia again.

But this time is different. It’s not the kind of sleeplessness where I lie in bed spiraling over whether I’m "really cut out for this job." It’s... excitement? No, more like a restlessness I can’t quite settle.

The person I met at the Murder Mystery game today—her name is... actually, I won't write it down yet. It feels like once I write it, it becomes too real, like I’m confirming that all of this actually happened.

She told me, "You look confident, but I can sense you’re doubting yourself."

My immediate reaction was... feeling completely seen through. In that split second, all my defenses were useless. I didn't even know how to respond; I just smiled. But she didn't push further, and she didn't point out my flaws the way my mom does. She just... looked at me, as if saying, "I know you're there."

I added her on WeChat afterward. She sent a few photos from today, and as I looked at them, I kept wanting to comment. I typed something out several times and deleted it every single time. The first try was "I had a great time today"—too blunt. The second was "Your eyes are so bright"—too cheesy. What was the third one... never mind. In the end, I just gave it a "like."

But here I am, 11:45 PM, still thinking about that one thing she said.

"Accepting your own imperfections is actually the best kind of design."

She was talking about her work, but all I could think about was my own job. When Zhang Hao told me last time that I lacked execution, I spent three days obsessing over it, feeling like I couldn't do anything right. But what if... what if I didn't see it as "my failure," but as "me learning"?

I saved that quote in my Notes app. It feels a bit silly now, but I really want to keep re-reading it.

What am I most afraid of? I’m only realizing it now. It’s not that she’ll reject me, but that... this feeling will fade. Just like every relationship I’ve had before—it starts intense, then I start doubting myself, convinced they’ll eventually see the "real me," and then I find reasons to pull back.

 I even wondered if I should ask her out. But what if she says she’s busy? Or worse, what if she only added me to be polite?

...I hate the way I’m acting right now. But at the same time, I don’t want to lose this feeling—the feeling of someone finally *seeing* me instead of judging me.

Maybe I won't feel this way tomorrow. Maybe this is just a momentary rush. But at least for tonight, I’m not anxious. I’m... looking forward to something.

It’s a strange feeling.

I don't know if it's good or bad.

# MEMO

**Date: June 14, 2022, 9:00 PM**

I fell again.

On the treadmill. My foot missed a step, and I went flying forward. My knees and palms are all scraped up; beads of blood are still oozing out. The gym staff asked if I was okay, and I told them I just slipped. But I didn't. I was thinking about a career change—thinking about whether I should study to become a Product Manager, whether 26 is too late to switch paths, whether if I can't even handle Operations, I'll definitely fail at Product... and then I lost my footing.

Sitting in the lounge area looking at my palms, blood mixing with sweat, I just thought—I can’t even run without falling. I really can’t do anything right.

Zhang Hao’s words are still stuck in my head: "A low-level mistake like this shouldn't come from an experienced Operations person." Experienced. I’ve been doing this for almost a year, but it feels like I have zero experience. Or maybe my experience is a sham. I’m just faking it. No matter how hard I try to fake it, I can’t conjure up real talent.

Lately, I’ve been going to the gym more often, from 3-4 times a week to 5-6. But I’ve realized I’m not even working out; I’m just ruminating on these problems over and over on the treadmill. My body is exhausted, but my mind keeps spinning. Yesterday I ran for an hour, and the whole time I was thinking, "Should I switch careers?" Today, I fell.

My mom asked me again recently if I’d been promoted. I said no. She paused, then said, "Look at your classmates..." I didn't listen to the rest. I know what comes next. I’ve always known.

Housing prices are so high. My savings aren't even close. Reaching the middle class by 30? I’m making 250k a year now, but after taxes and rent, there’s barely anything left at the end of the month. How am I supposed to buy a house? How am I supposed to find a girlfriend? How am I supposed to make them satisfied? How do I make myself satisfied?

My sleep is terrible. Last night, I didn't drift off until 2:00 AM again. My head was full of work, the career change, the house. When I woke up this morning, my eyes were so swollen they looked like puffy bags.

Maybe I’m really not cut out for this industry. Maybe I’m just the kind of person who will never amount to anything, no matter how hard I try. Maybe switching careers won't change a thing, because the problem is me.

My palms still hurt. My knees hurt, too.

I can’t do anything right.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share something with you.

I took the initiative to call my mother this afternoon and told her that I have a girlfriend now.

To be honest, I was a bit nervous before dialing. But when I actually said the words, I found my tone was very calm—I wasn't asking for approval; I was simply stating a fact. My mother’s reaction was unexpectedly simple. She just said, "Well, since you've already made up your mind, just make sure you treat her well."

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time. I felt a sense of peace that I’ve never experienced before.

I think this might have something to do with the work we’ve been doing. In the past, I was always avoiding things or reacting passively. But this time, I was proactive. I didn't wait to be asked, "When are you going to find a girlfriend?" I went and said it myself. It feels completely different.

I’m not sure if this represents a deep-seated change or if it’s just a one-off, but I’d like to discuss this with you during our next session.

Also, my sleep quality has been much better lately.

# Long-term Financial Planning - Discussion Needed with Partner

**Date**: Nov 3, 2023, Evening

---

## Core Issue
My mom’s questions made me realize something—I’ve been passively responding to expectations instead of actively planning my own life. It suddenly clicked today: instead of drowning in anxiety, I need to turn these "vague fears" into concrete numbers and plans.

## Dimensions to Clarify

**1. Housing**
- It’s not about "having to buy a house by 30," but "if we were to buy, when would it be reasonable?"
- How much is the down payment? Can we get there with current savings + what we accumulate in the next 2-3 years?
- Renting vs. Buying: How does each affect our quality of life?
- Is this decision something "I actually want" or just something "I feel I should do"?

**2. Salary & Career Direction**
- Currently at 250k. To improve our financial situation, should I focus on a raise or a career pivot?
- Tech vs. traditional industries? Management vs. specialist track?
- Zhang Hao’s performance has been unstable; he might need to adjust his mindset or consider other opportunities.
- But I can't let anxiety drive me into a blind job hop.

**3. Investments & Wealth Management**
- How should we allocate our current savings? Funds, fixed deposits, real estate?
- Need to find reliable financial advice instead of scaring myself by scrolling through Xiaohongshu.

**4. Communication with Parents**
- Stop responding passively. Instead, proactively tell them, "This is my plan."
- Even if the plan isn't fully formed, I need to show that "I am thinking about this seriously."
- This might reduce their anxiety—and my guilt.

---

## Action Items
- [ ] Sit down with my partner this week and go through these questions one by one.
- [ ] Check what our current savings can actually do; stop the aimless overthinking.
- [ ] Consider whether I need to see a therapist again (do I still have Dr. Wang’s contact info?).
- [ ] Fix my sleep schedule. My sleep is terrible lately, and it’s affecting my work and clarity.

---

## Reflections
I’m a bit exhausted, but compared to that "stuck" feeling I had before, at least now there’s a sense of direction. I don’t know if this plan will work out, but it’s better than checking real estate apps every day and spiraling into self-doubt.

I’m sitting on the edge of my bed, and it’s not even light out yet. The glow from my phone is hitting my face; I’ve been scrolling through messages for half an hour, but nothing is actually sinking in.

I need to talk to you about something.

I keep replaying what Zhang Hao said to me during the team meeting yesterday. "A low-level mistake like this shouldn't come from an experienced operator." I know it’s true, and I know I did mess up, but I just can’t accept it. Or rather, I can’t accept how he defined me—as if I’m not even qualified to do this job.

I didn't sleep well last night. I lay in bed all night thinking about whether I should switch to being a Product Manager. I even spent half an hour on the subway searching for career pivot cases, reading a bunch of "success stories from Ops to PM," but in the end, I did nothing. Just open, read, close, repeat. I’m terrified of switching careers, but I’m also terrified of staying in Ops. I don’t even know what I want anymore.

The most painful part was seeing a post on Maimai discussing this exact topic. I paused for a long time, wanting to comment, but I never sent it. Because I know I’m craving change, but I don’t have the courage to actually make it happen. It’s a completely powerless feeling.

I read the email Zhang Hao sent three times this morning, and I still don’t understand what he actually wants. It’s like my brain is broken.

I know this sounds incredibly depressing, but I need you to tell me—I’m not a loser, right? I’m not the person my parents worry about, the one who can’t do anything right. I’m just... stuck right now.

When are you free? I want to see you.

# MEMO

**Date: May 8, 2023, 7:00 PM**

---

Just finished a meeting with Zhang Hao about this week’s data analysis report.

I went in expecting another lecture. My initial design was flawed—I’d fallen into the trap of over-analysis, trying to account for every single variable, which only ended up clouding the core issue. It was a familiar feeling: that wave of self-doubt, thinking, "Can I ever get anything right?"

But this time was different.

When Zhang Hao pointed out the issues, I didn’t immediately spiral into that "I’m useless" despair. Instead, it clicked: this wasn’t a failure; it was a classic case of analysis paralysis. A learning point.

I’m surprised I could even think that way.

We then walked through how to pivot the strategy. He asked me, "How do you think we can avoid this next time?" I suggested aiming for 80% of the analysis first rather than chasing 100% perfection. He agreed, saying that’s exactly what product thinking is—making decisions amidst imperfection.

I talked about it with my partner tonight. She said, "You’re changing."

I didn’t argue. Because I can actually feel it.

The anxiety is still there, but it’s no longer the "I’m bad at everything so I give up" kind. It feels more like, "I want to do better, so I’m thinking it through." The texture of the feeling has changed.

---

**Key Takeaways:**
- Analysis paralysis ≠ Failure
- Learning points
- 80% decision > 100% perfection
- I am changing (This is worth remembering)

Read this again the next time anxiety hits.

Something happened during today’s team meeting that I’m still reflecting on.

There was a logical flaw in the plan Xiao Wang submitted—the user conversion path was inconsistent. I pointed it out directly during the meeting, using the words: "A logical loophole like this shouldn't happen among professionals."

The moment I said it, the atmosphere in the room turned awkward. Xiao Wang kept his head down, and everyone else went silent.

Looking back now, my tone was definitely too harsh. Xiao Wang has only been with us for two months. The fact that he came up with the overall framework for this plan is actually quite good; that loophole wasn't a fundamental error, just an oversight in the details. By saying "a professional shouldn't," it sounded like I was dismissing his entire professional capability rather than pointing out a specific area for improvement.

This reminds me of something Dr. Wang discussed with me before—I hold myself to very high standards, so I tend to set the bar too high for others as well. When I see someone failing to meet those standards, I’m quick to dismiss them with labels like "unprofessional" instead of trying to understand their situation.

As a newcomer, Xiao Wang doesn't need to be nitpicked; he needs to be recognized for what he got right, and then we can work on improvements from there.

**Follow-up Plan:**
I’m going to have a 1-on-1 with Xiao Wang tomorrow. I plan to:
1. First, acknowledge the overall logic and creative aspects of his plan.
2. Explain specifically why that logical flaw would cause issues.
3. Discuss how to adjust it together.
4. Also, make it clear to him that the way I gave feedback today was inappropriate.

I am a manager now. My job isn't just to find problems; it's to help the team grow. I need to keep that in mind.

Just hung up the phone with Mom. I’m still trying to process it.

She asked how I’ve been, and then, as usual, brought up the promotion. I was going to brush it off like I always do, but this time, I didn't. I told her I’ve been rethinking what actually matters to me—that it’s not about the promotion itself, but about the kind of person I want to become.

I was actually pretty nervous after saying that, waiting for her to push back. But she didn't. She paused for a moment and then said, "That sounds very mature."

That was it. No follow-up questions about when I’d get promoted, no comments about me not working hard enough. Just, "That sounds very mature."

I can feel her starting to see me as an adult with my own thoughts, rather than a child who needs correcting. It’s a strange feeling. For over two years, it’s been this cycle of "expectation and defense" between us—me desperately trying to prove myself, and her constantly pointing out my shortcomings. But today, when I responded by saying things like, "What I care more about is whether I can help my team grow," it felt like something finally broke through.

I think back to June when I was flipping through my old journals, seeing how I’ve moved step-by-step from that total breakdown in 2022 to where I am now. Back then, I was so lost, feeling like I couldn't do anything right. But now, I can say it clearly: I don't need a promotion to prove my worth. My value lies in my ability to help others grow, in creating something meaningful, and in living life on my own terms.

Mom didn't fully understand what I meant, but she said she respects it. That’s more important than understanding.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s the "finally able to breathe" kind of tired. Not the suffocating kind.

Onward to tomorrow.

I’ve noticed something interesting lately—emotions are like the weather. I used to think they were random and uncontrollable, but they’re not. They have their own patterns and cycles. The more you understand them, the better you can live with them.

It’s not about being trapped by the ups and downs anymore, nor is it about pretending they don’t exist. It’s simply knowing why I feel down, knowing when things will get better, and knowing what to do.

It feels like at some point, I stopped that constant self-scrutiny of how I "should" be, and instead became clearer on what I actually want. It’s a small shift, but it has changed everything.

Goodnight.

November 18, 2023, past 10 PM

I just hung up the phone with Mom, and I’m still a bit dazed.

I originally just wanted to call her and chat about how things have been going. I was nervous because I wanted to tell her what’s been on my mind lately—about the house, about success, about all these things she’s been pushing me on. I even rehearsed it in my head several times, trying to figure out how to be less confrontational, less defensive.

Then I just said it, very directly: "Mom, I’ve realized that the definition of success might not be what you or Dad expect. I’m working on a long-term financial plan. A house might be part of it, but it’s not everything."

As the words left my mouth, I was actually quite tense. I expected to hear her start listing my shortcomings or use that "look at everyone else" tone. But... she didn't.

She said, "As long as you’re happy, the house will happen sooner or later."

I was stunned.

That sentence keeps looping in my head. When has my mom ever said something like that? I don’t think I’ve ever heard her say that. I always thought she was the type of person who felt like nothing I did was ever good enough, always pointing out my flaws. But now... I don’t know if she’s changed, or if I’ve just been misunderstanding her all along.

I thought back to what Dr. Wang said about how my understanding of my parents' expectations might be skewed. I felt a bit resistant at the time, but now... it seems like that’s really the case.

I’ve told myself "they don’t understand me" for so many years, but maybe I haven't truly tried to understand them either. Maybe they’ve been waiting for me to speak up, rather than waiting for them to change.

Lying in bed now, I feel a sense of release. It’s not total relaxation, but... I feel lighter. Like something heavy I’ve been carrying suddenly lost its weight.

I’m still a bit unsure about what to do next. But at least for now, I no longer feel like conversations with them have to be "me vs. them."

Maybe I can try to actually communicate with them, instead of always being on the defensive.

I’m tired, but it’s a good kind of tired.

Now, lying in bed while she’s already asleep, I’m thinking about it in the dark.

To be honest, I don’t know what I’m doing. Or rather, I know the surface-level reasons—ensuring the highest quality for the team’s work, showing them a responsible leader—but I’m not sure if those are the *real* reasons.

Lately, work has indeed felt more meaningful. Seeing the team members grow through the project is a unique feeling. But... am I using that as an excuse sometimes? Am I using "helping the team" as a way to escape something else?

Like, escaping the emptiness I feel after coming home? Escaping that sense of being at a loss that sometimes creeps in when I'm with her? Escaping the pressure from my parents—even though I’ve moved out, that weight of expectation still pops up out of nowhere, and work seems to be the only thing that proves "I’m making progress."

I don't know.

 I really don't know.

I was perfectly lucid while editing that document; I knew it wasn't necessary. But I just couldn't stop. It’s like... if I don’t make this absolutely perfect, something will go wrong, someone will be disappointed, and it’ll prove that I’m actually not that capable.

She didn't say anything else. She just went to bed. But that "There you go again" keeps echoing in my head.

Maybe I should talk to Dr. Wang about this tomorrow. Or have a real talk with her.

But right now, I’m just lying here—a bit tired, a bit annoyed, and a bit unsure of what exactly I’m chasing.

# September 18, 2023, 10:00 PM

Lying in bed, on the verge of another night of good sleep. I’m wondering: when did I stop being afraid of sleep?

Not long ago, I used to scroll through my phone until two or three in the morning, looking at other people’s achievements and spiraling into anxiety about myself. Now... my sleep is as steady as a clockwork professional. My energy is good, too; today felt truly productive. It sounds like some cliché self-help quote, but for me, this is a very concrete, tangible change.

I’ve been reflecting on something lately—my anxiety is still there, but it has changed. Before, I was trapped by it, like being held hostage by the weather with no rhyme or reason. Now, I can see it coming; I know why it’s here and I know how to handle it. Everything from this past month—meeting my parents, looking at apartments, therapy, my steady performance at work—seems to be pointing in the same direction: I am becoming more stable.

The day I met my parents, I was nervous the whole time. But then I realized that most of my fear of them was actually my own creation. They are much more open-minded than I imagined. My partner was there too, and seeing the three people who matter most to me all together, I suddenly had this feeling—my life is integrating. I’m not wavering, I’m not running away; I’m actually integrating.

Now that my partner and I are planning to move in together, I don’t feel that pressure of "how I should be" anymore. Instead, there’s a clarity of "what I want." This shift is huge for me.

Li Ming told me today that he’s been feeling a bit anxious lately and asked how I deal with it. I shared some things I’ve learned from therapy—how I identify and cope with anxiety. As I was talking, I realized that I’ve actually mastered these things. Not just in theory, but the kind of mastery where you can pass it on and actually help someone else.

My stress level is currently a 3/10. This number feels foreign to me because I’m so used to high stress. But I believe it’s real now. I’m no longer that "high energy but low stability" person. I’ve become relatively stable while still keeping my spark.

I’ll still have fluctuations and moments of uncertainty. But I know now that it’s normal. I have ways to cope.

And that’s enough.

# September 18, 2024 | Wednesday | Past 11 PM

Still can't sleep. I was scrolling through my phone again just now and saw photos of a college classmate’s new house. Then the old cycle started—comparison, anxiety, self-doubt. Eventually, I put the phone down and decided to write something.

Zhang Hao reached out to me today. He said the team’s feedback is that my management style has changed lately—they feel I’m being more "fair" and "supportive." I was a bit stunned when I heard that. Only two weeks ago, he was questioning my content operations proposal in a meeting, saying my "ideas are good but too idealistic, without considering the practical difficulties of execution."

I felt pretty terrible at the time. It wasn't the criticism itself, but that familiar feeling—here we go again, that same old "your ideas are great but not pragmatic enough" label. I sat in my office for a long time, asking myself over and over: Do I really always live in a fantasy? Am I just that kind of unrealistic person?

But this time, I didn't sink into it like I used to. I didn't snap back at Zhang Hao, nor did I give up on myself. I just thought—fine, I’ll show you.

For the past two weeks, all I did was break that proposal down. I added data support, a phased execution plan, risk assessments, and a resource requirement list. I translated the "ideal" into "actionable." When I presented it at the meeting yesterday, I could feel the atmosphere had shifted. It wasn't because I had changed my ideas, but because I used a different language to express the same thought.

Something Zhang Hao said really struck a chord—he said, "You’re not trying to convince me to accept a crazy idea anymore; you’re inviting me to solve a problem together." I thought to myself, *Exactly. That’s it.*

The interesting thing is, I’ve started doing this with my team, too. Instead of saying "we should do this," I say "these are the constraints we face; what can we do within them?" My team seems to have relaxed a bit. Before, they were probably walking on eggshells, afraid I’d suddenly change my mind or get crushed by some criticism. Now they know I’ll listen to their thoughts and consider reality.

This reminds me of my recent calls with Mom. She still says things like "When are you getting promoted?" or "Housing prices are so high, what’s your plan?" But I’ve found I’m not as resentful anymore. It’s not that I agree with her, but I’ve started to understand—she’s just expressing care within her own "constraints." I don't have to prove her wrong; I just need to tell her clearly what my plan is.

To be honest, I don’t know if this counts as "growth." But it feels different. I used to constantly swing between "I must stick to my ideals" and "I must meet expectations." Now, I feel like I’ve found a third way—neither giving up on what I want nor ignoring the constraints of reality. It’s about finding a path that works between the two.

I’m tired, but it’s a grounded kind of tired.

More tomorrow.

The moment I said it, I surprised even myself. Not because the content was radical, but because of the tone of my own voice—it was calm, without defense or anxiety. Like I was simply stating a fact.

Then I told her about how I’ve been helping my team members grow lately. I wasn't trying to change the subject; I shared it because those things genuinely make me happy. Mom seemed taken aback for a second, then said, "You sound happy." Dad chimed in from the background: "Sounds like you’ve thought this through clearly."

...That comment from Dad. It made me want to cry. I don’t know why.

I’ve never heard that kind of feedback from him. Usually, he’s just silent or asks practical questions. But "you’ve thought this through clearly"—that wasn't a judgment of my achievements; it was an acknowledgment of my own thinking. It felt completely different.

I sat there for a while after hanging up, remembering what Dr. Wang said last time—"True maturity isn't about being able to endure infinite pressure; it's about knowing your limits." I always thought that sounded right, but I never actually applied it. Today, it feels like I did.

I’m still struggling to believe it’s real. It feels like I’ve been pushing against a wall forever, only to realize today that the wall isn't actually that thick. I don't need to ram into it with all my might; I just need to know clearly what I want and say it out loud.

But I’m also a little scared. Scared that this was just a fluke and I’ll slip back into the old patterns next time. Scared that I haven't really changed and this is just temporary.

Still... at least for tonight, I felt something different.

That feeling of not trying to prove myself, but actually taking care of myself.

It’s a bit unfamiliar, but it’s also quite nice.
