The moment the words left my mouth, I froze. What on earth was that? Since when do I say things like that? But the strange thing is... I didn't want to take it back.

She was right. When my partner told me the other day that I "seemed confident," I realized I had been faking it all along. I thought my disguise was seamless, but then a near-stranger saw right through it. I was terrified then—terrified of what would happen if I stopped pretending, if I showed my real self.

But now... now I don’t know.

Maybe things really are changing? Maybe I don’t have to completely negate myself every time I make a mistake. Maybe... maybe someone can accept the version of me that screws up, gets lost, and doubts herself.

But I still have my doubts. Is this just a one-off? Will I go back to my old ways tomorrow? Was Li Ming’s praise just politeness? Are my feelings about my partner just a fantasy?

It’s late. My phone screen is still glowing. I should sleep. But I want to think about it for a little longer—what if this isn't a fantasy?

Maybe I really am learning to accept myself.

Maybe.

# Work Log | 2023.06.08

11:30 PM. Just got back from the gym. Sitting on the sofa after a shower, and it suddenly hit me that I should jot down today’s thoughts.

---

**A Shift in Career Direction**

I’ve been mulling over a certain question lately, but today I finally arrived at a clear answer.

I used to constantly ask myself, "Should I switch careers?" or "Should I be doing something else?" This question has been looping in my head for a long time—especially when work gets frustrating, and I just want to escape. But while chatting with my neighbor today, I suddenly realized—I’ve been asking the wrong question.

The real question isn't "Should I leave?" but "How can I do better in this direction?"

I know that sounds like typical "chicken soup for the soul," but it feels genuinely different to me this time. Maybe it’s because I’ve been studying product thinking lately; I’m starting to understand that many of the issues I face at work aren't because "I’m not cut out for this," but because "I haven't figured it out yet." Take that "analysis paralysis" incident last week—at the time, I felt like a total failure. Looking back now, it was actually a lesson in how to balance analysis with execution.

I’ve noticed a few traits about myself:
- I tend to overthink (it’s both a bug and a feature).
- I’m good at coming up with creative ideas, but my execution occasionally falls short.
- I adapt to new environments very quickly, but my stability is average.
- I tend to ruminate on criticism for days.

I used to view these traits strictly as weaknesses. But if I shift my perspective—these actually suggest that I’m suited for **work that requires innovative thinking and rapid iteration.** I’m not the "steady output" type; I’m the type whose best ideas are sparked by challenges.

So, instead of running away, I should double down on this path. I need to truly apply product thinking to my operations work and turn my volatility into an advantage rather than a liability.

---

**Upcoming Plans**

- Nail the user segmentation proposal—this is my first project truly applying product thinking.
- Keep up with the classes, but shift the focus from theory to practical application.
- Have a chat with Zhang Hao to see if there are opportunities to take on more product-level work within the team.
- Accept that I will make mistakes and have doubts, but don't let them become reasons to stop.

---

**A Small Reflection**

The anxiety is still there, and so is the self-doubt. But it feels different than before. It used to be "I can't do anything right," and now it's "I'm learning how to do things better." The former is a dead end; the latter is a path.

Onward to tomorrow.

# Therapy Notes | 2.13

**Key Takeaway: "Good Enough" ≠ Perfect**

Just stepped out of Dr. Wang’s office. We covered a lot today, but this was the part that hit home: I’ve been measuring myself by my ability to "solve problems" or be the "perfect companion," as if anything less than 100% is a total failure.

Dr. Wang asked me, "Lin Hao, what score do you think counts as 'good enough'?"

I didn't know how to answer at first. It was only later that I realized I’d never actually thought about it. My standards are constantly shifting—yesterday I thought 80 was enough, but today I feel like it has to be 90.

**A New Definition: I am already doing my best**

It sounds simple, but saying it out loud made me feel like crying. I realized that I don’t need to be perfect. What I need is just—to be there.

At work, I don’t need every proposal to be a stroke of creative genius. I just need consistent output.  
In my personal life, I don’t need to be the perfect companion every single second. I just need to be truly present when I am there.  
With my parents, I don’t need to be the "perfect son" they expect. I just need to be honest with them about what I’m doing.

**Action Items**

- Try this at work next week: When I start spiraling into self-doubt, stop and ask, "Am I doing my best right now?"
- If the answer is yes, let it rest there. Don’t keep raising the stakes.
- I probably need to apply this logic to my relationship too. I’m always worrying about whether I’m "being a good enough partner," but the truth is, I’m already showing up.

**Reflections**

Living with my partner these past few months, I’ve realized that so much of my anxiety comes from the thought that "I should be doing better." I’m trying to let go of that now. I feel a bit lighter.

Sleep is still good. Work performance has been steady lately. Maybe this is what "good enough" actually looks like.

Before the next session, I want to think more about how to apply this mindset to my career development.

# Saturday Afternoon, July 6, 2024

I just spent nearly an hour on the sofa re-reading my diary entries from last June. My partner was working on some design drafts next to me, occasionally looking up to ask what was on my mind.

I couldn't explain it all at once.

What was I writing about this time last year? "Maybe I'm just not cut out for operations," "I feel like I can't do anything right," "Criticized by Zhang Hao again, I’m such a loser"... page after page of the same self-negation. Reading it now, I can still feel the despair from back then—that sense of powerlessness, like no matter how hard I tried, I could never get it right.

But looking at those words today, my reaction has changed.

I said to my partner, "I think I finally know who I am now." She looked up. I continued, "High-energy but needing a rhythm; creative but needing structure. These aren't flaws—they’re just who I am."

I felt very calm saying it. There was none of that old urgency to "fix my weaknesses," nor that forced self-consolation of "accepting my imperfect self." It just felt like... stating a fact.

Those anxieties actually left a trail. The despair didn't come out of nowhere; it came from not understanding myself, from failing to give myself the right rhythm and structure. That’s why I kept swinging between high energy and self-doubt. Now I see it clearly—those moments weren't about me "failing," but about me "not yet finding the way that works for me."

The transformation over these past two and a half years has been moving from "who I should be" to "who I actually am."

My partner smiled and went back to her drawing. We didn't discuss it further, but that sense of peace has stayed with me.

Still, I know this isn't the finish line. Lately, we’ve been talking about "what kind of life we want," a conversation that is far from having an answer. My relationship with my parents is also still a work in progress—during our last call, my mom asked about buying a house again. Instead of getting defensive like I used to, I just calmly said, "I have my own plan." She probably wasn't satisfied, but I no longer need her to be.

I just need to know what path I’m on.

And now, I do.

# July 10, 2023, 6:30 PM

Sitting in the office, Li Ming asked me again if I wanted to hit the bar tonight. I said no, I’m taking care of some personal things.

He smiled and said I’ve changed lately. The old me was always the first one to jump up and suggest where to go out.

I didn’t explain much. But after he said that, I just sat here, thinking—oddly enough—that I didn’t feel guilty.

I used to. Before, whenever I turned down an invitation, I’d feel like an outsider, or like I was wasting an opportunity. I’d make up excuses or just flat-out lie. Now, I just told the truth, and then... nothing. It was fine.

It sounds like a small thing. But to me, it’s not.

I’m thinking about the question my partner asked me last weekend. "Have you thought about our future?" I didn't give a direct answer at the time. I said I needed to think. She didn't push, but I can feel the question still lingering there, like a soft knocking at the door.

I’ve been running away from these kinds of questions. Running from my parents’ nagging, from career choices, even from what I actually want. So I kept moving—bars, murder mystery games, movies, overtime—as if as long as I stayed busy enough, I wouldn’t have to face those "what should I do" questions.

But now... I don’t know if it’s the therapy sessions starting to work, or because my partner makes me feel seen, but I’m starting to be able to hear my own voice.

I don’t want to go to the bar, not because I’ve become antisocial, but because I want to be alone tonight. I want to figure some things out. I want to know that when I say, "I want to plan a future with you," it’s what I truly mean, and not just another choice made to meet someone else's expectations.

The two feel very similar. Sometimes I can't tell them apart.

But maybe this is all I can do for now—just sit with this uncertainty instead of rushing to escape it.

I’m going to have a real talk with her tomorrow.

Dr. Wang, I wanted to share a small moment from today with you.

This afternoon at the office, Li Ming came to me to discuss the direction of a project. He seemed quite conflicted, repeatedly saying, "I don't know which option to choose; I'm afraid of making the wrong call."

In the past, I probably would have just given him a direct suggestion or said, "Don't overthink it, just go with this." But today, I didn't. Instead, I asked him a few questions—why he felt both options were risky, what the underlying assumptions were for each, and what the worst-case scenario would be if things failed.

He slowly cleared his head and eventually made a decision on his own. Throughout the whole process, I was just listening and asking.

Later, it suddenly hit me: this is exactly what you do for me in our sessions. You don't tell me "what I should do"; you help me see my own thoughts clearly. I used to find it a bit frustrating (I remember once I asked you if I should change jobs, and you asked me back, "What do you think?" I was actually a bit annoyed at the time 😅), but now I get it.

What’s even more interesting is that while helping Li Ming sort through things, I found myself feeling very relaxed. Not the "I fixed it" kind of relaxed, but... a sense of accepting the fact that I can offer advice, but I can also just be there. I don't always need to have the answers.

My sleep has been much better this month, and I’m not scrolling through my phone late at night as much, feeling anxious about other people’s achievements. I’ve learned to accept all parts of myself—including my uncertainty, my hesitation, and my contradictions. Ironically, once I accepted them, those things didn't seem so scary anymore.

I wanted to ask, is this what you mean by "the beginning of change"? It feels a bit surreal, but it also feels like it’s actually happening.

I’ve been thinking about what you said.

I’ve been sitting by the window for a while now, watching my phone screen light up and go dark, over and over. When you walked in and asked what I was doing, I said "nothing," but my head has actually been spinning.

You’re right. A lot of my fears are probably just things I’ve imagined. My mom has never actually said the words "you’re not good enough," but I keep adding those lines in my head. It’s like… I’m judging myself on her behalf. Weird, right?

I’ve made up my mind. I’m going to be honest with her. Not asking for her "permission," but just telling her: "I have a girlfriend now." But I need time to prepare. Not the "avoiding it" kind of preparation, but I really need to clarify what I want to say and how to say it. Dr. Wang also said this would be better—not to make this decision while caught up in my emotions.

I really appreciate that you haven't been pushing me. And thank you for believing that I can handle this. Sometimes I feel like you believe in me more than I believe in myself.

Are you free tomorrow? I’d like to talk more. Not because I’m getting anxious again, just… I want to make sure we’re on the same page.

Goodnight.

# Memo

**Date**: September 27, 2022, 9:15 PM

**Subject**: A New Understanding of Success

---

I just got off the phone with my mom. We were talking about work and buying a house. In the past, these conversations always made me tense—I’d either be desperate to prove myself or just snap back at her. But today, I seemed to respond in a different way.

I told her, "Right now, I’m learning how to be my authentic self."

The moment I said it, I was a bit surprised. It’s not the kind of thing I usually say, but it just came out naturally.

Then, when she asked about promotions and buying an apartment, I said, "Those things will happen in their own time."

**What I want to record right now is this shift**:

**The old me**: Success = Promotion + Owning a home + Standing out among my peers. Anything less was failure. So I was constantly anxious, constantly trying to prove myself, and constantly feeling unsettled by other people's achievements.

**The new me**: Maybe success is being able to completely let down your guard in front of someone, to be accepted without judgment. Maybe success is being able to look at mistakes at work calmly, seeing them as learning opportunities rather than failures. Maybe success is simply being able to tell my mom the truth, instead of rushing to either please her or push her away.

It sounds a bit abstract, but I can truly feel the difference now.

**The key shift**: When I stopped being anxious about proving myself, things like promotions and buying a house actually became less daunting. They might happen, or they might not, but they are no longer the only yardstick I use to measure my worth.

I used to place far too much weight on them.

---

**Note to self**: The next time you hit a setback at work, or feel that pang of anxiety when you see someone’s promotion on social media, come back and read this. Remember that you have found a different way to define yourself.

1. **The 1st Second**: My eyes definitely lit up. I could feel my own reaction—this is what I wanted, isn't it?
2. **The 2nd Second**: My hands started shaking slightly. My heart rate sped up.
3. **The Next 30 Seconds**: A flood of questions flashed through my mind—"Can I actually manage people?" "My own performance fluctuates; how can I manage others?" "Is this going to pull me back into that cycle of working myself to death just to prove my worth?"
4. **Finally**: I told Zhang Hao I needed a week to think it over. He said that was fine and asked for an answer by Friday.

---

## Current State

**Physical Signals:**
- Hands are still shaking a bit
- Might have trouble sleeping again tonight
- Feeling restless; can't sit still

**Questions Looping in My Head:**
- Is this position truly what I want, or am I just trying to escape the question of "Am I good enough?"
- Management and operations are completely different animals. I perform well in operations sometimes, but that doesn't mean I can lead people.
- If I accept, will I revert to that state of "overworking to prove myself"? I’ve only just managed to recover from that kind of anxiety.
- But what if I refuse? Will I regret it? Will it be interpreted as "I don't want to progress"?

---

## To-Do This Week

**People I Must Talk To:**
- My Partner – I need to hear her thoughts. She understands my contradictory nature better than anyone; she’ll help me see if I truly want this or if I’m just trying to meet an expectation.
- Dr. Wang – I need to discuss this in our next counseling session. This touches on my definition of "success" and whether I’m falling back into the pattern of "using a promotion to validate myself."

**Questions for Reflection:**
1. If I accept this position, how will my life change? Working hours? Stress? Time with my partner? Time for the gym?
2. Where does my fear of "management" come from? Is it a fear of failure? Or a fear of losing my current flexibility?
3. What does this position offer me? Salary? A sense of achievement? Or just the feeling of "finally being recognized"?
4. If I refuse, will I really regret it? Or am I just afraid of my parents' reaction?

---

## Reminders to Self

- Don't rush the decision. One week is a reasonable timeframe.
- This isn't an "all-or-nothing" binary choice. There might be other options.
- Anxiety is normal. It shows that this decision matters to me. But anxiety is not the answer.
- Listen to my partner and Dr. Wang, but the final decision must come from me.

I just got back from my mom’s.

I’m still in a bit of shock over what happened.

I was actually really nervous at first—my hands were shaking while I sat across from her. I’d spent so long thinking about how to bring it up, but in the end, I just blurted it out: "Mom, I have a girlfriend." I was braced for judgment, you know? I expected a barrage of questions like, "Why didn't you tell me sooner?" or "Where is she from?"

But she didn't do that.

She just asked how we met and how your work was going. Then she said, "You finally have a girlfriend. I was so worried about you being all alone in the city."

My eyes just welled up right then.

I never expected her to say that. For years, I always felt like she was judging me, pointing out my flaws. But in that moment, I realized she’s actually just been worried about me all this time. It wasn't the kind of worry like "you're not successful enough," but rather, "my son is far away on his own, and I hope he isn't too lonely."

When I asked her, "Do you really want to meet her?" I could hear the uncertainty in my own voice. Reality was just so different from what I had played out in my head.

She said she does. She said she wants to see who it is that’s been keeping me company.

I’m still a little dazed. For years, I’ve been running away from home, trying to escape that feeling of being judged. But maybe the thing I’ve been running from wasn't even real. Maybe I’m the one who made it up.

When you asked me the other day, "Do you really want to plan a future with me?" I was actually terrified. Terrified of commitment, of losing control, of messing things up like I always do. But it was because you asked that I finally found the courage to face my mom. And now...

I feel different.

I want to go see them with you. I mean it.

Thank you.

Just like you suggested during our last session, I asked myself, "Does this really have to be done right now?"

To be honest, I felt a bit of a panic the moment I turned them off. I kept thinking, "What if the project fails because I wasn't committed enough?" That thought kept looping, especially during the evening. But I stuck to my guns.

Later, I talked to my partner about it, and we discussed it together. I’ve decided that from today on, I won’t check work emails after 10 PM. I’ve disabled the alerts.

I know this might not sound like a big deal, but it feels different to me. It feels like the first time I’m actually following through on a commitment to myself, rather than just talking about it.

I just wanted to ask—is this level of anxiety normal? Should I keep going with this, or is this boundary a bit too rigid?

I’d love to hear your thoughts.

# MEMO

**Date**: 2023.09.24  
**Subject**: A New Understanding of "Success"

---

I’ve been chewing on this question lately: What do I actually want?

During that afternoon yesterday with my parents and my partner, it suddenly hit me—my previous definition of "success" wasn't actually mine. Promotions, buying a house, financial freedom... there’s nothing wrong with these goals, but they felt like a checklist someone else handed me. I’ve been running a race based on someone else's standards.

Looking back, the moments that truly felt like a release weren't about hitting milestones, but rather:
- Being able to speak my mind at work without having to perform perfection.
- Feeling understood rather than judged while planning the future with my partner.
- Finally losing that suffocating tension during conversations with my parents.

**The New Understanding**: Success = Professional Achievement + Authenticity + Deep Connection + Self-Acceptance.

This isn't about giving up on goals; it’s about changing why I pursue them. I still want the promotion and the house, but not to prove anything—only because I genuinely want them for myself.

---

There’s also been a major shift from **reacting** to **proactively planning**.

I used to live in a state of constant reaction: getting nervous when my parents asked questions, spiraling into self-doubt when criticized at work, and feeling anxious whenever I saw others' achievements on social media. Now, I’m starting to choose—choosing who I spend time with, choosing the kind of life I want, and even proactively communicating my thoughts to my parents.

This sense of agency has made my emotions much more stable. It’s not that the anxiety has vanished, but I now know how to coexist with it.

---

**Immediate Next Steps**:
- Finalize the specific timeline for moving in with my partner.
- Maintain this stability at work and lead the team well.
- Communicate with my parents regularly to maintain this new relational rhythm.
- Don’t let these insights slip away when pressure builds up.

This memo is for my future self. The next time I start spiraling into self-doubt, I need to read this.

I’m sitting in my office chair, my phone screen lights up, and I decide to send you a message. It’s 11:00 AM. The sunlight outside the window is blinding, but my mood is even brighter than the weather.

---

I’m still thinking about yesterday.

Sitting there in the restaurant, watching you chat with my parents, I suddenly felt like crying. It wasn’t out of nerves, it was... I can’t quite put it into words. It was just the moment I realized that this wasn't some kind of exam or a hurdle I had to clear. It was just three people I care about, sitting together, having a real conversation. That was it.

Do you remember when you took my hand? My palms were sweating like crazy, but you didn't let go. And then you took the initiative to ask my mom about her teaching and my dad about his projects at the state enterprise. Watching you do that, I finally understood—you weren't trying to "pass" them; you were just sincerely connecting with them. I never imagined it could be like that.

I used to think this meeting was a test, a pass-or-fail thing. But it wasn't like that at all. The way my parents looked at me wasn't judgmental; they were... seeing me. Really seeing me.

I felt a bit embarrassed when the tears started falling, but you said, "It's okay, this is good." That was the moment I truly believed that it’s okay to just let go.

I also wanted to say thank you for how you were yesterday. You made the whole meeting feel different. Not because you did anything grand, but just because you were yourself—sincere, natural, and genuinely interested in them. When I watched you talking to my mom, she had a smile on her face. A real smile.

I’m realizing now that a lot of the fear I had toward them was actually something I made up. They aren't as scary as I imagined. Or rather, they weren't scary to begin with; I was just the one constantly running away.

At work today, Zhang Hao asked for my thoughts on an operations plan. Instead of second-guessing myself like I usually do, I just said it. After he heard it, he said it was good. I didn't overanalyze his words like I normally would; I just accepted it. It was simple.

I wanted to tell you all this because it’s all connected to you.

Dinner tonight? I want to talk more.

qa_items.question: Lin Hao, I've seen you grinding away at this career change and those PM courses for months now. You went from being incredibly anxious at the start to where you are today—what would you say has been the biggest shift in your mindset?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I’ve actually realized that I was stuck in a bit of a trap before. Back then, I was obsessively looking up career-change stories and signing up for product management courses, but in reality, I was just trying to "switch tracks" to escape this sense of failure I had. It wasn't until I opened the course materials and saw that the content was pretty much the same as the operations work I’m doing now that it hit me: the problem might not be my career, but rather that I’m being too hard on myself. Now, I’ve kept the note "the issue isn't the profession, it's the mindset" in my phone. I actually feel a lot more relaxed; I’m no longer obsessing over "success" or buying a house, and my headspace is much more stable.

evidence.statement: Lin Hao frantically searched for terms like "product manager salary," "career change costs," and "career transition case studies" on the subway. Yet, these searches never progressed beyond mere browsing, leading to no actual learning or decisive action—this was a case of anxiety-driven searching rather than rational decision-making.

Upon opening the course, Lin Hao realized the content—user segmentation, requirements analysis, and A/B testing—merely mirrored the work he was already doing in operations. This crushed his last hope of escaping his predicament through a career change. After writing down the words, "Maybe the problem isn't the profession, but myself," he sank into a state of profound despair.

Once I stopped rushing for immediate results and focused on solving specific, small problems instead, that sense of uncertainty about the future naturally vanished. Now, I feel much more grounded in my daily work.

The biggest change is that I’ve learned the concept of "separation of tasks." I used to treat KPIs and my boss’s feedback as the sole measure of my self-worth, which left me so stressed I couldn't sleep. Later, I signed up for a meditation class and started a daily 30-minute morning routine, trying to decouple my emotions from objective facts. Now, even if a proposal gets sent back for revisions, I can calmly analyze the requirements instead of immediately doubting my own abilities. This sense of control makes me feel that as long as I have the right rhythm, changing careers doesn't feel so urgent anymore.

To be honest, it was that hospital stay that finally made everything click for me. During that week in the hospital, looking out the window, I realized that compared to your health, all that workplace competition and anxiety are truly insignificant. After I was discharged, I switched to a job closer to home with much less overtime. Even though the pay took a bit of a hit, I now have more time for hiking and photography. Once the focus of my life shifted from "career achievement" to "personal hobbies," I realized that my previous desperate urge to change careers was actually just because my life had become too one-dimensional.

qa_items.question: Looking back on the time from June until now—from the impulsive person who signed up for product management classes to switch careers, to the person who can now calmly handle Zhang Hao's criticism—what do you think this experience has helped you see clearly?
qa_items.answer: I’m a bit ashamed to admit that back in June, I let anxiety get the better of me. I was convinced that switching careers was the only way to escape the frustration I was feeling. It took some time to realize that the problem wasn't whether I was in operations or product management; it was that I was constantly holding myself hostage to the idea of "perfection." I’ve come to understand that instead of trying to outrun my setbacks by changing jobs, I should focus on accepting my own imperfections. Now, when Zhang Hao criticizes my work, my first thought isn't "I'm a failure" anymore. Instead, it’s "Okay, I’ll just fix this detail." Honestly, it feels much more grounding this way.

qa_items.question: Looking back at your period of struggle, you used to believe your misery stemmed from being in the wrong profession, and you even considered pivoting to product management. But now, you seem to have moved past all that. What was the turning point that changed your perspective?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? Honestly, I was in a really bad place for a while. Especially after getting torn apart by my boss that one time—I kept thinking I was such a failure because I’d just chosen the wrong career path. But later, I realized that thinking "a career change will fix everything" was actually just a way of running away. It wasn't until a deep talk with my partner recently that things clicked. She said something that really woke me up: the problem might not be the profession itself, but how I view myself. I realized that if I’m still the kind of person who crumbles the moment someone criticizes me, it won’t matter what industry I’m in. Once I wrapped my head around that, I actually relaxed. I stopped feeling this desperate need to prove something, and now, I’m even sleeping much better.

Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him both despair and relief—despair from the fundamental nature of the problem, and relief from finally ceasing to deceive himself.

It hit me then that my previous misery stemmed from not having produced any satisfying results in my operations role yet; you can't find a sense of achievement by simply switching tracks. After I got home that day, I audited my entire workflow and decided to stay put until I achieved a major win. Having that sense of purpose instantly cleared away all my indecision.

It’s mainly because while working on the final capstone project for that advanced product management course I took, I realized that although I could grasp the theories, I didn’t actually enjoy the constant requirement alignments and documentation as much as I thought I would. I realized my previous desire for a career change was just a case of "the grass is greener on the other side"—I simply assumed what I hadn't tried must be better. Once I redirected that same drive for product research back into operational strategy, everything just clicked. Naturally, I saw no point in pushing for a career pivot anymore.

It’s quite strange to think about, but it all started when I took a week off to recover from an illness. During that time, I completely disconnected from work and dove into a lot of philosophy books. I came to see that work is just one part of life; as long as I keep learning and growing, no profession is inherently superior to another. qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I actually think it would have been worse. To be honest, the day I deleted those records, I had a premonition: if I went into interviews with that "escapist" mindset, even if I landed a new offer, I’d just be repeating the same old vicious cycle. Back then, I constantly felt like a total failure, and everything I looked at annoyed me. If I hadn't first let go of that "must be perfect" baggage, I would have still spiraled into self-doubt over the smallest mistake, even in a different industry. I’m still in operations now, but being able to stay calm under Zhang Hao’s criticism and actually present a plan for improvement—that’s where the real sense of groundedness comes from. And that’s not something you can get just by switching jobs.

If I hadn't stepped up to lead that cross-departmental review project, or if I hadn't laid bare my own logical blind spots during that summary meeting, I’d probably still be stuck in that self-indulgent "unrecognized genius" loop. Zhang Hao gave me this opportunity mostly because he saw that I was finally willing to deconstruct my own workflow, rather than just looking for an exit the moment things got tough. It was this shift in mindset that actually allowed me to step up and deliver on the roadmap he laid out for me.

To be honest, there’s no way I would’ve gotten it. Back then, I was completely hollow inside, constantly telling myself that switching tracks would fix everything that was going wrong. In reality, I was just looking for a painkiller. If I hadn't forced myself to delete all those career-change bookmarks and confronted my "all-or-nothing" perfectionist mindset, I would have still been timid and ready to retreat the moment I stood before Zhang Hao. It was only because I decided to stop running that he saw that sense of certainty in my eyes—and that’s probably why he was willing to bet the team’s future on me.

It was a real struggle for me. Back then, I was obsessed with those "career personality tests," always trying to find a role that would be a perfect fit for my personality while ignoring the fact that every job comes with its own growing pains. If I hadn’t pushed through to finish that independent review of the major client we almost lost, or if I hadn’t been honest during the weekly meeting about my lack of data sensitivity, I’d probably still be stuck in that restless state of mind. It was only because I stopped blaming my mistakes on "mismatched roles" and chose to stay put and fix my own shortcomings that Zhang Hao saw the resilience in me to lead a team. That was what finally made him willing to sit down and have that serious conversation with me about the three-year plan.

To be honest, it was a lost cause. At that time, I was already secretly sending out resumes, thinking that switching companies would solve the internal friction caused by poor communication. If I hadn't let go of my "fear of losing face" during that project retrospective and taken the initiative to ask Zhang Hao for advice on handling the pressure of multi-threaded tasks, there might have always been a wall between us. I realized then that if I didn't fix my habit of avoiding conflict, it wouldn't matter where I went. It was only because I started showing my genuine confusion and my determination to improve that Zhang Hao began to see me as a partner worth investing in for the long haul, rather than just a passerby ready to jump ship at any moment.

qa_items.question: After that career crisis last year, you impulsively signed up for that product management course. What was it that suddenly made you realize the problem wasn't the career, but yourself, and led you to finally give up on switching fields?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I felt like a complete idiot when I opened the course syllabus that day. I had thought a career change would give me a "new lease on life," only to find that topics like user segmentation and A/B testing were exactly what I was already doing in my current operations role. It was at that moment I realized I didn't actually want a new career; I was just running away from the frustrations of my job. Having that path of self-deception blocked off felt pretty hopeless, but strangely enough, it also brought a sense of peace. I finally didn't have to keep lying to myself that I’d magically shine just by switching tracks.

evidence.statement: After being indirectly criticized during the project retrospective, Lin Hao impulsively purchased a product management course for ¥1,299 that very same day, attempting to alleviate his sense of powerlessness by simply "doing something."

Upon opening the course, Lin Hao realized that the content—user segmentation, requirements analysis, and A/B testing—overlapped with what he was already doing in operations. This crushed his last hope of escaping his predicament through a career change. After writing down the words, "Maybe the problem isn't the profession, but myself," he fell into a state of profound despair.

Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him both despair and relief—despair from the fundamental nature of the issue, and relief from finally ending his self-deception.

choices.text: It’s funny how things worked out. That day, I was struggling with my first competitive analysis report, unable to get the logic straight, so I went back to my old weekly reports from my operations days for inspiration. As I read through them, I realized that the logic I had built into those plans was actually quite solid; back then, I just always felt like I wasn't doing enough. I realized that what I lacked wasn't "product thinking," but rather an acknowledgment of my own professional competence. That kind of missing self-confidence isn't something you can fix just by switching career paths.

To be honest, I felt like a complete idiot when I opened the syllabus that day. I had convinced myself that a career change would be my "fresh start," only to find that the topics covered—user segmentation, A/B testing, and the like—were exactly what I was already doing in my current operations role. It was at that moment I realized I didn't actually want a new career; I was just trying to run away from the frustrations of my job. Having that path of self-deception blocked off felt devastating, but strangely enough, it also brought a sense of relief. I finally didn't have to lie to myself anymore that I’d magically shine just by switching tracks.

It actually came up during a dinner with a former colleague. He’d switched to product management about six months ago, and that day he was venting to me about how his life was nothing but writing docs and fighting over requirements—he even had to order takeout for the devs. As I listened, I realized the anxiety he was describing was identical to the internal burnout I was feeling in my operations role. It hit me then: if I don't fix the way I handle negative emotions, even if I change my job title, I’d just be dragging my current misery to a different desk. There was no point in making the move.

That day, I made a point of meeting my former mentor for coffee to seek his advice. Instead of directly persuading me to stay, he asked me to make a list of the three moments I hated most about being an operations manager. Once I finished, he pointed out that these moments all traced back to my weakness: social anxiety. It hit me then that even if I switched to product management, I would still have to face these same communication hurdles. At that moment, I decided to stop running away and stay put until I finally cleared this hurdle.

qa_items.question: Since you restarted therapy recently, has there been anything Dr. Wang said that particularly struck a chord with you—something that truly let you let go of the obsession with "getting ahead" and started you thinking about what kind of professional life you actually want for yourself?
qa_items.answer: Well... how can I put it? That day, Dr. Wang asked me what kind of life I would actually want if I set aside my mother’s expectations and comparisons with my peers. I just froze, and my eyes welled up instantly, because I realized that in twenty-six years of living, I had never once asked myself that question. He later added that my value is defined by the essence of who I am, not by my achievements. At that moment, I felt something inside me finally let go. It turns out I don’t have to force myself to become a "success" in everyone else’s eyes.

evidence.statement: When Dr. Wang asked, "If you stripped away your mother's expectations and the constant comparisons with your peers, what kind of professional life would you truly want?" Lin Hao fell into silence, his eyes welling up—he realized he had never actually asked himself that question.

Lin Hao redefined the root of his anxiety from "difficulty in making career choices" to a "fear of failure and an obsession with perfection," reflecting his deep understanding of the essence of the problem.

choices.text: At the time, Dr. Wang suggested I look back on the happiest moments of my life, and I realized that, surprisingly, none of them had anything to do with job titles or bonuses. He went on to say that we often travel so far that we forget why we started in the first place, and that the "must-win" mentality is actually a defense mechanism. After that conversation, I went home and cleared away all those books on social rhetoric and how to be successful. I felt myself finally breaking free from that tense, competitive state, wanting for the first time to try planning my future at my own pace.

It was actually that time Dr. Wang asked me to close my eyes and imagine an ideal day ten years from now. I realized there were no office buildings or spreadsheets in the picture—only floor-to-ceiling windows and bookshelves. He told me that often, the "success" we chase is just a way to fill our inner insecurities rather than a pursuit of true passion. At that moment, I realized that if I kept living to meet other people's standards, I might never find that genuine sense of inner peace. That’s what made me decide to re-examine my current career path.

That day, Dr. Wang pulled out a blank sheet of paper and asked me to write down the three things I would want to do most if salary and reputation were off the table. I sat there holding the pen for a long time, unable to write a single word, feeling a profound sense of distress. He told me that a career is just one way to explore oneself, not the sum total of life, and there’s no need to treat it as the sole evaluation system. Hearing that, I suddenly felt it was pointless to keep obsessing over competing with friends who were climbing the corporate ladder so fast. I started thinking about how to make work serve my life, rather than sacrificing my life for the sake of achievement.

Well… how can I put this? That day, Dr. Wang asked me what kind of life I would actually want if I stripped away my mom’s expectations and the comparisons with my peers. I just froze. My eyes welled up instantly because I realized that in twenty-six years of living, I had never once asked myself that question. qa_items.question: Lin Hao, have you noticed? You seem to have this habit of obsessively bookmarking professional tips and "career hacks" late at night or whenever you're feeling down. But looking back, has all this "action" actually made you feel any more secure?
qa_items.answer: Now that you mention it, I actually feel a bit ashamed... It’s true, whenever I’m lying awake at night, tormented by anxiety, I go on a shopping spree on Xiaohongshu and Dedao, even buying e-books about career changes. Back then, I always felt that as long as I bought or bookmarked them, it meant I was making a change, and it gave me a little peace of mind. But to be honest, I usually close most of those courses after watching for barely ten minutes, and the next day, I’m back to posting angsty, lost updates on my Moments. This kind of "false hope" is actually quite draining; aside from shrinking my bank balance, it hasn't done anything to truly solve my current career crisis.

qa_items.question: In the past, whenever Li Ming pointed out your mistakes, you’d always feel like a failure. But recently, when he offered feedback again, you actually managed to calmly ask, "How will this impact the execution?" What do you think has been the biggest shift in your mindset?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to treat every mistake at work like a "final judgment" on who I am. Whenever I messed up, I’d take it as proof that I just wasn't cut out for it, and I even considered switching careers altogether. But lately, I’ve started to realize that a mistake is just a mistake. It’s something that can be fixed, and it doesn't mean I’m a failure as a person. Learning to separate myself from the task has honestly been such a relief; I’m no longer tearing myself down before I’ve even had a chance to solve the problem.

evidence.statement: After seeing the news of his classmate's promotion, Lin Hao liked the post but left no comment or private message. When Li Ming pointed out the errors in the operational plan, Lin Hao didn't spiral into self-doubt. Instead, he reframed the mistakes as "learning opportunities" by asking, "How will this affect our execution? How should we adjust?"

When Li Ming pointed out the errors in the operational plan, Lin Hao didn't spiral into self-doubt. Instead, he reframed the mistake as a "learning opportunity" by asking, "How will this affect our execution, and how can we adjust?"

choices.text: To be honest, it’s because I’ve learned to "embrace uncertainty as a default." I used to always chase a perfect execution path, and any deviation would send me into a spiral of anxiety. But recently, while conducting a retrospective with the newcomers, I suddenly realized that every plan is essentially just a hypothesis until it's implemented. Li Ming’s feedback was actually helping me eliminate potential risks in advance. This shift from a "defensive stance" to a "collaborative stance" has allowed me to focus entirely on risk hedging, rather than obsessing over saving face.

It’s actually about having a mental "buffer zone." I used to view this kind of feedback as a confrontation, feeling like I had to prove who was right or wrong, which ended up being exhausting. Later, I tried reviewing successful cases and realized that everyone’s growth is built on this level of "high-granularity" feedback. Now, I focus more on the logical reasoning behind the feedback; as long as it helps the project land more effectively, it doesn't really matter who suggested it. This objective perspective has helped me avoid a lot of emotional burnout.

I think the most fundamental change is that I’ve developed a "multi-dimensional evaluation" mindset. In the past, whenever I hit a snag at work, I felt like my entire life was falling apart because I had staked my entire sense of self-worth on that one thing. Recently, I’ve started trying to find anchors outside of work—like sticking to a fitness routine and a regular sleep schedule. This has made me realize that even when work is volatile, I can still maintain control over the fundamentals of my life. What matters is that I finally have the courage to stop running and instead face the challenge of how to hone myself and become more grounded in my current profession.

There’s something incredibly captivating about unearthing growth opportunities from massive datasets; it feels like I’ve found the "second curve" of my career. Since my current platform offers such vast room for exploration, why would I want to start from scratch in a completely unfamiliar field?

Actually, it was while attending an industry summit last year that I heard a talk by a senior growth expert. The "omni-channel operations" logic he mentioned was a real wake-up call for me. I realized my previous understanding of operations had been far too narrow. Later, I tried incorporating some automation strategies into my current projects, and the results were surprisingly good, which helped me regain a sense of control. Now, I feel there's no need to switch tracks just for the sake of it; the real skill lies in mastering the business you already have in front of you.

I’ve taken on a few freelance projects privately, originally intending to sharpen my product thinking for interviews. However, as I got into the work, I realized my true strength lies in community conversion and private domain maintenance. Seeing those tangible revenue growth numbers gives me a much greater sense of achievement than drawing prototypes ever did. Plus, my parents are now supportive of me staying in this city to build my career. I no longer feel the need to burn myself out for those so-called high-paying positions, so my mindset has become much more at peace.

I'm ashamed to admit it, but back then, a few words of criticism from my boss were enough to make me completely break down. I felt like operations was a dead end and wanted to switch careers just to escape. But after chatting with Dr. Wang a few times, I realized that I actually really enjoy the process of bringing creative ideas to life and seeing user feedback. I’ve also had an open and honest talk with Zhang Hao now, and he even helped me with some career planning. Although I still worry about whether I can stick it out, this sense of groundedness—of knowing exactly what I need to do—is worlds apart from the blind anxiety I felt last year.

That old reaction of mine—wanting to run away the moment I was criticized—was really just a way to soothe my anxiety through the "fake productivity" of switching careers. It’s just like last year when Zhang Hao gave me some feedback, and I impulsively bought a Product Manager course; I was actually just escaping. Later, I went back to talk to Dr. Wang and gradually realized that "instability" is a challenge I need to manage, not a death sentence for my career. When I reached out to Zhang Hao for advice a while ago, I discovered he had gone through a similar phase. Once that wall of him being a "judge" came down, I actually felt seen, and I feel much more grounded now.

Later, I started giving myself a five-minute "cool-down period" whenever I was criticized—I’d go grab a glass of water in the pantry or take a quick walk downstairs. This physical change of scenery helped me break the cycle of self-doubt. By the time I got back to my desk to face Zhang Hao, I was able to look at things much more objectively.

qa_items.question: Looking back, from that moment last June when you impulsively signed up for a course to switch careers after being criticized, to now, where you’ve decided to commit to the operations field—how has your mindset shifted over the past year or so?
qa_items.answer: I’m honestly a bit embarrassed looking back. Last year, I let frustration get the best of me, thinking that switching tracks would solve everything—I even dropped over 400 yuan on a Product Manager course. I eventually realized that whether it’s Operations or Product, the core logic is the same; running away wasn't going to fix my own "bottlenecks." Thanks to Dr. Wang’s guidance and my partner’s acceptance over the past year, I’ve gradually come to see that my emotional fluctuations aren't a flaw, but a trait. Now that I’ve had a heart-to-heart with Zhang Hao and committed to a plan to dive deep into Operations, I feel so much more grounded. This feeling of "knowing what I need to do and being able to manage my own pace" is worlds apart from the blind desperation I felt when I wanted to switch careers.

evidence.statement: In an impulsive move, Lin Hao signed up for a product management course costing 499 yuan a month and immediately announced to his partner, "I might pivot to product management." This was a classic example of him using "action" as a way to escape his current sense of failure.

Upon opening the course, Lin Hao realized that the curriculum—covering user segmentation, requirements analysis, and A/B testing—merely mirrored the work he was already doing in operations. This crushed his final hope of escaping his predicament through a career change. After writing down the words, "Maybe the problem isn't the profession, but myself," he spiraled into a state of profound despair.

In his conversation with Zhang Hao, Lin Hao articulated his career direction with clarity and candor: "I've gone through periods of self-doubt, but I now realize that I truly want to deepen my expertise in the operations field." This stood in stark contrast to his previous tentative or anxious tone.
Lin Hao expressed a newfound understanding of himself to his partner: "My volatility is not a flaw, but a trait"—a reflection of his fundamental acceptance of his professional identity.

choices.text: The real turning point came late last year at an industry salon, where I happened to meet Sister Lin, a senior operations expert at ByteDance. After reviewing my project debriefs, she told me bluntly that the issue wasn't a lack of ability, but a lack of systematic methodology. Following that, I dropped all those random cross-industry online courses and started grinding through the data modeling reading list she recommended. As several S-level mega-promotion projects successfully rolled out, I found my grasp of the business growing stronger. That sense of achievement from data-driven growth completely erased any thought of changing careers, and it earned me the respect of the entire team.

To be honest, after that impulsive attempt to quit my job, I went to Dali by myself for a week. It was while staring blankly at Erhai Lake that I finally realized my previous anxiety was actually a warning signal from my body. When I returned, I signed up for an offline psychological resilience workshop, where I learned how to decouple my sense of self-worth from my KPIs. I started trying to infuse more creative expression into my operations work instead of just mechanically completing tasks. Now, I’ve successfully transferred to the brand team. Although it’s still under the broader operations umbrella, the shift in content has helped me rediscover my original passion. I no longer need to rely on extreme fantasies of "changing careers" just to keep myself going.

In doing so, I realized I had actually built up significant industry expertise—I was just too insecure to see it before. Later, I took the initiative to talk to HR about our internal trainer program and started passing my experience on to newcomers through workshops. This shift in identity has allowed me to re-evaluate the value of this career; now, I care more about the long-term compounding interest in this field rather than my immediate job title.

qa_items.answer: To be honest, this transition hasn't been easy. I used to see "fluctuation" as a flaw and kept trying to force myself into this mold of constant stability, but the more I pushed, the more anxious and conflicted I became. Especially lately, I’ve come to realize that this sensitivity and volatility are actually part of who I am. As long as I learn to manage the rhythm, there’s nothing wrong with moving forward in waves.

qa_items.question: Lin Hao, looking back on the journey so far, how would you say your definition of "success" has changed over time?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to think success meant "standing out from the crowd," just like my mom always said. I felt like I had to get promoted faster and earn more than my peers, so seeing a classmate move up would make me incredibly anxious. But lately, I’ve started to realize that true success is actually being able to accept your own imperfections. Like my girlfriend says, this "volatility" of mine is actually a trait in itself. I’ve stopped obsessing over being a perfect "operations machine" and instead learned to manage my own pace. Being able to find balance within these ebbs and flows of life—to me, that’s already quite an achievement.

qa_items.question: Hao, if a friend of yours was in the same boat you were last year—rushing to sign up for classes just to switch careers and escape a job they hate—how would you talk them out of it?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’d probably hold him back and tell him to cool off for a few days. Last year, I was the poster child for "desperate times call for desperate measures." In a fit of impulse, I dropped 500 bucks on a Product Manager course, thinking a change of scenery would magically fix everything. But once I started the classes, I realized that while the jargon was new, the underlying logic of problem-solving was exactly the same as what I was doing in Operations. That’s when it hit me: the problem wasn’t my career choice; it was my habit of using "running away" as an escape from difficulties. So now I feel that instead of rushing to switch industries, it’s better to check if your coping mechanisms are the real issue. After all, if you carry the same old mindset with you, it’ll be the same story no matter where you go.

evidence.statement: In an impulsive move, Lin Hao signed up for a product management course costing 499 yuan a month and immediately announced to his partner, "I might pivot to product management." This was a classic example of him using "action" as a way to escape his current sense of failure.

Upon opening the course, Lin Hao realized the content—user segmentation, requirements analysis, and A/B testing—merely mirrored the work he was already doing in operations. This crushed his last hope of escaping his predicament through a career change. After writing down the words, "Maybe the problem isn't the profession, but myself," he sank into a state of profound despair.

Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him both despair and relief—despair from the fundamental nature of the problem, and relief from finally ending his self-deception.

qa_items.question: Looking back at the ups and downs of the past year, if a young person came to you for advice—someone who, just like you back then, was doubting their career choice over a mistake and looking to switch fields as a way to escape—what would you say is the most critical misconception they need to overcome?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, what I want to tell him most is this: don't think that simply switching tracks will solve everything. Back when I was frantically searching for career-change stories and signing up for Product Manager courses, I was actually just lying to myself, using "not a good fit" as a universal shield. I later realized that the core mistake is treating a "career choice" as an escape hatch from yourself. If you don't deal with the self-doubt and fear you feel when facing setbacks, that cycle of "I'm not good enough" will follow you no matter what industry you move into. Real change isn't about getting a new desk; it’s about first learning to face your own imperfect self.

evidence.statement: Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him a sense of both despair and relief—despair from the deep-seated nature of the issue, and relief from finally ceasing to deceive himself.

choices.text: The most important thing I want to share is this: never make major decisions when you are at a low point. choices.text: It’s actually quite interesting—about six months ago, I attended a cross-departmental review meeting and noticed that even Zhang Hao himself would get tongue-tied when questioned by the CTO. At that moment, it suddenly clicked: his usual harshness wasn't a personal attack on me, but simply his communication style. Since then, I’ve started documenting the core concerns behind each of his criticisms, treating his feedback like a "requirements document." Over time, I naturally stopped letting his emotions dictate my pace.

It’s actually a shift in mindset. I used to think that his disapproval meant I wasn't capable enough; I even went as far as grabbing coffee with a former boss to see if he could help me transfer to another department. But he told me that the workplace isn't short on smart people—what’s rare are those with the "thick skin" to separate their emotions from the task at hand. This past year, I’ve been practicing how to focus on solving specific problems instead of overanalyzing his tone of voice. Looking back, that practice has really paid off.

It’s mainly because I later attended some offline workshops on effective communication and realized that much of our anxiety stems from information asymmetry. Before, a single frown from him would make me want to quit; now, I take the initiative to lay out the pros and cons of various options along with contingency plans before he even speaks. Once I realized I had taken control of the conversation and could address his uncertainties with data and facts, I felt much more confident. Naturally, I no longer feel the need to act on impulse like I used to.

I’m ashamed to admit it, but back then, one word from him was enough to make me feel utterly worthless. All I could think about was "escaping," convinced that switching tracks would solve everything. Looking back, that ¥1299 course was really just a "quick-fix heart pill" I bought for my own peace of mind. Over the past year, however, I’ve gradually realized that these ups and downs are simply part of who I am. Now, instead of denying everything the moment I hit a setback, I’m more interested in figuring out how to master my own rhythm within the field of operations.

qa_items.question: You used to get so anxious watching your peers get promoted, even doubting if there was any future in operations. Now, not only are you doubling down on this field, but you're even leading a team. How did you manage to shift your mindset like that?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I used to be a real perfectionist and would get stuck in my own head. I always felt that not getting promoted meant I just wasn't good enough, and I even thought about switching careers entirely. Later, I gradually realized that all that self-doubt was actually just a way of escaping the problems at hand. Now, I no longer treat every minor mistake as a "judgment" on my character. Instead, I find the process of solving problems with the team quite grounding. That’s why I finally felt brave enough to try leading the group; this sense of "we’re in this together" is much more liberating than just trying to prove myself.

evidence.statement: Upon seeing the news of his classmate's promotion, Lin Hao gave it a like but refrained from commenting or sending a private message. He asked himself, "Why was he promoted while I can't even handle my current job?" This was a classic case of him using someone else's success to reinforce his own sense of failure.

By taking the initiative to tell the team, "This is our project; let's make it a success together," Lin Hao has demonstrated his transformation from a self-centered, self-doubting operator into a supportive manager capable of inspiring his team.

I realized that the details I used to obsess over were actually just a form of internal friction, whereas the true logic behind growth is actually quite fascinating. This shift in perspective reignited my curiosity about this career, and once I returned to my company, I started leading my team with a partner-level mindset.

It was mainly due to the company's restructuring last year—my original department was split up, and I was forced to step up and take charge of a new project. During those days of working around the clock to meet deadlines, I realized that the workplace politics I had worried about didn't actually matter when faced with result-oriented tasks. When I saw the real conversion data for the first time after the project went live, it hit me that the sense of achievement brought by data is far more substantial than any job title. That was the moment I decided to settle down and truly commit myself to this field.

qa_items.question: Lin Hao, I remember you used to think your "emotional instability" was a major weakness in your career—you were even so anxious about it that you considered a career change. How is it that you’re now able to tell your partner that "volatility is a trait, not a flaw"? How did this shift in mindset happen?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I used to really hate myself. I always felt that this cycle of being super pumped one moment and wanting to disappear the next was some kind of social defect. But later I realized that even when I tried to escape by signing up for product management classes, I ended up facing the exact same problems. It was actually something my partner said—"This is the person I've always seen"—that finally woke me up. I realized that instead of forcing myself to become some kind of high-precision machine, I should just accept that this is who I am. I need to learn how to manage this rhythm rather than constantly denying it.

choices.text: It was actually quite accidental. Last year, while I was on a hiking trip during my vacation, I got stranded in the mountains for half a day due to extreme weather. That feeling of being completely out of control made me realize that the "absolute stability" I had always chased was actually a fragile illusion. After I returned, I read a lot about complex systems and began to understand the significance of biodiversity. I realized that my emotional fluctuations are actually like the changing seasons in an ecosystem; they have their own natural metabolic rhythms. Forcing an intervention would only lead to a systemic collapse.

To be honest, the results of that physical exam really hit me hard. The doctor told me that being in such a long-term state of tension had already started affecting my endocrine system. Later, I tried attending an offline meditation workshop, where I met a woman in her late fifties. She shared with me how she learned to coexist with that sense of "uncertainty" when she was younger. It was then I realized that everyone has cracks, and there’s no need to force yourself to be a perfect vessel. If you look out through those cracks, you actually get to see a completely different landscape.

To be honest, I used to really hate myself. I always felt that this cycle of being super pumped one moment and wanting to disappear the next was some kind of social defect. It made me realize that instead of forcing myself to become some kind of precision instrument, I should just accept that this is who I am. I need to learn how to manage this rhythm rather than deny it.

It’s mainly because of the new department head; he’s completely different from the result-oriented bosses I’ve dealt with before. He once initiated a project debrief with me when I was in a slump and falling behind schedule. Instead of reprimanding me, he shared his own experience of hitting a "rock bottom" early in his career. That conversation made me realize that the workplace isn't just about moving forward at a constant speed. As long as you take responsibility for the final delivery, the ups and downs in between are actually part of an acceptable institutional buffer.

qa_items.question: Last summer, I saw you were struggling so hard with whether to switch to product management—you even signed up for classes and everything. But now, you seem much more settled in operations. How did your mindset shift over the past year?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, looking back on it now, I’m really filled with emotion. At that time, I was indeed quite anxious; I kept feeling like operations was a dead-end career, and I even wanted to switch fields just to escape the frustrations at work. But later I realized that if I didn't accept my "up-and-down" personality, it wouldn't matter what profession I changed to. Now, I actually feel that these fluctuations are just part of who I am. As long as I manage my pace well, I can still create really interesting things in operations. Like Zhang Hao said, he can already see how much I’ve changed. This feeling of being able to clearly articulate what I want is honestly so much more grounding than blindly switching careers.

qa_items.question: Lin Hao, I remember you used to be terrified of being criticized by Zhang Hao—you’d just want to hide away the moment he started lecturing you. But after this past half year, I see you’re even taking the initiative to talk to him about your career planning. How did you build up that kind of courage to face authority?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, looking back, it all feels quite surreal. There was a time when him calling me "unprofessional" felt like the end of the world—I even thought about switching careers just to run away from it all. It wasn't until I went through a severe bout of anxiety that, with the support of Dr. Wang and my girlfriend, I realized hiding wasn't the answer. My palms were soaking wet when I finally knocked on Zhang Hao’s office door that day, but I told myself that instead of constantly trying to read his expressions, I should just ask him directly how to stay steady under high pressure. To my surprise, he didn't scold me; instead, he shared some of his own setbacks from when he was younger. It made me realize he’s human just like the rest of us, and suddenly, that wall between us just crumbled.

qa_items.question: Lin Hao, I’ve noticed a significant shift in your attitude toward work compared to last year, when you were spiraling into deep self-doubt. You used to view your emotional volatility as a flaw, but now you seem to be embracing that "instability." Looking back on this journey of self-reconciliation, what would you say was the key turning point?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? That turning point was actually quite subtle. At first, I was truly hopeless, feeling like this pattern of wanting to run away whenever things got tough was a fatal flaw etched into my very bones. Then Dr. Wang asked me a question: what would I actually want if there were no parental expectations or benchmarks from others? That really stumped me; my eyes even welled up. But the real breakthrough came when my partner told me that my volatility is actually a "trait" rather than a "defect." It made me realize I don't need to prune myself into some rigid form of "stability," but rather learn to manage my own rhythm within my operations work. Now, I prefer to see it as a wave-like energy rather than a failure in life.

evidence.statement: Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him both despair and relief—despair from the fundamental nature of the issue, and relief from finally ending the self-deception.

qa_items.question: Looking back at your impulse buy two years ago—if you hadn't bought that product management course, or if you hadn't realized how much the content overlapped with operations, do you think you'd still be diving this deep into the ops field today?
qa_items.answer: Well... to be honest, that "collision" hit me pretty hard, but it was also a much-needed wake-up call. If I hadn't noticed those overlaps back then, I’d probably still be clinging to the fantasy that "switching tracks" would make me take off, and I'd just keep burning myself out by running away. It was only after seeing that the underlying logic of product and operations is essentially the same that I realized the problem wasn't the profession itself, but my own state of mind. So, even if I hadn't bought that course, as long as I didn't deal with the anxiety in my heart, I’d likely still be "running in circles" no matter where I went.

In the past, whenever Zhang Hao criticized me, my gut reaction was pure defensiveness and self-loathing; I couldn't even begin to think about how to fix the plan. It was only because that sentence kept looping in my head that I realized for the first time: treating mistakes as simple logical problems to solve is so much easier than drowning in frustration.

qa_items.question: Just as you were about to give up on the idea of a career change entirely, what made you suddenly decide in July 2022 to clear all your search history related to it and stop fantasizing that switching tracks would solve your problems?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, as I sat there that night staring at the syllabus for that Product Manager course, it suddenly hit me: things like user segmentation and A/B testing were almost identical to what I was already doing in Operations. My heart just sank. I realized that even if I switched tracks, I’d still be facing the exact same problems. I’d been running away this whole time, convinced that a change of scenery would finally let me shine. But that day, I finally saw things clearly: if I don’t change my habit of wanting to bolt the moment things get tough, it won’t matter if I switch careers ten more times. When I deleted all those saved materials, I actually felt a huge weight lift off my shoulders. Since there’s nowhere left to hide, I might as well stay right here and have it out with myself once and for all.

evidence.statement: Upon opening the course, Lin Hao realized the content—user segmentation, requirements analysis, and A/B testing—merely mirrored the work he was already doing in operations. This crushed his last hope of escaping his predicament through a career change. After writing down the words, "Maybe the problem isn't the profession, but myself," he sank into a state of profound despair.

Lin Hao realized that "the problem lay not in his career choice, but in his own way of coping." This realization brought him both despair and relief—despair from the fundamental nature of the issue, and relief from finally ending his self-deception.

Lin Hao took the initiative to delete all his search history and bookmarks related to career changes. His belief that "change is achieved not through escape, but through confrontation" marked his transition from a mode of avoidance to one of engagement.

I remember the year before last, I signed up for a product management course just to escape my operations job, only to find out the content was everything I’d already done. In that moment, I truly felt like there was something wrong with me as a person; I even spiraled into deep despair. Later, it was something my partner said that snapped me out of it. She told me that this kind of volatility isn't a flaw, but a trait. That’s when I realized I’d always been waiting for my parents or my boss to define me. Now, I’ve learned to use ‘pausing and observing’ to manage my pace, rather than blindly beating myself up. This shift from ‘escaping’ to ‘choosing’ has honestly made me feel so much more grounded.

Looking back, though, if I hadn't gone through the struggle of that "darkest hour," I wouldn't be able to embrace my own volatility like I do now—treating it as a trait rather than a flaw.
